topics.title: Brief History of Plastics: Advantages and Disadvantages, Thermoplastics and Thermosets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT403 - PLASTICS MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Brief History of Plastics: Advantages and Disadvantages, Thermoplastics and Thermosets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plastics (KTU Definition):** Plastics are a broad class of **synthetic or semi-synthetic organic polymers** of high molecular weight (typically $\geq 10{,}000 \text{ g/mol}$) that are **malleable** (can be molded, extruded, or cast) under specified conditions of heat and pressure and **retain their final shape** upon cooling or curing. The word "plastic" is derived from the Greek word *plastikos*, meaning "capable of being shaped or molded."

A **polymer** is a large molecule (*macromolecule*) composed of many repeating structural subunits called **monomers**, joined together by **covalent bonds** through the process of **polymerization**.

$$\text{Monomer} \xrightarrow{\text{Polymerization}} \text{Polymer}$$

The two main families of plastics are:
1. **Thermoplastics** — Linear/branched chain polymers that soften on heating and harden on cooling (reversible process).
2. **Thermosetting Plastics (Thermosets)** — Cross-linked network polymers that undergo an irreversible chemical change (curing) upon heating, forming a rigid 3D structure.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plastics as a "Modern Clay"

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Smart Clay" Analogy**
> Imagine **kitchen clay** used by a child. When you wet it, it is soft and you can shape it into a toy. When it dries, it hardens and keeps the shape permanently. Now imagine that clay could be **re-melted and reshaped** infinitely many times — that is essentially what a *thermoplastic* does. If, instead, the clay undergoes a chemical reaction while drying, becoming so hard that no amount of reheating can soften it again (it would just burn), that is a *thermoset*. The key difference: **thermoplastics are reversible, thermosets are permanent.**

Plastics revolutionized the 20th century because they offered the **ease of clay** combined with **strength rivaling metals**, at a fraction of the cost.

---

### 1.3 Brief History of Plastics — The Revolutionary Timeline

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Milestones at a Glance**

| Year | Material | Inventor / Company | Significance |
|------|----------|-------------------|--------------|
| **1839** | Polystyrene (discovered) | Eduard Simon | First observation of a polymer from natural source (storax) |
| **1862** | Parkesine (first semi-synthetic plastic) | Alexander Parkes (UK) | First man-made plastic, exhibited at the Great International Exhibition, London |
| **1869** | Celluloid | John Wesley Hyatt (USA) | Replacement for ivory in billiard balls; first thermoplastic |
| **1892** | Viscose Rayon | Charles Frederick Cross | Regenerated cellulose fiber |
| **1907** | **Bakelite** | Leo Hendrik Baekeland | **First fully synthetic, commercially successful thermoset**; the "Material of a Thousand Uses" |
| **1920s** | PVC (Polyvinyl Chloride) | Waldo Semon (B.F. Goodrich) | Commercialized; became second-most used plastic |
| **1933** | Polyethylene (LDPE) | ICI (UK), Eric Fawcett & Reginald Gibson | Discovered accidentally during high-pressure experiments |
| **1935** | Nylon 6,6 | Wallace Carothers (DuPont) | First fully synthetic fiber |
| **1938** | Teflon (PTFE) | Roy Plunkett (DuPont) | Accidental discovery; extreme chemical resistance |
| **1940s** | Silicones, Acrylics, Polyurethanes | Various | Wartime innovation |
| **1953** | Polypropylene | Karl Ziegler & Giulio Natta | Nobel Prize 1963; one of the most versatile polymers |
| **1960s–70s** | Engineering Plastics (PC, PEEK, ABS) | Bayer, GE, etc. | High-performance plastics replacing metals |
| **1990s–2000s** | Bioplastics (PLA, PHB) | Cargill, NatureWorks | Bio-based and biodegradable plastics |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Highlight:** Baekeland's **Bakelite (1907)** is universally considered the *birth of the plastics industry*. Before Bakelite, plastics were merely modified natural materials. After Bakelite, polymers could be designed from scratch at the molecular level.

---

### 1.4 Advantages and Disadvantages of Plastics

#### Advantages (Why Plastics Replaced Traditional Materials)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Advantages of Plastics**

1. **Lightweight** — Density typically **0.85–1.5 g/cm³** (vs. 7.87 g/cm³ for steel); up to 8× lighter than metals, reducing transportation costs.
2. **Corrosion Resistance** — Plastics do not rust or oxidize; suitable for chemical plants, marine applications, and outdoor use.
3. **Design Flexibility** — Can be molded into complex shapes (intricate geometries impossible with metals or ceramics).
4. **Low Cost** — Mass production via injection molding, extrusion, and blow molding is highly economical.
5. **Electrical Insulation** — Most plastics are excellent dielectrics (volume resistivity $10^{12}$–$10^{18} \ \Omega \cdot \text{cm}$); essential for cables, PCBs, and electronics.
6. **Thermal Insulation** — Low thermal conductivity ($\approx 0.1$–$0.3 \ \text{W/m·K}$) makes them good insulators.
7. **Colorability & Aesthetics** — Can be dyed in any color, transparent, translucent, or opaque.
8. **Durability & Toughness** — High impact resistance; some (e.g., UHMWPE) rival steel in impact strength per weight.
9. **Chemical Resistance** — Resistant to acids, alkalis, and solvents (depending on polymer type).
10. **Mass-Producibility** — High production rates (e.g., injection molding cycles of 5–30 seconds).

#### Disadvantages (The Environmental and Engineering Costs)

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Disadvantages — KTU Frequently Asks These**

1. **Non-Biodegradability** — Most commodity plastics persist in the environment for **hundreds of years**, causing severe pollution and microplastic contamination.
2. **Toxic Fumes on Burning** — Burning plastics release **toxic gases** (e.g., HCl from PVC, HCN from polyamides, CO from incomplete combustion).
3. **Low Heat Resistance** — Most thermoplastics deform at relatively low temperatures (e.g., PS at $\sim 100^\circ\text{C}$, PE at $\sim 80$–$120^\circ\text{C}$).
4. **UV Degradation** — Sunlight breaks down polymer chains, causing embrittlement and discoloration.
5. **Recycling Challenges** — Mixed plastics are difficult to recycle; down-cycling reduces quality.
6. **Petroleum-Dependent** — Most plastics are derived from non-renewable fossil fuels (crude oil, natural gas).
7. **Static Charge Accumulation** — Surface charge attracts dust; can cause ESD damage in electronics.
8. **Poor Mechanical Strength (vs. metals)** — Low modulus and strength for commodity plastics; require fillers/reinforcement for structural use.
9. **Flammability** — Most plastics are combustible (LOI typically 17–21%; air is $\approx 21\%$).
10. **Environmental Persistence (Microplastics)** — Break into microplastics, entering the food chain.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Foundations — Why Plastics Behave the Way They Do

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core 'Why' Behind Plastics' Behavior**
> All plastic behavior (thermal, mechanical, chemical) is governed by **three structural factors**:
> 1. **Chain Architecture** (linear, branched, cross-linked, networked)
> 2. **Intermolecular Forces** (van der Waals, dipole-dipole, hydrogen bonding)
> 3. **Degree of Crystallinity** (amorphous vs. semi-crystalline)

### 2.2 Thermoplastics vs. Thermosets — The Master Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> **Definitions**

- **Thermoplastics:** Polymers that consist of **linear or branched chains** held together by weak **secondary forces** (van der Waals, hydrogen bonds, dipole interactions). They have **no chemical cross-links** between chains. On heating, the secondary forces weaken, allowing chains to slide past each other — the polymer flows. On cooling, secondary forces re-establish and the material solidifies. This is a **physical change** and is **fully reversible** (in principle, infinitely).

- **Thermosetting Plastics (Thermosets):** Polymers that undergo an **irreversible chemical reaction** (curing) to form a **3D covalently cross-linked network**. Once cured, the cross-links are permanent covalent bonds that cannot be broken by heating. On reheating, a thermoset does **not melt** — it **decomposes (chars/burns)**.

#### Tabular Comparison (KTU Board-Ready)

| Property | Thermoplastics | Thermosets |
|----------|----------------|------------|
| **Chain structure** | Linear or branched | Cross-linked 3D network |
| **Bonding** | Weak secondary forces | Strong covalent cross-links |
| **On heating** | Softens → melts → flows | Does not melt; degrades |
| **On cooling** | Solidifies (reversible) | Remains hard (irreversible) |
| **Processability** | Remelted & reprocessed | Cannot be remelted/reprocessed |
| **Solubility** | Soluble in suitable solvents | Insoluble (only swells) |
| **Recyclability** | Easily recyclable | Not recyclable by remolding |
| **Mechanical strength** | Moderate | High strength & rigidity |
| **Chemical resistance** | Generally good | Excellent (cross-links block solvents) |
| **Creep resistance** | Lower (chains slide) | Excellent (cross-links lock chains) |
| **Examples** | PE, PP, PVC, PS, PA, PMMA, PET | Bakelite, Epoxy, Phenolic, Urea-formaldehyde, Melamine, Polyurethane (cured), Silicone |
| **Typical applications** | Packaging, bottles, toys, films, pipes | Adhesives, circuit boards, cookware handles, composites, automotive parts |

---

### 2.3 Schematic Representation of the Two Structures

**Thermoplastic (linear chains, no cross-links):**
```
   ── ── ── ── ──    (Chain A)
        ── ── ── ── ──    (Chain B)
   ── ── ── ── ──    (Chain C)
        ── ── ── ──    (Chain D)
```

**Thermoset (cross-linked 3D network):**
```
   ──C───C───C───C───
    │    │    │    │
   ──C───C───C───C───
    │    │    │    │
   ──C───C───C───C───
```
*(Each `C` represents a covalent cross-link node.)*

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Equations and Parameters for Plastics Materials**

| Concept | Equation / Parameter | Description |
|---------|---------------------|-------------|
| **Degree of Polymerization (DP)** | $DP = \dfrac{\overline{M}_n}{M_0}$ | $\overline{M}_n$ = number-average molecular weight; $M_0$ = molar mass of repeat unit |
| **Number-Average Molecular Weight** | $\overline{M}_n = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | $N_i$ = number of molecules of mass $M_i$ |
| **Weight-Average Molecular Weight** | $\overline{M}_w = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | Sensitive to high-MW species |
| **Polydispersity Index (PDI)** | $PDI = \dfrac{\overline{M}_w}{\overline{M}_n}$ | PDI = 1 for perfectly uniform polymer; typical commercial plastics: 2–10 |
| **Glass Transition Temperature** | $T_g$ | Temp. at which amorphous polymer transitions from glassy to rubbery state |
| **Melting Temperature** | $T_m$ | Temp. at which crystalline regions of a semi-crystalline polymer melt |
| **Crystallinity (%)** | $\% \text{Crystallinity} = \dfrac{\rho_c (\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s (\rho_c - \rho_a)} \times 100$ | $\rho_c$, $\rho_a$, $\rho_s$ = densities of fully crystalline, fully amorphous, and sample |
| **Flory-Fox Equation** | $T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \dfrac{K}{\overline{M}_n}$ | $K \approx 2 \times 10^5 \ \text{K·g/mol}$ for many polymers |
| **Mark-Houwink Equation** | $[\eta] = K \cdot M^a$ | Intrinsic viscosity $[\eta]$ related to molecular weight $M$; $K$, $a$ are polymer-solvent constants |
| **Melt Flow Index (MFI)** | $\text{MFI} = \dfrac{\text{mass extruded (g)}}{\text{time (10 min)}}$ at specified T and load | Inverse indicator of molecular weight |
| **Limiting Oxygen Index (LOI)** | $\text{LOI} = \dfrac{[O_2]}{[O_2] + [N_2]} \times 100\%$ | $> 27\%$ = self-extinguishing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Thermal Transitions in One Line:** For amorphous polymers, $T_g$ dominates; for semi-crystalline polymers, both $T_m$ (crystalline melting) and $T_g$ (amorphous phase transition) are observed, with the general rule $T_m \approx (1.5 \text{ to } 2.0) \times T_g$ (in Kelvin).

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The thermoplastic/thermoset distinction drives **every major design decision** in modern engineering:
- **Automotive**: Thermoplastic bumpers and dashboards (recyclable, impact-resistant) vs. thermoset composite body panels (lightweight, stiff, heat-resistant).
- **Electronics**: Thermoplastic PCB substrates (FR-4 is thermoset epoxy + glass reinforcement) vs. thermoplastic enclosures.
- **Aerospace**: Thermoset epoxy/carbon-fiber composites for primary structures (irreversible cure = high dimensional stability at elevated temperatures).
- **Packaging**: Thermoplastics (PE, PP, PET) dominate because they can be melt-extruded into films, bottles, and containers rapidly and at low cost.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Demonstration: Why Thermosets Are Irreversible — A Cross-Linking Reaction

Consider a simple thermoset: **Phenol-Formaldehyde (Bakelite)**.

**Step 1: Hydroxymethylation (formation of methylol groups)**

Phenol reacts with formaldehyde in the presence of an acid or base catalyst to form ortho- and para-hydroxymethyl phenols:

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{HCHO} \xrightarrow{\text{Acid/Base}} \text{HO-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-CH}_2\text{OH}$$

**Step 2: Condensation (formation of methylene bridges)**

Methylol groups on different phenol rings condense, eliminating water and forming **methylene ($\text{-CH}_2\text{-}$) bridges**:

$$\text{Ar-CH}_2\text{OH} + \text{HO-CH}_2\text{-Ar} \longrightarrow \text{Ar-CH}_2\text{-Ar} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 3: Network formation (3D cross-linking)**

When many such bridges form between multifunctional phenol rings (phenol has *three* reactive sites: two ortho, one para), an **infinite 3D covalent network** is created. The final Bakelite structure is essentially one giant molecule — a thermoset.

**Why irreversible?** Each cross-link is a **covalent bond** (bond energy $\approx 350 \ \text{kJ/mol}$). Simply heating the polymer does not break these bonds — the polymer will **decompose** (break C–C and C–H bonds) before the cross-links break. This is why thermosets char rather than melt.

---

### 3.2 Demonstration: Why Thermoplastics Are Reversible — A Linear Polymerization

Consider the **free-radical polymerization of polyethylene (PE)**:

**Step 1: Initiation**

A peroxide initiator (e.g., benzoyl peroxide) decomposes to form a free radical:

$$\text{(C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COO)}_2 \longrightarrow 2 \ \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COO}^{\bullet} \longrightarrow 2 \ \text{C}_6\text{H}_5^{\bullet} + 2 \ \text{CO}_2$$

**Step 2: Propagation**

The radical attacks the ethylene double bond, creating a new radical at the chain end:

$$\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{CH}_2=\text{CH}_2 \longrightarrow \text{R-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2^{\bullet}$$

This step repeats thousands of times:

$$\text{R-(CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{)}_n\text{-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2^{\bullet} + \text{CH}_2=\text{CH}_2 \longrightarrow \text{R-(CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{)}_{n+1}\text{-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2^{\bullet}$$

**Step 3: Termination**

Two growing chains combine (combination) or transfer a hydrogen (disproportionation):

$$\text{R-(CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{)}_m^{\bullet} + \text{R'-(CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{)}_n^{\bullet} \longrightarrow \text{R-(CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{)}_m\text{-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-R'-(CH}_2\text{)}_{n}\text{(dead chain)}$$

**Why reversible?** The final PE chains are held together *only* by weak **van der Waals forces** (London dispersion, $\approx 4 \ \text{kJ/mol}$ — about 100× weaker than covalent bonds). Heating provides enough thermal energy to overcome these forces, allowing chains to slide freely — the polymer melts. On cooling, the secondary forces re-form, and the polymer solidifies. This cycle can be repeated.

---

### 3.3 Quantitative Example: Calculating Degree of Polymerization

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (Typical KTU Calculation Style)**

A commercial polyethylene sample has a number-average molecular weight $\overline{M}_n = 1.4 \times 10^5 \ \text{g/mol}$. The molar mass of the ethylene repeat unit is $M_0 = 28 \ \text{g/mol}$.

**Solution:**

$$DP = \dfrac{\overline{M}_n}{M_0} = \dfrac{1.4 \times 10^5}{28}$$

$$DP = 5000$$

**Interpretation:** Each PE chain contains, on average, **5000 monomer units**. This is typical for high-density polyethylene (HDPE) used in bottles and pipes.

**Polydispersity consideration:** Suppose the same sample has $\overline{M}_w = 4.2 \times 10^5 \ \text{g/mol}$. Then:

$$PDI = \dfrac{\overline{M}_w}{\overline{M}_n} = \dfrac{4.2 \times 10^5}{1.4 \times 10^5} = 3.0$$

This PDI of 3.0 indicates a **moderately broad molecular weight distribution**, typical of free-radical polymerized polymers (anionic "living" polymerizations give PDI $\approx 1.0$).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Classifying Plastics by Behavior

| Behavior on Heating | Class | Examples | Recyclable? |
|---------------------|-------|----------|-------------|
| Softens & melts (linear) | Thermoplastic — semi-crystalline | PE, PP, PA (Nylon), PET, POM | Yes |
| Softens & flows (amorphous) | Thermoplastic — amorphous | PS, PMMA, PVC (plasticized), PC | Yes |
| Hardens irreversibly | Thermoset — phenolic | Bakelite, Novolac, Resole | No |
| Cures at room T | Thermoset — ambient cure | Epoxy + amine, Silicone RTV | No |
| Foams and cross-links | Thermoset — foam | PU foam, Urea-formaldehyde foam | No |
| Elastomeric (rubbery) | Elastomer (cross-linked but lightly) | Natural rubber (vulcanized), SBR, EPDM | Limited (reclaimed rubber) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Elastomers** are sometimes considered a third class: they have *light* cross-linking (a few cross-links per chain) that allows large elastic deformation but prevents flow. Vulcanized rubber is the classic example. KTU exams may ask you to differentiate elastomers from thermosets.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model]** *Define plastics. List any four advantages of plastics over conventional materials.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Plastics are a broad class of **synthetic or semi-synthetic organic polymers** of high molecular weight (typically $\geq 10{,}000 \ \text{g/mol}$) that are **malleable** (can be molded, extruded, or cast) under specified conditions of heat and pressure and **retain their final shape** upon cooling or curing.

**Four Advantages:**
1. **Lightweight** — Low density ($0.85$–$1.5 \ \text{g/cm}^3$) compared to metals ($\approx 7.87 \ \text{g/cm}^3$ for steel), reducing transportation and structural costs.
2. **Corrosion resistance** — Plastics do not rust, oxidize, or corrode in most environments, unlike metals.
3. **Design flexibility** — Can be molded into complex shapes and intricate geometries that are difficult or impossible to achieve with metals or ceramics.
4. **Electrical insulation** — High volume resistivity ($10^{12}$–$10^{18} \ \Omega \cdot \text{cm}$) makes them excellent insulators for cables, switchgear, and PCBs.

*(Any other valid advantage — low cost, chemical resistance, colorability, mass-producibility — also accepted.)*

---

#### **Question A2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]** *Differentiate between thermoplastics and thermosetting plastics with two examples each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Thermoplastics | Thermosetting Plastics |
|----------|----------------|------------------------|
| **Structure** | Linear or branched chains with no chemical cross-links | 3D network with covalent cross-links |
| **On heating** | Soften and melt (reversible) | Do not melt; decompose on excessive heating (irreversible) |
| **Recyclability** | Can be remelted and reprocessed | Cannot be remelted or reprocessed |

**Examples:**
- **Thermoplastics:** Polyethylene (PE), Polypropylene (PP), PVC, Polystyrene, Nylon.
- **Thermosets:** Bakelite (Phenol-formaldehyde), Epoxy resin, Urea-formaldehyde, Melamine.

---

### Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** — *Option 1*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the brief history of plastics highlighting the contributions of Alexander Parkes, Leo Baekeland, and Wallace Carothers.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the structural differences between thermoplastics and thermosets with neat diagrams. Why are thermosets not recyclable by remolding?

---

#### **Model Solution for B-A:**

**(a) Brief History of Plastics — Three Pioneers**

**1. Alexander Parkes (1856–1862) — The Pioneer:**
- In 1862, Alexander Parkes (UK) developed **Parkesine**, the world's first man-made plastic, derived from cellulose (a natural polymer) treated with nitric acid and a solvent.
- It was exhibited at the **Great International Exhibition in London (1862)** and won a bronze medal.
- *Significance:* Parkesine was the first material to be deliberately engineered as a plastic, paving the way for the entire plastics industry. However, it was flammable and expensive, limiting commercial success.

**2. Leo Hendrik Baekeland (1907) — The Father of the Plastics Industry:**
- In 1907, Baekeland (a Belgian-American chemist) invented **Bakelite** — the world's **first fully synthetic plastic** (made entirely from non-natural chemicals: phenol and formaldehyde).
- Bakelite was a **thermosetting phenol-formaldehyde resin** that hardened irreversibly upon heating and could not be softened again.
- It was an excellent electrical insulator, heat-resistant, and could be mass-produced.
- *Significance:* Bakelite launched the **modern plastics industry** and was used in radios, telephones, electrical insulators, jewelry, and cookware. Baekeland's 1907 patent (US Patent 942,699) is often called the "birth certificate" of the plastics industry.

**3. Wallace Carothers (1934–1935) — The Polymer Chemist:**
- Carothers, working at **DuPont (USA)**, led the fundamental research that established the science of **polycondensation** and **polyaddition** polymerizations.
- In 1935, his team synthesized **Nylon 6,6** — the first fully synthetic fiber — by polycondensation of hexamethylenediamine and adipic acid.
- *Significance:* Carothers' work proved that polymers were genuine macromolecules with predictable structures (defeating the "colloidal aggregate" theory of his day). Nylon revolutionized the textile industry, replacing silk in stockings, parachutes, and tires.

---

**(b) Structural Differences and Non-Recyclability of Thermosets**

**Thermoplastics — Linear/Branched Structure:**

- Polymer chains are arranged linearly or with branches, **without** chemical cross-links.
- Chains are held together by **weak intermolecular forces** (van der Waals, dipole-dipole, hydrogen bonding).
- *Schematic:*
  ```
  [Monomer-Monomer-Monomer-...] (Chain 1)
        [Monomer-Monomer-...]    (Chain 2)
  [Monomer-Monomer-Monomer-...] (Chain 3)
  ```
- **On heating**, the weak secondary forces are overcome, allowing chains to slide — the polymer flows (melts).
- **On cooling**, secondary forces re-form, the polymer solidifies. The process is **reversible** and the polymer can be remelted many times.

**Thermosets — Cross-Linked 3D Network:**

- During curing, covalent bonds form between chains, creating a **three-dimensional network** that is essentially one giant molecule.
- *Schematic:*
  ```
       C---C---C
       |   |   |
       C---C---C
       |   |   |
       C---C---C
  ```
  *(Each `C` is a cross-link node; horizontal/vertical lines are covalent bonds.)*
- **On heating**, the covalent cross-links (bond energy $\approx 350 \ \text{kJ/mol}$) do **not** break at typical processing temperatures. The polymer does **not melt or flow**. At very high temperatures, the entire network **decomposes** (chars or burns).

**Why thermosets are not recyclable by remolding:**

A recyclable material must be able to flow (melt) and re-solidify without chemical change. Thermosets, due to their covalent cross-linked network:
1. Do not melt on heating — the network is held by covalent bonds, not weak secondary forces.
2. Begin to **degrade (oxidize, char, release volatiles)** before reaching a state where flow could occur.
3. Even if heated near degradation, the chemical structure is permanently altered — the cross-links are irreversible.
4. They are also **insoluble** in most solvents (only swell slightly), so solvent-based recycling is impractical.

Therefore, thermosets can only be **recycled mechanically** (grinding into filler for composite applications) or **chemically** (depolymerization to monomers, if at all feasible), but not by simple remolding like thermoplastics.

---

#### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** — *Option 2*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** List and briefly explain **any six advantages** and **any five disadvantages** of plastics.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a labeled flow diagram, classify plastics into thermoplastics, thermosets, and elastomers, giving two examples of each.

---

#### **Model Solution for B-B:**

**(a) Six Advantages and Five Disadvantages of Plastics**

**Six Advantages:**

1. **Lightweight:** Plastics have densities of $0.85$–$1.5 \ \text{g/cm}^3$ compared to steel ($\approx 7.87 \ \text{g/cm}^3$) and aluminum ($\approx 2.7 \ \text{g/cm}^3$). This makes them ideal for transportation (automotive, aerospace) where weight reduction improves fuel efficiency.

2. **Corrosion Resistance:** Plastics resist corrosion from water, acids, alkalis, and atmospheric gases, unlike metals which rust. They are widely used in chemical plants, water pipes (PVC, HDPE), and marine applications.

3. **Design Flexibility & Complex Geometry:** Plastics can be molded into intricate, complex shapes (e.g., integral hinges, hollow parts, thin walls) that would be expensive or impossible to achieve with metals or ceramics.

4. **Low Cost & Mass-Producibility:** Injection molding, extrusion, and blow molding allow millions of parts to be produced per day at low per-unit cost. Commodity plastics (PE, PP) cost as little as $1$–$2 \ \text{USD/kg}$.

5. **Electrical & Thermal Insulation:** Plastics are excellent electrical insulators (volume resistivity $10^{12}$–$10^{18} \ \Omega \cdot \text{cm}$) and thermal insulators (thermal conductivity $0.1$–$0.3 \ \text{W/m·K}$), making them essential for cable sheathing, PCBs, appliance housings, and thermal insulation panels.

6. **Colorability & Aesthetics:** Plastics can be manufactured in any color, transparency, opacity, or finish (glossy, matte, textured) by adding pigments and dyes directly to the melt. This eliminates the need for post-painting.

**Five Disadvantages:**

1. **Non-Biodegradability / Environmental Pollution:** Most commodity plastics persist in the environment for hundreds of years, causing severe plastic pollution in oceans and landfills. They fragment into **microplastics** ($\leq 5 \ \text{mm}$) that enter the food chain.

2. **Toxic Emissions on Burning:** Burning plastics releases toxic gases — e.g., **HCl** from PVC, **HCN** from nitrogen-containing polymers (Nylon, PU), **CO** from incomplete combustion. Dioxins and furans can be released from PVC combustion.

3. **Low Heat Resistance:** Many thermoplastics deform at relatively low temperatures — e.g., PE softens at $\approx 80$–$120^\circ\text{C}$, PS at $\approx 100^\circ\text{C}$. This limits their use in high-temperature applications (e.g., engine components, boiling water).

4. **UV Degradation:** Prolonged exposure to sunlight (UV radiation) breaks polymer chains, causing embrittlement, loss of mechanical strength, and yellowing/discoloration. UV stabilizers are required for outdoor use.

5. **Petroleum-Dependence & Non-Renewability:** About 90% of plastics are derived from fossil fuels (crude oil, natural gas), making them unsustainable in the long term and subject to oil price volatility.

---

**(b) Classification Flow Diagram with Examples**

```
                         [PLASTICS]
                             |
        ┌────────────────────┼────────────────────┐
        |                    |                    |
   [Thermoplastics]    [Thermosets]         [Elastomers]
        |                    |                    |
   Linear/Branched     3D Cross-linked     Lightly Cross-linked
   Weak forces         Covalent bonds      Elastic network
        |                    |                    |
   ┌────┴────┐         ┌─────┴─────┐        ┌────┴────┐
   |         |         |           |        |         |
[Amorphous] [Semi-Cryst] [Phenolic] [Epoxy] [Natural] [Synthetic]
   |         |         |           |        |         |
  PS,PMMA   PE,PP,Nylon Bakelite   Bis-A   Rubber    SBR,EPDM
  PVC,PC    PET,POM    Urea-Form.  cured            Neoprene
            PA         Melamine,PU
```

**Examples:**
- **Thermoplastics:** Polyethylene (PE), Polypropylene (PP), Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC), Polystyrene (PS), Nylon (PA), Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET), Polycarbonate (PC).
- **Thermosets:** Bakelite (Phenol-Formaldehyde), Epoxy resin, Urea-Formaldehyde, Melamine-Formaldehyde, Cured Polyurethane, Cured Silicone.
- **Elastomers:** Vulcanized Natural Rubber, Styrene-Butadiene Rubber (SBR), Ethylene-Propylene-Diene Monomer (EPDM), Neoprene (Polychloroprene), Silicone Rubber.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (from KTU Board Examiner Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Confusing thermosets and elastomers.** Elastomers have *sparse* cross-linking (just enough to prevent flow but allow elastic stretch). Thermosets have *dense* cross-linking (rigid, brittle, no elasticity). Writing "all cross-linked polymers are thermosets" loses marks.
>
> 2. **Writing "Bakelite is a thermoplastic" or "Polyethylene is a thermoset."** This is the most common factual error in KTU scripts. **Memorize** the categorization: PE, PP, PVC, PS, Nylon, PET, PMMA, PC = thermoplastic. Bakelite, Epoxy, Urea-Formaldehyde, Melamine = thermoset.
>
> 3. **Skipping the "Why" in the thermoplastic/thermoset question.** Merely stating "thermoplastics melt on heating" without explaining that the chains are held by *weak secondary forces* (not covalent bonds) loses 2–3 marks. Always explain the **structural reason** behind the macroscopic behavior.
>
> 4. **Forgetting units and year-tagging in history questions.** When mentioning Baekeland, always cite **1907** and the term **Phenol-Formaldehyde**. When mentioning Carothers, cite **1935** and **Nylon 6,6**. Examiners reward precision.
>
> 5. **Listing advantages/disadvantages without relating to the polymer structure.** The best answers link property → structure. E.g., "Plastics have low heat resistance **because** the secondary forces between chains are weak and break at low temperatures." This shows deep understanding and scores full marks.
>
> 6. **Not drawing a diagram for the classification question (Part B).** A neat, labeled flow chart of plastics classification is worth 2–3 marks by itself. Always include a diagram.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Quick Recap — Module 1, Topic 1**

- **Plastics** = high-MW organic polymers that can be molded/cast/extruded into shape.
- **Greek origin:** *plastikos* = "capable of being shaped."
- **Three pioneers of plastics history:**
  - **Alexander Parkes (1862)** → Parkesine, first semi-synthetic plastic (celluloid-based).
  - **Leo Baekeland (1907)** → Bakelite, first *fully synthetic* plastic (Phenol-Formaldehyde); birth of the modern plastics industry.
  - **Wallace Carothers (1935, DuPont)** → Nylon 6,6; established the science of polymer chemistry.
- **Other landmark dates:** PE (1933), PVC (1926), PTFE (1938), PP (1953), PC (1958).
- **Key advantages (memory hook: "LCCDEC"):** Lightweight, Corrosion-resistant, Cheap, Design-flexible, Electrical insulator, Colorable.
- **Key disadvantages (memory hook: "NTLURP"):** Non-biodegradable, Toxic on burning, Low heat resistance, UV degradation, Recyclable-but-difficult, Petroleum-dependent.
- **Thermoplastics** = linear/branched chains, **weak secondary forces**, **reversible** (soften on heating, harden on cooling), **recyclable** by remolding. Examples: **PE, PP, PVC, PS, PA (Nylon), PET, PMMA, PC**.
- **Thermosets** = 3D covalent cross-linked network, **irreversible**, **do not melt**, **not recyclable by remolding**. Examples: **Bakelite, Epoxy, Urea-Formaldehyde, Melamine, cured PU, cured Silicone**.
- **Elastomers** = lightly cross-linked, **elastic** (recover large deformations), not a true thermoset. Examples: **Vulcanized Natural Rubber, SBR, EPDM, Neoprene**.
- **Key formulas to memorize:** $DP = \overline{M}_n / M_0$, $PDI = \overline{M}_w / \overline{M}_n$, Flory-Fox, Mark-Houwink, LOI definition.
- **Crystallinity rule:** Thermoplastics can be amorphous (PS, PMMA) or semi-crystalline (PE, PP, Nylon). Thermosets are always amorphous (cross-linking prevents crystallization).
- **Environmental angle:** Modern trend is toward **bioplastics** (PLA, PHA), **recycling**, and **circular economy** — KTU may ask this as an application-level question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT403 - PLASTICS MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Monomer Preparation, Polymerisation, Properties and Application of LDPE, HDPE, Cross Linked and Chlorinated PE and PP, Polyvinylidene Chloride

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Polyolefin Family: Introduction

The polyolefins are the largest volume family of thermoplastics in the world, accounting for nearly **55%** of global polymer consumption. They are produced by the polymerization of simple olefin (alkene) monomers whose general formula is **C_nH_{2n}**. The four most industrially significant members — **LDPE, HDPE, PP, and PVDC** — along with the specialty variants **XLPE (Cross-linked PE) and CPE (Chlorinated PE)** — are derived from three core monomers: **ethylene (ethene, CH₂=CH₂), propylene (propene, CH₂=CHCH₃), and vinylidene chloride (1,1-dichloroethylene, CH₂=CCl₂)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 scheme requires students to master **monomer synthesis**, **polymerization mechanism**, **structure–property relationship**, and **end-use applications** for each polymer. Marks are heavily skewed toward mechanism-based questions and application mapping.

## 1.2 Formal KTU Definitions

| Polymer | Abbreviation | Repeating Unit | Polymerization Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| Low Density Polyethylene | LDPE | –(CH₂–CH₂)_n– | Free-radical (high pressure) |
| High Density Polyethylene | HDPE | –(CH₂–CH₂)_n– | Coordination (Ziegler–Natta / Metallocene) |
| Cross-linked Polyethylene | XLPE / PEX | –(CH₂–CH₂)_n– + crosslinks | Free-radical + peroxide/silane |
| Chlorinated Polyethylene | CPE | –(CH₂–CH₂)_n(CCl₂–CH₂)_m– | Post-polymerization chlorination |
| Polypropylene | PP | –(CH₂–CH(CH₃))_n– | Coordination (Ziegler–Natta / Metallocene) |
| Polyvinylidene Chloride | PVDC | –(CH₂–CCl₂)_n– | Free-radical (emulsion/suspension) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Beaded Necklace"

Imagine each polymer chain as a **string of beads** where:
- Each **bead = one monomer unit** (ethylene, propylene, or vinylidene chloride)
- The **string = the covalent C–C bond** that holds them together
- The **way the beads are arranged** = tacticity (isotactic, syndiotactic, atactic)
- The **presence of kinks/joints in the string** = chain branching (long branches = LDPE; no branches = HDPE)
- The **glue spot connecting two strings** = crosslink (XLPE)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The number of branches, the stereochemistry, and the degree of crosslinking determine whether a polymer will be soft and flexible (LDPE) or hard and rigid (HDPE). A single chemical modification can completely change a polymer's properties — this is the central theme of Module 1.

## 1.4 Monomer Background — Ethylene, Propylene, and Vinylidene Chloride

### 1.4.1 Ethylene (CH₂=CH₂)
The simplest alkene, a colourless, flammable gas with a sweet odour. It is the **C2 petrochemical building block**, produced primarily by **steam cracking** of naphtha, ethane, or natural gas liquids at temperatures of **750–900 °C** followed by rapid quenching.

$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_6 \xrightarrow{750-900°C, \text{steam}} \text{CH}_2=\text{CH}_2 + \text{H}_2$$

### 1.4.2 Propylene (CH₂=CHCH₃)
A colourless gas produced mainly as a **by-product of ethylene steam cracking** and **fluid catalytic cracking (FCC)** of vacuum gas oil. Modern on-purpose technologies include **propane dehydrogenation (PDH)** and **methanol-to-olefins (MTO)**.

$$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3 \xrightarrow{\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3/\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3, 600°C} \text{CH}_2=\text{CHCH}_3 + \text{H}_2$$

### 1.4.3 Vinylidene Chloride (CH₂=CCl₂)
A colourless, volatile liquid with a chloroform-like odour. It is produced by the **dehydrochlorination of 1,1,2-trichloroethane** using a caustic alkali or via direct chlorination of ethylene followed by dehydrochlorination.

$$\text{CH}_2=\text{CHCl} + \text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_2\text{Cl–CHCl}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{NaOH}} \text{CH}_2=\text{CCl}_2 + \text{NaCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Polymerization Mechanisms — Operational Logic

### 2.1.1 Free-Radical Polymerization (LDPE, PVDC)

The free-radical mechanism proceeds in **three distinct stages**:

**Step 1 — Initiation:**
A thermal initiator (e.g., **di-tert-butyl peroxide**, O₂) decomposes to form a free radical (R•), which attacks the C=C double bond of the monomer.

$$\text{I} \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2\text{R}^{\bullet}$$
$$\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{CH}_2=\text{CH}_2 \rightarrow \text{R–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2^{\bullet}$$

**Step 2 — Propagation:**
The newly formed radical adds successive monomer units, with the active site migrating to the chain end. The high pressure (1000–3000 atm) and temperature (200–300 °C) cause **chain transfer to polymer**, generating the long-chain branches that distinguish LDPE from HDPE.

$$\text{R–(CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{)}_n^{\bullet} + \text{CH}_2=\text{CH}_2 \rightarrow \text{R–(CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{)}_{n+1}^{\bullet}$$

**Step 3 — Termination:**
Two growing chains combine (**combination**) or transfer a hydrogen (**disproportionation**), ending chain growth.

$$\text{R–(CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{)}_n^{\bullet} + \text{R'–(CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{)}_m^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{R–(CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{)}_{n+m}\text{–R'}$$

### 2.1.2 Coordination Polymerization (HDPE, PP)

Developed by **Karl Ziegler (1953)** and **Giulio Natta (1954)** — awarded the Nobel Prize in 1963. The catalyst is a transition-metal halide (TiCl₄ or TiCl₃) co-activated with an **organoaluminium cocatalyst** (Al(C₂H₅)₃, triethylaluminium). The monomer coordinates to an empty site on the metal centre in a **Cossee–Arlman mechanism**, then inserts into the metal–carbon bond with defined stereochemistry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HDPE vs LDPE — Why the Difference?**
> - In **LDPE** (free radical), the high-energy radical intermediate abstracts a hydrogen from an existing chain, creating a **long-chain branch (LCB)** every 100–200 main-chain carbons. These branches prevent efficient chain packing, lowering crystallinity (40–50%) and density (0.910–0.925 g/cm³).
> - In **HDPE** (Ziegler–Natta), the catalyst site is too sterically crowded to permit branch formation. The result is a **linear chain** that packs tightly, giving high crystallinity (70–80%) and density (0.941–0.965 g/cm³).

### 2.1.3 Cross-linking of Polyethylene (XLPE / PEX)

XLPE is produced by introducing chemical bonds between adjacent PE chains. Three industrial methods are used:

| Method | Reagent | Trigger | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Peroxide (PEX-a)** | Dicumyl peroxide | Heat + pressure | Hot water pipes, underfloor heating |
| **Silane (PEX-b)** | Vinylsilane + moisture | Water + catalyst | Domestic plumbing |
| **Electron beam (PEX-c)** | None | e-beam irradiation | Wire & cable insulation |

$$\text{–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–} + \text{ROOR} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{–CH}_2\text{–}\overset{\bullet}{\text{CH}}\text{–CH}_2\text{–} \rightarrow \text{Crosslink}$$

### 2.1.4 Chlorination of Polyethylene (CPE)

CPE is manufactured by **post-polymerization chlorination** of HDPE in an inert solvent (CCl₄) or in aqueous suspension. Chlorine radicals (Cl•) substitute hydrogen atoms along the chain.

$$\text{–(CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{)}_n\text{–} + \text{Cl}_2 \xrightarrow{h\nu \text{ or } \Delta, \text{ peroxide}} \text{–(CH}_2\text{–CHCl)}_n\text{–} + \text{HCl}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why chlorinate PE?** CPE has improved **oil resistance, flame retardancy, weatherability, and impact flexibility**. It is widely used as an impact modifier in PVC and as a rubber component.

### 2.1.5 Polymerization of Vinylidene Chloride (PVDC)

PVDC is produced by **free-radical polymerization** in **emulsion or suspension**. The two symmetrical chlorine atoms on the same carbon create a highly polar, dense polymer with extremely low gas permeability. Homopolymer PVDC is difficult to process (degrades near melting point), so most commercial grades are **copolymers of VDC with 10–20% vinyl chloride (VC) or methyl acrylate (MA)** — sold as **Saran™**.

$$\text{n CH}_2=\text{CCl}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{AIBN/K}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_8, 30-60°C} \text{–(CH}_2\text{–CCl}_2\text{)}_n\text{–}$$

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Units / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Degree of Polymerization (DP)** | $DP_n = \dfrac{M_n}{M_0}$ | Dimensionless | Average number of monomer units per chain |
| **Number-average Mol. Wt.** | $M_n = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | g/mol | End-group analysis |
| **Weight-average Mol. Wt.** | $M_w = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | g/mol | Light scattering / sedimentation |
| **Polydispersity Index (PDI)** | $PDI = \dfrac{M_w}{M_n}$ | $\geq 1$ (always) | 1.0 = monodisperse, 3–20 = free-radical, ~2 = Ziegler |
| **Percent Crystallinity** | $\%X_c = \dfrac{\rho_c(\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s(\rho_c - \rho_a)} \times 100$ | % | From density $\rho_s$ |
| **Tacticity (% isotactic)** | $m = \dfrac{\text{isotactic dyads}}{\text{total dyads}} \times 100$ | % | Measured by ¹³C NMR |
| **Monomer reactivity ratio** | $r_1 = \dfrac{k_{11}}{k_{12}}$ | 0–∞ | Predicts copolymer composition |
| **Copolymer equation (Mayo-Lewis)** | $\dfrac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \dfrac{[M_1](r_1[M_1] + [M_2])}{[M_2]([M_1] + r_2[M_2])}$ | – | Instantaneous copolymer composition |
| **Glass Transition (Fox)** | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g2}}$ | K | For copolymers/blends |
| **Mark-Houwink** | $[\eta] = K M^a$ | dL/g | Intrinsic viscosity → $M_w$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crystallinity vs. Density Master Rule:** A higher crystallinity gives higher density, higher tensile strength, higher stiffness, lower transparency, and lower permeability. Memorise the crystallinity order:
> **HDPE (70–80%) > LDPE (40–50%) > PP (40–70%, depends on tacticity) > PVDC (high, but slow to crystallize) > CPE (amorphous)**

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

- **LDPE film** dominates food packaging (bread bags, cling wrap) due to transparency and flexibility.
- **HDPE pipes** carry natural gas, water, and chemicals due to chemical resistance and strength.
- **XLPE insulation** is the standard for medium- and high-voltage cables due to thermal endurance up to **90–110 °C continuous** and excellent dielectric strength.
- **CPE** is a critical impact modifier for **rigid PVC window profiles and pipes**, providing low-temperature toughness.
- **PP** is the material of choice for **living hinges, hot-fill containers, and non-woven fibres** due to a melting point of **165–170 °C** (highest among commodity polyolefins).
- **PVDC** (Saran) provides the **oxygen barrier in multilayer food packaging** — its oxygen transmission rate is **10–100× lower** than LDPE.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Degree of Polymerization (DP_n) from M_n

The **number-average degree of polymerization** relates the molecular weight of a polymer to the molecular weight of a single repeat unit. The standard textbook derivation proceeds as follows.

By definition, the number-average molecular weight of a polymer sample is the **sum of the molecular weights of all chains divided by the total number of chains**. For a polymer containing chains of lengths $i = 1, 2, 3, \ldots, n$, with $N_i$ chains of length $i$:

$$M_n = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i \cdot M_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i}$$

The molecular weight of a single chain of length $i$ is equal to $i$ times the molecular weight of the repeat unit $M_0$, plus a negligible contribution from the end-groups (which is dropped for high polymers where $i \gg 1$):

$$M_i = i \cdot M_0$$

Substituting this relationship into the $M_n$ equation:

$$M_n = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i \cdot (i \cdot M_0)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i}$$

Factor out the constant $M_0$ from the numerator:

$$M_n = M_0 \cdot \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i \cdot i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i}$$

The bracketed term, by definition, is the **number-average degree of polymerization** $DP_n$:

$$DP_n \equiv \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i \cdot i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i}$$

Therefore, the governing equation is:

$$\boxed{DP_n = \frac{M_n}{M_0}}$$

**Interpretation:**
- $M_0$ for polyethylene (C₂H₄) = **28.05 g/mol**
- $M_0$ for polypropylene (C₃H₆) = **42.08 g/mol**
- $M_0$ for polyvinylidene chloride (C₂H₂Cl₂) = **96.94 g/mol**

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — DP of Commercial LDPE

A commercial LDPE grade has $M_n = 1.4 \times 10^5$ g/mol. The repeat unit is **–CH₂–CH₂–** with $M_0 = 28.05$ g/mol.

**Step 1:** Apply the DP formula:

$$DP_n = \frac{M_n}{M_0}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values:

$$DP_n = \frac{1.4 \times 10^5 \text{ g/mol}}{28.05 \text{ g/mol}}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the quotient:

$$\boxed{DP_n = 4{,}991 \approx 5000}$$

This means the average LDPE chain contains approximately **5000 ethylene units** and is roughly **1.25 μm long** (assuming a C–C bond length of 1.54 Å and tetrahedral bond angle of 109.5° giving an effective advance of 1.25 Å per CH₂ group).

## 3.3 Derivation — Percent Crystallinity from Density

The percent crystallinity of a semi-crystalline polymer is calculated using a **two-phase model** in which the polymer is treated as a mixture of a fully crystalline phase (density $\rho_c$) and a fully amorphous phase (density $\rho_a$). The sample density is $\rho_s$, and the volume fraction of the crystalline phase is $v_c$.

**Mass conservation:** the total mass equals the sum of the masses of the two phases:

$$m_{\text{total}} = m_c + m_a$$

Expressing in terms of densities and volumes:

$$\rho_s V_s = \rho_c V_c + \rho_a V_a$$

**Volume fraction relationship:** $V_c = v_c V_s$ and $V_a = (1 - v_c) V_s$. Substituting:

$$\rho_s V_s = \rho_c \cdot v_c V_s + \rho_a \cdot (1 - v_c) V_s$$

Cancel $V_s$ from both sides:

$$\rho_s = \rho_c v_c + \rho_a (1 - v_c)$$

**Solve for the crystalline volume fraction $v_c$:**

$$\rho_s - \rho_a = v_c (\rho_c - \rho_a)$$

$$v_c = \frac{\rho_s - \rho_a}{\rho_c - \rho_a}$$

**Convert volume fraction to mass fraction** (which is the percent crystallinity). Since $m_c = \rho_c v_c V_s$ and $m_s = \rho_s V_s$:

$$\frac{m_c}{m_s} = \frac{\rho_c v_c}{\rho_s}$$

Substitute $v_c$:

$$\frac{m_c}{m_s} = \frac{\rho_c}{\rho_s} \cdot \frac{\rho_s - \rho_a}{\rho_c - \rho_a}$$

**Percent crystallinity** in percent:

$$\boxed{\% X_c = \frac{\rho_c(\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s(\rho_c - \rho_a)} \times 100}$$

**Standard reference densities:**

| Polymer | $\rho_c$ (g/cm³) | $\rho_a$ (g/cm³) |
|---|---|---|
| Polyethylene | 1.004 | 0.853 |
| Polypropylene (isotactic) | 0.946 | 0.854 |
| Polyvinylidene chloride | 1.957 | 1.660 |

## 3.4 Worked Example — Crystallinity of HDPE

An HDPE sample has measured density $\rho_s = 0.952$ g/cm³. Compute $\%X_c$.

**Step 1:** Identify $\rho_c$ and $\rho_a$ for PE:

$$\rho_c = 1.004 \text{ g/cm}^3, \quad \rho_a = 0.853 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the crystallinity equation:

$$\%X_c = \frac{1.004 \times (0.952 - 0.853)}{0.952 \times (1.004 - 0.853)} \times 100$$

**Step 3:** Compute the numerator:

$$1.004 \times 0.099 = 0.0994$$

**Step 4:** Compute the denominator:

$$0.952 \times 0.151 = 0.1438$$

**Step 5:** Divide and convert to percent:

$$\%X_c = \frac{0.0994}{0.1438} \times 100 = 69.1\%$$

$$\boxed{\%X_c \approx 69\%}$$

This value is consistent with HDPE's typical crystallinity range of **65–80%**.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — LDPE vs HDPE vs PP vs PVDC

| Property | LDPE | HDPE | PP (isotactic) | PVDC (Saran) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Density (g/cm³) | 0.910–0.925 | 0.941–0.965 | 0.895–0.920 | 1.60–1.75 |
| Crystallinity (%) | 40–55 | 65–80 | 50–70 | High (slow) |
| $T_m$ (°C) | 105–115 | 125–135 | 160–170 | 160–170 |
| $T_g$ (°C) | –110 | –110 | –10 to –20 | –19 |
| Tensile strength (MPa) | 9–17 | 25–40 | 30–40 | 35–55 |
| Elongation at break (%) | 100–700 | 10–700 | 100–600 | 10–40 |
| OTR (cm³·mm/m²·day·atm) | 1500–3000 | 500–1000 | 600–1500 | **1–10** |
| Transparency | Translucent | Opaque | Translucent | Clear-yellow |
| Polymerization process | High-pressure radical | Ziegler–Natta / metallocene | Ziegler–Natta / metallocene | Free-radical emulsion |
| $M_w / M_n$ (PDI) | 3–20 | 2–5 (ZN), 2 (metallocene) | 3–10 | 2–4 |
| Key applications | Film, squeeze bottles | Bottles, pipes, drums | Hinges, fibres, packaging | Food wrap, barrier layers |

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Polyolefin Manufacturing Routes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawmat[Crude Oil / Naphtha / Natural Gas] --> crack[Steam Cracking Unit 750-900 C]
    crack --> ethy[Ethylene CH2=CH2]
    crack --> prop[Propylene CH2=CHCH3 byproduct]
    prop --> pdh[Propane Dehydrogenation on purpose]

    hdpe_reactor --> cpe_line[CPE Chlorination reactor Cl2 in CCl4 or aqueous]

    prop --> pp_reactor[PP Reactor Ziegler Natta isotactic atactic syndiotactic]

    vdc_raw[1,1,2 Trichloroethane] --> dehydro[Dehydrochlorination NaOH]
    dehydro --> vdc_mono[Vinylidene Chloride CH2=CCl2]

```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Free-Radical vs Coordination Polymerization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FRP[Free Radical Polymerization LDPE PVDC]
        A1[Initiator I] --> A2[2R radicals]
        A2 --> A3[R + Monomer to RM1 radical]
        A3 --> A4[Propagation chain growth with branching]
        A4 --> A5[Termination combination or disproportionation]
    end

    subgraph COP[Coordination Polymerization HDPE PP]
        B1[TiCl4 or TiCl3 Catalyst] --> B2[AlR3 Cocatalyst]
        B2 --> B3[Active Site Ti C Bond]
        B3 --> B4[Monomer Coordination Cossee Arlman]
        B4 --> B5[Cis insertion into Ti C Bond]
        B5 --> B6[Linear Stereoregular Chain]
    end

    FRP -- "high T, high P" --> outP1[Branched amorphous polymer]
    COP -- "low T, low P" --> outP2[Linear highly crystalline polymer]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Cross-Linking of Polyethylene

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Linear HDPE chain] --> perc[Add Dicumyl Peroxide for PEX a]
    start --> sil[Add Vinylsilane for PEX b]
    start --> eb[Expose to Electron Beam for PEX c]

    perc --> heat[Heat 150-200 C in mold]
    sil --> wat[Expose to hot water + Sn catalyst]
    eb --> xlink

    heat --> xlink[Free radicals abstract H from chain]
    wat --> xlink
    eb --> xlink

    xlink --> cross[Two radical sites on adjacent chains combine]
    cross --> prod[XLPE 3D network insoluble infusible thermoset like]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
**Differentiate between LDPE and HDPE in terms of (a) polymerization process, (b) chain structure, and (c) density and crystallinity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

(a) **Polymerization Process:**
- **LDPE** is produced by **free-radical polymerization** at very high pressures of **1000–3000 atm** and temperatures of **200–300 °C**, using a peroxide initiator.
- **HDPE** is produced by **coordination (Ziegler–Natta) polymerization** at low pressures (1–10 atm) and lower temperatures (50–100 °C), using a TiCl₄/Al(C₂H₅)₃ catalyst.

(b) **Chain Structure:**
- **LDPE** has a **highly branched** structure (long-chain branches every 100–200 C atoms) and contains some short branches.
- **HDPE** has an essentially **linear** chain with very few short branches.

(c) **Density and Crystallinity:**
- **LDPE** density = **0.910–0.925 g/cm³**, crystallinity = **40–55%**.
- **HDPE** density = **0.941–0.965 g/cm³**, crystallinity = **65–80%**.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**Write a short note on the preparation and applications of PVDC (Polyvinylidene Chloride).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Preparation:** PVDC is prepared by **free-radical polymerization** of vinylidene chloride monomer (CH₂=CCl₂), which is itself obtained by the **dehydrochlorination of 1,1,2-trichloroethane** with aqueous NaOH. The polymerization is carried out in **emulsion or suspension** using persulfate or AIBN initiators at temperatures of **30–60 °C**.

**Applications:**
- Multilayer **food packaging films** (Saran wrap) — the dominant use due to exceptional oxygen and moisture barrier.
- **Coatings** for paper, board, and other plastics to provide barrier properties.
- **Monofilaments** for insect screens and filter fabrics.
- **Lacquer coatings** for pharmaceutical blister packs.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students often confuse **vinyl chloride (CH₂=CHCl)** with **vinylidene chloride (CH₂=CCl₂)**. Vinyl chloride has **one** Cl on a carbon, vinylidene chloride has **two** Cl atoms on the **same** carbon. The symmetric 1,1-dichloro substitution is what gives PVDC its barrier properties.

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU ESE Pattern Notice]** — As per the 2024 scheme, students answer **one full question of 14 marks** in the module section, with **internal choice** between two alternatives. Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern]**

**(a) Describe the high-pressure free-radical process for the manufacture of LDPE with a neat flow diagram. Explain how the process conditions affect the polymer properties. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Monomer Preparation (1 mark):**
Ethylene monomer of **>99.9% purity** is obtained from the steam cracking of naphtha/ethane. Traces of oxygen, CO, and water are removed by passing through **molecular sieves and activated alumina** to prevent catalyst poisoning and uncontrolled reactions.

**Step 2 — Compression (1 mark):**
The purified ethylene is compressed in a **multi-stage diaphragm or reciprocating compressor** to a pressure of **1000–3000 atm** (modern autoclave and tubular reactors operate at the lower end, ~1000–2000 atm).

**Step 3 — Polymerization Reactor (2 marks):**
- **Autoclave reactor:** Stirred, large-volume vessel with internal cooling coils.
- **Tubular reactor:** Long jacketed tube (length up to 1 km, inner diameter 25–75 mm) with multiple initiator injection points.
- Initiator: **Oxygen (10–300 ppm) or di-tert-butyl peroxide** is injected at multiple points.
- Temperature: **200–300 °C**.
- Pressure: **1000–2000 atm**.
- Conversion per pass: **15–30%** (single-pass); unreacted ethylene is recycled.

**Step 4 — Separation and Pelletization (1 mark):**
The reactor effluent is depressurized; unreacted ethylene is recycled. The molten polymer is extruded, cooled, and pelletized.

**Step 5 — Effect of Process Conditions (2 marks):**
- **Higher temperature** → lower molecular weight, more branching, lower density, lower crystallinity.
- **Higher pressure** → higher molecular weight, fewer long-chain branches.
- **More initiator** → lower molecular weight.
- **Modifier addition (e.g., propylene, butene)** → introduces short-chain branches for **LLDPE** properties.

**(b) Discuss the manufacture, properties, and applications of HDPE. Compare its properties with LDPE. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Manufacture (3 marks):**
HDPE is produced by **coordination polymerization** using **Ziegler–Natta catalysts** (TiCl₄ + Al(C₂H₅)₃) or **metallocene catalysts** (e.g., Cp₂ZrCl₂ + MAO). The process can be carried out by three technologies:
- **Slurry process:** Catalyst and polymer particles suspended in an inert hydrocarbon (hexane). Temperature 50–100 °C, pressure 1–10 atm.
- **Gas-phase process (UNIPOL):** Fluidized bed of polymer particles; gaseous ethylene feeds the reactor. Used by Union Carbide/ExxonMobil.
- **Solution process:** Polymer dissolves in the solvent; high temperature (~150 °C).

**Step 2 — Properties (2 marks):**
- Density: **0.941–0.965 g/cm³**
- Crystallinity: **65–80%**
- $T_m$: **125–135 °C**; $T_g$: **–110 °C**
- High tensile strength (**25–40 MPa**), high stiffness, opaque, excellent chemical resistance, low permeability, good impact resistance at low temperatures, easy to process by all methods.

**Step 3 — Applications (1 mark):**
- Blow-moulded **bottles for milk, detergents, and chemicals**.
- **Pipes** for water, gas, and sewage (PE-80, PE-100 grades).
- Injection-moulded **crates, pails, and toys**.
- **Geomembranes** for landfill liners.
- **Fuel tanks** for automobiles.

**Step 4 — Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Property | LDPE | HDPE |
|---|---|---|
| Density (g/cm³) | 0.910–0.925 | 0.941–0.965 |
| Crystallinity | 40–55% | 65–80% |
| Branching | Many LCBs | Linear |
| Tensile strength | 9–17 MPa | 25–40 MPa |
| Transparency | Translucent | Opaque |
| Polymerization | Free-radical, 1000–3000 atm | ZN, 1–10 atm |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** Many students write "LDPE is branched and HDPE is linear" — this is **partially correct but incomplete**. You must mention the **type of branching** (LDPE has both **short-chain branches** like ethyl/butyl and **long-chain branches** of 100+ carbons) and explain **how** these branches are formed (intra-chain **chain transfer to polymer** in free-radical polymerization). Marks will be cut for vague answers.

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[Alternative Choice]**

**(a) Explain the manufacture of cross-linked polyethylene (XLPE). Describe the three industrial methods with suitable reactions. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Concept (1 mark):**
Cross-linked polyethylene is a modified PE in which individual linear chains are **chemically bonded** to form a 3D network. The product is **insoluble and infusible**, with greatly improved mechanical strength, thermal endurance, chemical resistance, and stress-cracking resistance.

**Step 2 — Method 1: Peroxide Cross-linking (PEX-a) (2 marks):**
- **Reagent:** Dicumyl peroxide (DCP), 1–3 wt%.
- **Process:** The peroxide is blended with HDPE and extruded. The extrudate is then heated to **150–200 °C** in a mould or salt bath.
- **Reaction:** Peroxide decomposes to cumyloxy radicals, which abstract H from PE chains to form macroradicals. Two macroradicals on different chains combine to form a C–C crosslink.
$$\text{ROOR} \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2\text{RO}^{\bullet}$$
$$\text{RO}^{\bullet} + \text{–CH}_2\text{–} \rightarrow \text{ROH} + \text{–}\overset{\bullet}{\text{CH}}\text{–}$$
$$\text{–}\overset{\bullet}{\text{CH}}\text{–} + \text{–}\overset{\bullet}{\text{CH}}\text{–} \rightarrow \text{–CH–CH– crosslink}$$

**Step 3 — Method 2: Silane Cross-linking (PEX-b) (2 marks):**
- **Reagent:** Vinyl silane (e.g., vinyl trimethoxysilane, VTMS) + peroxide initiator.
- **Process:** Silane is grafted onto PE in an extruder (peroxide-initiated), then exposed to **hot water** in the presence of a **dibutyltin dilaurate catalyst** to hydrolyse the methoxy groups and condense adjacent silanol groups into siloxane (–Si–O–Si–) crosslinks.
- This method allows crosslinking to occur **after** the product is shaped, enabling PEX-b pipes.

**Step 4 — Method 3: Electron-beam Cross-linking (PEX-c) (1 mark):**
- The PE article is exposed to a high-energy **electron beam** (1–10 MeV) which ionises the chain and creates radicals that combine to form crosslinks.
- Advantage: no chemical additives, very clean product, used for **wire and cable insulation**.

**Step 5 — Properties and Applications (1 mark):**
- Continuous service temperature up to **95–110 °C**.
- Excellent resistance to **stress cracking, creep, and solvents**.
- Used for **hot and cold water plumbing, underfloor heating, radiator hoses, medium- and high-voltage cable insulation**.

**(b) Describe the preparation, properties, and applications of (i) Chlorinated Polyethylene (CPE) and (ii) Polyvinylidene Chloride (PVDC). (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) CPE — Chlorinated Polyethylene (3.5 marks)**

**Preparation:** CPE is produced by **post-polymerization chlorination** of HDPE in **solution** (inert solvent like CCl₄) or in **aqueous suspension** at **60–120 °C**. Chlorine is introduced into the polymer chain by **free-radical substitution**. The chlorine content is varied from **25% to 42%** by weight to tailor properties.

**Reaction:**
$$\text{–(CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{)}_n\text{–} + n\text{Cl}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{peroxide, } \Delta, h\nu} \text{–(CH}_2\text{–CHCl)}_n\text{–} + n\text{HCl}$$

**Properties:**
- Amorphous (chlorination disrupts crystallinity).
- Excellent **oil, ozone, and weather resistance**.
- Good **flame retardancy** (Cl content).
- $T_g$ increases with Cl content; low-temperature flexibility retained.
- Miscible with PVC as an **impact modifier**.

**Applications:**
- Impact modifier in **rigid PVC window profiles, pipes, and siding**.
- Rubber for **hoses, gaskets, and cable jackets**.
- Single-ply roofing membranes.

**(ii) PVDC — Polyvinylidene Chloride (3.5 marks)**

**Preparation:** Vinylidene chloride (CH₂=CCl₂) monomer is prepared by the **dehydrochlorination of 1,1,2-trichloroethane** with NaOH. PVDC is then produced by **free-radical polymerization** in **emulsion** (using K₂S₂O₈ initiator) or **suspension** (using AIBN) at **30–60 °C**. To improve processability, the monomer is **copolymerized with 10–20% vinyl chloride or methyl acrylate** (e.g., Saran™).

**Reaction:**
$$n\text{CH}_2=\text{CCl}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{K}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_8 / 50°C} \text{–(CH}_2\text{–CCl}_2\text{)}_n\text{–}$$

**Properties:**
- Density: **1.60–1.75 g/cm³** (highest among commodity plastics).
- OTR: **1–10 cm³·mm/m²·day·atm** — among the lowest of all polymers.
- Excellent **chemical resistance, flame retardancy, and barrier to O₂, H₂O vapour, and aromas**.
- Difficult to process; degrades near melting point; emits HCl on heating.

**Applications:**
- **Multilayer food packaging films** (Saran wrap) — the iconic use.
- Coatings for **paper, paperboard, and pharmaceutical blister packs**.
- Monofilaments for **insect screens, filter fabrics**.
- Barrier layer in **cosmetics and toiletries tubes**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #3:** Students frequently confuse **PVDC** (polyvinylidene chloride) with **PVC** (polyvinyl chloride). Note carefully:
> - **PVC** = –(CH₂–CHCl)– (one Cl per repeat unit, on alternate carbons).
> - **PVDC** = –(CH₂–CCl₂)– (**two Cl** per repeat unit, both on the **same** carbon).
> The 1,1-dichloro structure of PVDC produces a highly **symmetric, dense, crystalline** polymer — that is why its oxygen barrier is so exceptional.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Three monomers, six polymers** — All polymers in this module derive from ethylene (LDPE, HDPE, XLPE, CPE), propylene (PP), or vinylidene chloride (PVDC).
- **Process determines properties** — Free-radical polymerization at high pressure gives **branched, low-density** PE; coordination polymerization at low pressure gives **linear, high-density** PE.
- **Memorize the density–crystallinity chain:** LDPE 0.91 → LLDPE 0.92 → HDPE 0.96 → PP 0.90–0.92 → PVDC 1.70.
- **Polypropylene tacticity** determines whether it is a high-melting fibre/film grade (isotactic, 165 °C) or a soft rubbery waste (atactic, no true $T_m$).
- **Cross-linked PE (XLPE)** is *not* a thermoplastic — it is a thermoset-like material used where the polymer must not melt (cable insulation, hot water pipes).
- **CPE** is essentially a **PVC impact modifier** — know the Cl content range (25–42%) and the chlorination reaction.
- **PVDC** is the **oxygen barrier king** — used in Saran wrap and multilayer food packaging.
- **Key catalysts:** TiCl₄ + Al(C₂H₅)₃ (Ziegler–Natta); Cp₂ZrCl₂ + MAO (metallocene); DCP (peroxide crosslinker).
- **Three methods of XLPE:** Peroxide (PEX-a), Silane (PEX-b), E-beam (PEX-c).
- **Standard repeat unit molecular weights:** PE 28.05; PP 42.08; PVDC 96.94 g/mol.
- **Polydispersity Index (PDI):** Free-radical polymers 3–20; Ziegler–Natta 2–5; Metallocene ~2.
- **DP_n = M_n / M_0** — used in numerical problems.
- **Crystallinity formula:** $\%X_c = \dfrac{\rho_c(\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s(\rho_c - \rho_a)} \times 100$ — frequently asked in numericals.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT403 - PLASTICS MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Monomer Preparation, Polymerisation, Properties and Application of PVA, Polyvinyl Acetate, PMMA, PET, PAN, Nylon-5, 6-12, Aromatic Polyamide and Polyacrylamide, Regenerated Cellulose, and Epoxies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Definition of Key Polymer Families

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition Block — Vinyl & Condensation Polymers**
> A **polymer** is a high molecular weight compound formed by the chemical combination of a large number of repeating structural units called **monomers**. The process of their formation is **polymerisation**.

**1.1.1 Classification Relevant to this Module**

| Family | Mechanism | Examples in Module |
|---|---|---|
| **Vinyl (Addition) Polymers** | Chain-growth via free radicals/ions | PVA, PVAc, PMMA, PAN |
| **Step-Growth (Condensation) Polymers** | Stepwise with elimination of small molecules | PET, Nylons, Aramids, Epoxies |
| **Semi-Synthetic Polymers** | Chemically modified natural polymer | Regenerated Cellulose |
| **Acrylic Polymers** | Addition of acrylate/acrylamide monomers | Polyacrylamide |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Train Coupling" Model of Polymerisation**
> Imagine a railway train. Each **monomer** is a single railway coach. In **addition polymerisation**, coaches are added one-by-one to a growing chain (locomotive pulls the chain forward) — no by-product leaves. In **condensation polymerisation**, every time two coaches join, a small "ticket" (water/HCl/methanol) is dropped — the chain is built like assembling a delicate chain-link fence, where each link removal is a small sacrifice.

### 1.2 Monomer Preparation, Polymerisation, Properties & Applications — Overview

#### **1.2.1 Polyvinyl Alcohol (PVA / PVOH)**
- **Monomer**: Vinyl alcohol (CH₂=CH–OH) — unstable, so PVA is **never** made directly. It is prepared by **saponification (hydrolysis) of Polyvinyl Acetate**.
- **Monomer Preparation (indirect)**: From acetylene + acetic acid → Vinyl acetate; then hydrolysis with NaOH/CH₃OH.
- **Polymerisation**: Not directly polymerisable; obtained via **partial hydrolysis** of PVAc (87–89% hydrolyzed = fully water-soluble grade).
- **Properties**: Water-soluble film former, excellent adhesive, good oxygen barrier, biodegradable, Tg ≈ 75–85 °C, high tensile strength.
- **Applications**: Water-soluble packaging films (laundry pods, hospital laundry bags), paper sizing, textile warp sizing, sacrificial films, controlled drug release.

#### **1.2.2 Polyvinyl Acetate (PVAc)**
- **Monomer**: Vinyl acetate (CH₂=CH–OCOCH₃).
- **Monomer Preparation**: 
  1. From acetylene: CH≡CH + CH₃COOH → CH₂=CHOCOCH₃ (vapour phase, Zn(OAc)₂ catalyst).
  2. From ethylene: CH₂=CH₂ + ½ O₂ + CH₃COOH → CH₂=CHOCOCH₃ + H₂O (Pd-Au catalyst, Wacker-type).
- **Polymerisation**: Free-radical addition polymerisation (bulk, solution, emulsion, suspension).
- **Properties**: Tg ≈ 28–32 °C, transparent, optically clear, brittle at low T, flexible above Tg, good adhesion to many substrates.
- **Applications**: White glue (woodworking PVA emulsion), chewing gum base, latex paints, book-binding adhesives, paper coatings, carpet backings.

#### **1.2.3 Polymethyl Methacrylate (PMMA)**
- **Monomer**: Methyl methacrylate (MMA), CH₂=C(CH₃)–COOCH₃.
- **Monomer Preparation**:
  1. Acetone-cyanohydrin route: (CH₃)₂C=O + HCN → (CH₃)₂C(OH)CN → hydrolysis with H₂SO₄ → methacrylamide sulfate → esterification with methanol → MMA.
  2. Isobutylene oxidation route (modern): isobutylene → methacrolein → methacrylic acid → esterification → MMA.
- **Polymerisation**: Free-radical chain polymerisation; also anionic (living) for stereoregular grades.
- **Properties**: Optical transparency (~92% light transmission, better than glass), Tg ≈ 105 °C, hard, scratch-resistant surface, UV stable, weather resistant.
- **Applications**: Aircraft windows, safety glass interlayer, signage, lenses, LED light guides, dentures/bone cement, aquarium walls, optical fibres.

#### **1.2.4 Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET)**
- **Monomers**: Terephthalic acid (TPA, HOOC–C₆H₄–COOH) + Ethylene glycol (EG, HOCH₂CH₂OH).
- **Monomer Preparation**:
  1. TPA via **Amoco/Mid-Century process**: p-xylene + O₂ (Co-Mn-Br catalyst, acetic acid solvent, 200 °C, 15-30 atm).
  2. EG via oxidation of ethylene → ethylene oxide → hydrolysis.
- **Polymerisation**: Two-step esterification + polycondensation; modern **DMT (dimethyl terephthalate)** route uses trans-esterification.
- **Properties**: Tg ≈ 67–81 °C, Tm ≈ 245–265 °C, high tensile strength, excellent chemical resistance, good barrier to CO₂, semicrystalline (transparent amorphous / opaque crystalline).
- **Applications**: Beverage bottles (carbonated soft drinks), food packaging trays, polyester textile fibres ("terylene"/"dacron"), audio/video film, boil-in-bag pouches.

#### **1.2.5 Polyacrylonitrile (PAN)**
- **Monomer**: Acrylonitrile (CH₂=CH–CN).
- **Monomer Preparation**: 
  1. **Sohio process**: Propylene + NH₃ + 1.5 O₂ → acrylonitrile (Bi-Mo oxide fluidised-bed catalyst, ~450 °C).
  2. Acetylene + HCN → acrylonitrile (Reppe process, older).
- **Polymerisation**: Free-radical (mostly emulsion/solution), anionic for narrow-MWD grades.
- **Properties**: Tg ≈ 85–105 °C, Tm ≈ 317 °C (decomposes before melting), high stiffness, excellent solvent resistance, brittle, characteristic yellow-to-black on heating.
- **Applications**: Carbon-fibre precursor (after oxidative stabilisation + pyrolysis), Acrylic fibres ("Acrilan"/"Orlon"), reverse osmosis membranes, SAN/ABS copolymer component, hot-gas filtration.

#### **1.2.6 Nylon-5 (Polyamide of 5-amino-valeric acid)**
- **Monomer**: δ-valerolactam (6-membered ring, technically a 5-carbon repeat unit) OR 5-amino-valeric acid.
- **Monomer Preparation**: From cyclopentanone via Beckmann rearrangement (with hydroxylamine + H₂SO₄ → caprolactam is C6; for valerolactam, cyclopentanone oxime → Beckmann → valerolactam).
- **Polymerisation**: **Ring-opening polymerisation (ROP)** of valerolactam with water/amine initiator (hydrolytic ROP) at 250 °C.
- **Properties**: Tg ≈ 50 °C, Tm ≈ 258 °C, similar feel to Nylon-6, good dyeability, hydrophilic.
- **Applications**: Specialty fibres, research biomaterials (biodegradable scaffold).

#### **1.2.7 Nylon-6,12 (Polyamide 6,12)**
- **Monomers**: Hexamethylene diamine (HMDA, H₂N–(CH₂)₆–NH₂) + Dodecanedioic acid (HOOC–(CH₂)₁₀–COOH).
- **Monomer Preparation**:
  - HMDA from adiponitrile (from butadiene) hydrogenation.
  - Dodecanedioic acid from cyclododecatriene (CDT, trimer of butadiene) via ozonolysis or nitric acid oxidation.
- **Polymerisation**: Step-growth polycondensation (interfacial or melt) with removal of water; **AA + BB** type nylon.
- **Properties**: Lower water absorption than Nylon-6 (longer CH₂ segments), Tg ≈ 50 °C, Tm ≈ 212 °C, low moisture regain (~0.4%), excellent dimensional stability, good chemical resistance.
- **Applications**: Automotive fuel lines, brake hoses, pneumatic tubing, cable jacketing, monofilament fishing lines, toothbrush bristles.

#### **1.2.8 Aromatic Polyamide (Aramid — e.g., Kevlar®)**
- **Monomers**: p-Phenylene diamine (PPD) + Terephthaloyl chloride (TCl) for **Kevlar**; or m-phenylene diamine + isophthaloyl chloride for **Nomex**.
- **Monomer Preparation**:
  - PPD: Dinitrotoluene → reduction.
  - TCl: Terephthalic acid + SOCl₂ / PCl₃.
- **Polymerisation**: **Low-temperature solution polycondensation** (Schotten-Baumann) in NMP with CaCl₂ or LiCl — interfacial type; chain alignment occurs during spinning (liquid-crystalline solution).
- **Properties**: **Para-aramid (Kevlar)**: ultra-high tenacity, Tg ≈ 340 °C (no true melt), decomposes ~500 °C, anisotropic, density 1.44 g/cc, strength-to-weight 5× steel, flame resistant.
- **Applications**: Ballistic vests, helmets, cut-resistant gloves, brake linings, composites, ropes/cables, aerospace panels.

#### **1.2.9 Polyacrylamide (PAM)**
- **Monomer**: Acrylamide (CH₂=CH–CONH₂).
- **Monomer Preparation**: Acrylonitrile + H₂O (with Cu catalyst, partial hydrolysis → acrylamide) — **biocatalytic hydration** using **nitrile hydratase enzyme** in modern green chemistry.
- **Polymerisation**: Free-radical (aqueous solution is most common); anionic and cationic PAM made by copolymerisation / Hofmann degradation.
- **Properties**: Water-soluble (hydrogen bonding), linear flexible chain, molecular weight up to 18 million g/mol, flocculant behaviour.
- **Applications**: Wastewater treatment flocculant, enhanced oil recovery (EOR), soil conditioner/anti-erosion, paper strengthening, gel electrophoresis (PAGE), superabsorbent precursor.

#### **1.2.10 Regenerated Cellulose (Rayon / Cellophane)**
- **Source Polymer**: Natural cellulose (wood pulp / cotton linters).
- **Process (Viscose Process)**: 
  1. Steeping: cellulose + NaOH (18–20%) → alkali cellulose.
  2. Pressing & shredding & ageing (oxidative depolymerisation).
  3. Xanthation: CS₂ + NaOH-cellulose → sodium cellulose xanthate (yellow crumb).
  4. Dissolution in dilute NaOH → **viscose dope**.
  5. Spinning into H₂SO₄/Na₂SO₄/ZnSO₄ bath → regeneration as continuous filament/film.
- **Alternative**: Lyocell process (NMMO solvent, more eco-friendly).
- **Properties**: Tg ≈ 230 °C, hydrophilic, high moisture regain (11–13%), biodegradable, smooth feel, low wet strength (viscose).
- **Applications**: Textiles (rayon staple & filament), Cellophane packaging film, sausage casings, non-woven wipes.

#### **1.2.11 Epoxies (Epoxy Resins)**
- **Monomers/Prepolymers**: Bisphenol-A + Epichlorohydrin → DGEBA (diglycidyl ether of bisphenol-A).
- **Monomer Preparation (DGEBA)**:
  1. Bisphenol-A + 2 epichlorohydrin → DGEBA + HCl (NaOH catalyst).
  2. Reflux in inert atmosphere; washed free of NaCl.
- **Polymerisation**: **Curing (cross-linking)** — NOT addition polymerisation. Epoxy is a 2-component system:
  - **Resin (DGEBA)** + **Hardener** (amines, anhydrides, polyamides, dicyandiamide).
  - Step-growth with ring-opening of epoxide group, exothermic.
- **Properties**: Excellent adhesion to metals/glass/ceramics, low shrinkage on cure (<2%), high chemical resistance, Tg up to 180 °C (anhydride-cured), good electrical insulator, brittle unless toughened.
- **Applications**: Adhesives ("Araldite"), circuit board laminates (FR-4), aerospace composites, floor coatings, electronic potting, marine coatings, tooling, wind-turbine blades.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Takeaway** — Recognise **monomer → polymer** routes as either:
> (a) **Direct** polymerisation of monomer → polymer (PVAc, PMMA, PET, PAN, Nylon-6,12, Aramid, PAM).
> (b) **Indirect** via prepolymer transformation (PVA from PVAc, regenerated cellulose from cellulose xanthate).
> (c) **Cure** of multifunctional prepolymer (epoxies).

## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Polymerisation Mechanism Fundamentals

#### **2.1.1 Free-Radical Addition Polymerisation (Vinyl Polymers)**
Three classic stages — **initiation → propagation → termination**.

**Initiation:**
1. Initiator decomposition: I–I → 2 I• (e.g., AIBN → 2 (CH₃)₂(CN)C• + N₂; BPO → 2 C₆H₅COO•)
2. Initiation of monomer: I• + CH₂=CHX → I–CH₂–CH•X

**Propagation:** R–(CH₂–CHX)ₙ• + CH₂=CHX → R–(CH₂–CHX)ₙ₊₁•

**Termination:**
- Combination: 2 P• → P–P
- Disproportionation: 2 P• → P=CHX + P–CH₂–CH₂X

**Chain transfer:** P• + XY → P–X + Y• (controls MWD)

#### **2.1.2 Step-Growth Condensation Polymerisation**
- Any two reactive chain-ends can combine at any time (no initiator).
- Conversion must be very high (>99%) to achieve high molecular weight (Carothers' equation).
- Each step eliminates a small molecule (H₂O, HCl, CH₃OH).

#### **2.1.3 Ring-Opening Polymerisation (ROP)**
- Cyclic monomer (e.g., lactam) opens and adds to chain end; water/amine acts as initiator.
- For Nylon-5: valerolactam + H₂O → amino acid → ROP → nylon-5.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why These Polymers Matter in Engineering Practice**
> - **PVA films** → unit-dose detergent "pods" and biodegradable hospital laundry bags — a single-use sustainability win.

### 2.4 Cross-Linking Theory Specific to Epoxies

Epoxy curing follows **step-growth kinetics** with branching. The cross-link density $\nu_e$ governs Tg:

$$
\nu_e = \dfrac{\rho \cdot E''}{3 R T}
$$

where $E''$ is the rubbery plateau modulus from dynamic mechanical analysis. Higher functionality of amine/anhydride hardener → higher cross-link density → higher Tg, lower elongation, higher chemical resistance.
