## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of Carothers' Equation (Step-Growth DP)

> Used extensively for **PET, Nylon-6,12, Aramid, PAM, Epoxy curing**.

**Starting principle**: In step-growth, every monomer link-up is a random event. Let p = fraction of functional groups that have reacted.

**Step 1 — Probability of finding an unreacted group at a chain end:**
The probability that a given functional group is unreacted is $(1-p)$.

**Step 2 — A chain of n repeat units requires (n−1) bonds and 2 unreacted ends:**
Number-average DP $\overline{X}_n$ = probability of picking one molecule, then all units of that molecule.

**Step 3 — Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{X}_n &= \frac{\text{Total number of monomer units originally present}}{\text{Total number of molecules remaining}} \\
&= \frac{N_0}{N} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

After reaction, total functional groups remaining = $N_0 \cdot (1-p)$ molecules each have 2 ends, so $N = N_0(1-p)$.

Therefore:

$$
\overline{X}_n = \frac{N_0}{N_0 (1-p)} = \frac{1}{1-p}
$$

This is **Carothers' equation** — required for **p > 0.99** to achieve $\overline{X}_n > 100$.

**Step 4 — Worked numerical example for PET**:

Take TPA + EG, p = 0.99 (i.e., 99% ester bonds formed). Find $\overline{X}_n$:

$$
\overline{X}_n = \frac{1}{1-0.99} = \frac{1}{0.01} = 100
$$

For textile-grade PET, $\overline{X}_n \approx 100–120$ (M_n ≈ 19,200–23,000 g/mol). For bottle-grade, $\overline{X}_n \approx 150$ → p ≈ 0.9933.

### 3.2 Derivation of Mark-Houwink-Sakurada Equation

**Step 1 — Intrinsic viscosity [η]** relates to molecular size:

$$
[\eta] = \lim_{c \to 0} \left(\frac{\eta_{sp}}{c}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Empirically (Staudinger, Flory), [η] scales with M:**

$$
[\eta] = K \cdot M^a
$$

where K and a are polymer–solvent–temperature specific constants.

**Step 3 — Take logarithm for linear plot:**

$$
\log[\eta] = \log K + a \log M
$$

By measuring [η] at several M (via MALLS or osmometry) and plotting log[η] vs log M, slope = **a**, intercept = **log K**.

**Step 4 — Numerical example for PMMA in chloroform at 25 °C**: K = 0.0044 mL/g; a = 0.79.

If measured [η] = 1.00 dL/g:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1.00 \times 1000 \; \text{mL/g} &= 0.0044 \cdot M^{0.79} \\
M^{0.79} &= \frac{1000}{0.0044} = 2.273 \times 10^{5} \\
M &= (2.273 \times 10^{5})^{1/0.79} \\
&= (2.273 \times 10^{5})^{1.2658} \\
&\approx 4.1 \times 10^{6} \; \text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

This molecular weight is typical of high-impact acrylic sheet-grade PMMA.

### 3.3 Derivation of Free-Radical Polymerisation Rate (Rp)

**Step 1 — Initiation rate**: $R_i = 2 f k_d [I]$, where f = initiator efficiency.

**Step 2 — Steady state**: $R_i = R_t \Rightarrow [P\cdot] = \left(\dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}\right)^{1/2}$

**Step 3 — Propagation rate**:

$$
R_p = k_p [M][P\cdot] = k_p [M] \left(\frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}\right)^{1/2}
$$

**Step 4 — Degree of polymerisation in steady state**:

$$
\overline{X}_n = \frac{R_p}{R_i + R_{tr}} = \frac{k_p [M]}{2(fk_d k_t [I])^{1/2} + k_{tr}[X]}
$$

This is why **low initiator concentration** and **high monomer concentration** give high MW (relevant to PAN, PMMA, PVAc).

### 3.5 Worked Example — Production of 10 kg PET at p = 0.99

**Given**: p = 0.99, target M_n ≈ 19,200 g/mol.
**Find**: Mass of water removed.

**Step 1**: M₀ = 192 g/mol → X_n = 100.

**Step 2**: For 10 kg polymer, moles of repeat unit = 10,000 / 192 = 52.08 mol.

**Step 3**: Each TPA + EG coupling releases 1 H₂O. Number of ester bonds formed = 52.08 × 99 = 51.56 mol.

**Step 4**: Mass of H₂O removed = 51.56 × 18 = **928 g of water removed per 10 kg PET**.

This justifies why **PET reactors have vertical column condensers and vacuum systems** — water must be driven out continuously to push equilibrium forward (Le Chatelier's principle).

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

Explain the preparation of **Polyvinyl Alcohol (PVA)** from vinyl acetate. Why is PVA not synthesised by direct polymerisation of vinyl alcohol?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

PVA cannot be made directly because **vinyl alcohol (CH₂=CH–OH) is unstable and tautomerises to acetaldehyde (CH₃CHO)**. Instead:

1. **Step 1 — Polymerisation of Vinyl Acetate:** Vinyl acetate (CH₂=CHOCOCH₃) is polymerised by free-radical mechanism in methanol to give polyvinyl acetate (PVAc).
2. **Step 2 — Saponification (Hydrolysis):** PVAc is treated with **sodium hydroxide in methanol** (or methanol/KOH), which converts the acetate groups to hydroxyl groups, releasing methyl acetate as by-product:

$$
\text{-(CH}_2\text{-CH(OCOCH}_3\text{))}_n \xrightarrow{\text{NaOH/CH}_3\text{OH}} \text{-(CH}_2\text{-CHOH)-}_n + n \; \text{CH}_3\text{COOCH}_3
$$

The degree of hydrolysis (typically 87–89%) controls water solubility and crystallinity.

**(1 mark for tautomerism reason, 1 mark for polymerisation, 1 mark for saponification step.)**

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

Write a short note on **Regenerated Cellulose** with the **Viscose Process**.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Regenerated cellulose is **cellulose that has been dissolved and re-precipitated as a continuous fibre or film** from natural cellulose (wood pulp).

**Viscose Process steps (each 1 mark for 2 main steps + 1 for application):**
1. **Steeping**: Cellulose treated with 18–20% NaOH → alkali cellulose.
2. **Xanthation**: Alkali cellulose reacts with **CS₂** → sodium cellulose xanthate, which dissolves in dilute NaOH to form **viscose dope**.
3. **Spinning & Regeneration**: Viscose is extruded into an acid bath (H₂SO₄/Na₂SO₄/ZnSO₄) where it regenerates as continuous cellulose filament (rayon) or film (cellophane).

Application: textiles (rayon), packaging (cellophane), tyre cord, surgical dressings.

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each) — KTU Internal Choice Format

---

### **QUESTION A** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Discuss the monomer preparation, polymerisation mechanism, and key properties of **Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET)**. Explain why PET can be both a fibre and a bottle-grade polymer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Nylon-6,12** and **Aromatic Polyamide (Kevlar)** with respect to monomer preparation, structure, Tg, density, water absorption, and applications. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION

**Part (a) — PET: Monomer, Polymerisation, Properties (7 Marks)**

**1. Monomer Preparation (2 Marks):**
- **Terephthalic Acid (TPA):** Made by **Amoco/Mid-Century oxidation** of p-xylene using O₂/air with Co-Mn-Br catalyst in acetic acid solvent at 200 °C and 15–30 atm:

$$
\text{p-CH}_3\text{C}_6\text{H}_4\text{CH}_3 + 2\text{ O}_2 \rightarrow \text{HOOC-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{COOH} + 2 \text{ H}_2\text{O}
$$

- **Ethylene Glycol (EG):** Ethylene → ethylene oxide (Ag catalyst) → hydrolysis:

$$
\text{CH}_2\text{=CH}_2 \xrightarrow{O_2/Ag} \text{CH}_2\text{OCH}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \text{HOCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH}
$$

**2. Polymerisation (2 Marks):**
PET is made by **two-step esterification + polycondensation**:
- **Step 1 (Esterification):** TPA + EG at 240–260 °C, 3–4 atm → BHET oligomer + H₂O.
- **Step 2 (Polycondensation):** 280–290 °C, <1 mm Hg vacuum, Sb₂O₃ catalyst → high MW PET + ethylene glycol.

Modern route uses **DMT (dimethyl terephthalate)** with trans-esterification followed by polycondensation.

$$
\text{n HOOC-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-COOH + n HOCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH} \rightarrow \text{-(OCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OOC-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{CO)-}_n + 2n\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**3. Properties (2 Marks):**
- Tg ≈ 67–81 °C, Tm ≈ 245–265 °C.
- High tensile strength, low creep, excellent chemical resistance.
- Good barrier to CO₂ and O₂ (critical for carbonated beverages).
- Semicrystalline: amorphous → transparent (films, bottles); crystalline → opaque, high-strength (fibres).

**4. Why both Fibre and Bottle? (1 Mark):**
- **Fibre grade:** Slow crystallisation under tension → oriented crystalline → high tenacity yarn ("terylene"/"dacron").
- **Bottle grade:** Quench cooling gives amorphous preform → injection stretch blow moulding at ~100 °C (above Tg) → strain-induced crystallisation → transparent, biaxially oriented, pressure-resistant bottle.

---

**Part (b) — Nylon-6,12 vs. Aramid (Kevlar) Comparison (7 Marks)**

| Parameter | **Nylon-6,12** | **Aramid (Kevlar)** |
|---|---|---|
| Monomer type | Aliphatic AA + BB (HMDA + dodecanedioic acid) | Aromatic AA + BB (PPD + terephthaloyl chloride) |
| Polymerisation | Melt polycondensation, water removal | Low-T solution (Schotten-Baumann), HCl removed |
| Repeat unit | –NH(CH₂)₆NHCO(CH₂)₁₀CO– | –NH–C₆H₄–NH–CO–C₆H₄–CO– |
| Tg | ~50 °C | ~340 °C (no melt) |
| Tm | ~212 °C | Decomposes before melting |
| Density | 1.06 g/cc | 1.44 g/cc |
| Water absorption | 0.4% (low) | 3.5% (low) but tight H-bonds |
| Tensile strength | 50–60 MPa | 2800–3600 MPa (5× steel) |
| Applications | Fuel lines, monofilaments | Body armour, ropes, composites |

**Why Kevlar is so strong (2 Marks explanation):**
- **Para-substituted aromatic rings** give rigid rod-like chains.
- Strong **interchain hydrogen bonding** between –NH– and –CO– groups, plus π-π stacking.
- Chains pack into highly oriented liquid-crystalline domains during spinning.
- Result: exceptional axial strength, low elongation, high modulus.

---

### **QUESTION B** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Discuss the **monomer preparation (Sohio process)** and **polymerisation** of **Polyacrylonitrile (PAN)**. Why is PAN used as a carbon-fibre precursor? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat flow diagram, explain the **synthesis of Epoxy resin (DGEBA)** and its **curing mechanism** with diamine hardener. List four industrial applications. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION

**Part (a) — PAN Monomer, Polymerisation & Carbon-Fibre Use (7 Marks)**

**1. Sohio Process (3 Marks):**

The dominant commercial route is the **Sohio process** (Standard Oil of Ohio), using a fluidised-bed catalytic reactor:

$$
\text{CH}_2\text{=CH–CH}_3 + \text{NH}_3 + 1.5\,\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Bi-Mo-Ox, 450°C}} \text{CH}_2\text{=CH–CN} + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

- **Catalyst:** Bismuth-molybdenum oxide supported on silica.
- **By-products:** HCN (recovered and recycled), acetonitrile, acrolein.
- **Yield:** ~75–80% selectivity to acrylonitrile.

**2. Polymerisation (2 Marks):**
- Free-radical chain polymerisation: aqueous **suspension**, **emulsion** (for latex), or **solution** (DMF solvent for fibre spinning).
- Initiator: AIBN, potassium persulphate, or redox initiators.
- Strict control of MW needed (M_w 50,000–150,000 for fibres).

$$
\text{n CH}_2\text{=CH–CN} \xrightarrow{\text{I}^\bullet} \text{-(CH}_2\text{-CH(CN))}_n\text{-}
$$

**3. Why PAN as Carbon-Fibre Precursor (2 Marks):**
- PAN has a **high carbon yield (~50–55%)** after pyrolysis, ideal for carbon-fibre production.
- The **–CN group** undergoes cyclisation to form a ladder polymer (stabilisation step) at 200–300 °C in air.
- Subsequent carbonisation at 1000–1500 °C in inert atmosphere converts to nearly pure carbon fibre with very high tensile strength and modulus.
- Three stages: (i) oxidative stabilisation → (ii) carbonisation → (iii) graphitisation (optional).

---

**Part (b) — Epoxy (DGEBA) Synthesis & Curing (7 Marks)**

**1. DGEBA Synthesis (3 Marks):**

DGEBA = **Diglycidyl Ether of Bisphenol-A**, made by reacting Bisphenol-A with excess **epichlorohydrin** in presence of NaOH:

**Step 1 (Etherification):** BPA + Epichlorohydrin → Chlorohydrin intermediate.

**Step 2 (Dehydrochlorination):** NaOH removes HCl → epoxide ring forms.

**Overall:**

$$
\text{BPA} + 2\,\text{Epichlorohydrin} \xrightarrow{\text{NaOH}} \text{DGEBA} + 2\,\text{NaCl} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

Reaction conducted at 60–80 °C; product washed free of NaCl; molecular weight controlled by BPA/epichlorohydrin ratio.

**2. Curing Mechanism with Diamine (e.g., Ethylene diamine, DETA) (2 Marks):**

Primary amine attacks epoxide ring → ring opens → secondary alcohol + secondary amine. Secondary amine then attacks another epoxide → tertiary amine + another OH. Each H of –NH₂ opens one epoxide.

**Step 1:**
$$\text{R-NH}_2 + \text{Epoxide} \rightarrow \text{R-NH-CH}_2\text{-CH(OH)-R'}$$

**Step 2:**
$$\text{R-NH-CH}_2\text{-CH(OH)-R'} + \text{Epoxide} \rightarrow \text{R-N(–CH}_2\text{-CH(OH)-R')}_2$$

This produces a **3-D cross-linked network** (thermoset).

**3. Four Industrial Applications (2 Marks):**
1. Structural adhesives (Araldite, Loctite epoxy) for metals, ceramics.
2. FR-4 printed circuit board laminates (epoxy + glass fibre).
3. Carbon-fibre reinforced composites in aerospace (Boeing 787, Airbus A350).
4. Floor coatings and marine primers — chemical-resistant top coats.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing PVA with PVAc**: PVA = polyvinyl alcohol (water-soluble); PVAc = polyvinyl acetate (white glue). Both are *different polymers*. Writing "polyvinyl alcohol is made from vinyl alcohol" loses a mark — vinyl alcohol does NOT exist as a stable monomer.
> 2. **PET monomer mistake**: Some students write "ethylene + terephthalic acid" → yes, but the actual feed is ethylene glycol, not ethylene. EG ≠ ethylene.
> 3. **Forgetting by-product removal in step-growth**: Carothers' equation only works if water/HCl/methanol is removed. If you do not mention vacuum or stripping column, you lose process-engineering marks.
> 4. **PAN melting confusion**: PAN does NOT melt — it decomposes exothermally at ~317 °C. Stating "PAN Tm = 317 °C" without "decomposes" is incomplete.
> 5. **Epoxy vs. addition polymer**: Epoxy is **NOT addition polymerised** — it is a **thermoset** made by **step-growth curing** of a multifunctional prepolymer. Saying "epoxy is an addition polymer" is a serious error.
> 6. **Aramid monomer mistake**: Kevlar uses **p-phenylene diamine + terephthaloyl chloride**, NOT isophthalic acid. Meta-aramid = Nomex (different polymer).
> 7. **Regenerated cellulose ≠ cellulose acetate**: Viscose process regenerates pure cellulose, whereas acetylation gives cellulose acetate (a different ester).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 (Polymers Covered)**

- **PVA**: NEVER made directly; only by saponification of PVAc. Vinyl alcohol tautomerises to acetaldehyde → unstable.
- **PVAc**: Vinyl acetate monomer → free-radical polymerisation → wood glue (emulsion). Tg ≈ 30 °C.
- **PMMA**: MMA from acetone-cyanohydrin or isobutylene route → bulk cast sheets for aircraft windows. Tg ≈ 105 °C; optically transparent (92%).
- **PET**: TPA (Amoco oxidation of p-xylene) + EG → two-step esterification + polycondensation. Fibre-grade vs. bottle-grade by strain-induced crystallisation.
- **PAN**: Acrylonitrile from Sohio process (propylene + NH₃ + 1.5 O₂, Bi-Mo-Ox catalyst, 450 °C). Carbon-fibre precursor because of high carbon yield and ladder-polymer stabilisation.
- **Nylon-5**: Valerolactam (5-ring lactam) → hydrolytic ROP → polyamide of 5-amino-valeric acid.
- **Nylon-6,12**: HMDA + dodecanedioic acid → AA-BB step-growth → low water absorption, automotive fuel lines.
- **Aromatic Polyamide (Kevlar)**: PPD + terephthaloyl chloride → low-T solution polycondensation in NMP/LiCl → liquid-crystal spinning → ultra-high tenacity fibre.
- **Polyacrylamide**: Acrylamide from acrylonitrile hydration (nitrile hydratase enzyme route is green) → free-radical polymerisation in water → flocculant for EOR and wastewater.
- **Regenerated Cellulose**: Viscose process — cellulose → alkali cellulose → cellulose xanthate → viscose dope → acid bath regeneration. Modern alternative: Lyocell (NMMO solvent).
- **Epoxy (DGEBA)**: BPA + epichlorohydrin (NaOH) → DGEBA prepolymer → **cure with amines/anhydrides** to cross-linked thermoset (NOT addition polymerised). Applications: adhesives, FR-4 PCBs, composites, coatings.
- **Universal equations to memorise**: Carothers $\overline{X}_n = 1/(1-p)$; PDI = 1+p; $[\eta] = K M^a$; $T_g \approx 2/3 \, T_m$ (Kelvin).
- **Mechanism keywords**: Initiation → Propagation → Termination (free-radical); Step-growth with by-product removal; Ring-opening polymerisation (lactams); Curing with hardener (epoxies).
- **Direct vs. Indirect polymerisation**: PVA and regenerated cellulose are the only "indirect" routes in this module — all others are direct polymerisation of prepared monomers.
- **Industrial mnemonics** — "PPMA-PVA-PA-PAM" (all vinyl addition); "PET-Nylon-Kevlar-Epoxy" (all step-growth); "Nylon-5" (ROP); "Cellulose" (regeneration of natural polymer).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Monomer Preparation, Polymerisation, Properties and Application of PU, PTFE, Polyvinyl Fluoride, Polyvinylidene Fluoride, HIPS, and Polyacetal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT403 - PLASTICS MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Monomer Preparation, Polymerisation, Properties and Application of PU, PTFE, Polyvinyl Fluoride, Polyvinylidene Fluoride, HIPS, and Polyacetal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Polyurethane (PU)

**Formal Definition:** Polyurethane (PU) is a class of polymers containing the characteristic urethane linkage (–NH–CO–O–) formed by the step-growth polyaddition reaction between a diisocyanate (or polyisocyanate) and a diol (or polyol) in the presence of suitable catalysts and chain extenders.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** PU is NOT formed by a condensation reaction that releases small molecules (despite older classification); it is a true **polyaddition** (step-growth) reaction. The byproduct in foamed PU is CO₂ (from water-isocyanate reaction), not from polymer chain formation.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of PU as a "molecular Lego" where you have two types of blocks: diisocyanates (blocks with two "hooks" on each end) and polyols (long flexible chains with "hooks" on both ends). When the hooks of different blocks meet, they snap together to form a strong urethane bond, creating a continuous network whose flexibility can be tuned by choosing polyols of different chain lengths.

The general urethane-forming reaction is:
$$n\ \text{OCN–R–NCO} + n\ \text{HO–R'–OH} \longrightarrow [-\text{OCN–R–NH–CO–O–R'–O}-]_n$$

---

## 1.2 Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE)

**Formal Definition:** Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) is a fully fluorinated linear thermoplastic polymer of the repeat unit –(CF₂–CF₂)ₙ–, prepared by free-radical polymerisation of the gaseous monomer tetrafluoroethylene (TFE, CF₂=CF₂) under high pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** PTFE has a melting point of approximately **327 °C**, a service temperature range of **–260 °C to +260 °C**, and a coefficient of friction of about **0.05–0.10** against polished steel — among the lowest of any known solid.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine coating your kitchen pan with a "Teflon jacket" — a sheath of carbon atoms, each tightly wrapped by four fluorine atoms. The fluorine sheath is so tightly packed and electronically stable that practically nothing sticks, slides, or reacts with it. This is the molecular basis of its "non-stick" reputation.

---

## 1.3 Polyvinyl Fluoride (PVF)

**Formal Definition:** Polyvinyl fluoride (PVF) is a semi-crystalline linear polymer with the repeat unit –(CH₂–CHF)ₙ–, obtained by free-radical polymerisation of vinyl fluoride (VF, CH₂=CHF), a partially fluorinated monomer.

**Conceptual Analogy:** PVF is like a "half-fluorinated" version of PTFE — only every alternate carbon in the backbone carries a fluorine, the others carry hydrogen. This asymmetric fluorination makes PVF tougher and easier to process as thin films, but it sacrifices some of the chemical inertness of PTFE.

---

## 1.4 Polyvinylidene Fluoride (PVDF)

**Formal Definition:** Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) is a highly crystalline linear fluoropolymer of the repeat unit –(CH₂–CF₂)ₙ– prepared by free-radical or suspension polymerisation of vinylidene fluoride (VDF, CH₂=CF₂).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** PVDF exhibits strong **piezoelectric** and **pyroelectric** properties when electrically poled — a rare property among thermoplastics.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Whereas PTFE has all carbons fully fluorinated, PVDF has alternating –CF₂– and –CH₂– units. The dipole created by this asymmetry (each CF₂ carries a strong –C–F dipole) is the key to its piezoelectric behaviour — squeezing the film generates a measurable voltage.

---

## 1.5 High Impact Polystyrene (HIPS)

**Formal Definition:** High Impact Polystyrene (HIPS) is a modified polystyrene produced by dissolving polybutadiene (PBD) rubber in styrene monomer and then polymerising the styrene by free-radical mechanism. The resulting two-phase system contains rubber particles dispersed in a polystyrene matrix, with graft copolymer at the interface acting as a compatibiliser.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Picture plain polystyrene as a brittle glass cake. Now imagine embedding tiny rubber balls ("stress absorbers") throughout this cake. When you try to break it, the crack must travel around these rubber particles, dissipating energy. The result is a polymer that is much tougher than the sum of its parts — exactly what HIPS achieves.

---

## 1.6 Polyacetal (Polyoxymethylene, POM)

**Formal Definition:** Polyacetal, also called polyoxymethylene (POM) or acetal resin, is a highly crystalline linear engineering thermoplastic with the repeat unit –(CH₂–O)ₙ–, prepared by polymerisation of formaldehyde (or its cyclic trimer, 1,3,5-trioxane) using anionic or cationic catalysts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** POM has a continuous service temperature of approximately **–40 °C to +100 °C**, a melting point of **165–181 °C**, a tensile strength of about **70 MPa**, and a low coefficient of friction (≈ **0.2–0.3**).

**Conceptual Analogy:** POM is like a "molecular version of a metal gear": like metal, it is stiff, dimensionally precise, low-friction, and tough — but unlike metal, it is light, self-lubricating, and corrosion-resistant. It is often called the "polymer steel" of engineering plastics.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Polyurethane (PU) — Theory

### 2.1.1 Monomer Preparation
PU is unique in that it has **no single monomer**. It is built from two co-monomers:

**A. Diisocyanates (RDI):** Prepared by **phosgenation of diamines**.
$$\text{H}_2\text{N–R–NH}_2 + 2\ \text{COCl}_2 \longrightarrow \text{OCN–R–NCO} + 4\ \text{HCl}$$
Common diisocyanates:
- **MDI** (Methylene diphenyl diisocyanate) — aromatic, gives rigid foams
- **TDI** (Toluene diisocyanate, 80:20 2,4-/2,6-isomer) — aromatic, flexible foams
- **HDI** (Hexamethylene diisocyanate) — aliphatic, gives light-stable coatings
- **IPDI** (Isophorone diisocyanate) — cycloaliphatic

**B. Polyols:**
- **Polyether polyols:** From propylene oxide / ethylene oxide on glycerol
- **Polyester polyols:** From adipic acid + ethylene glycol

### 2.1.2 Polymerisation Mechanism — Step-Growth Polyaddition
PU polymerisation is a **step-growth polyaddition** (no small molecule released). The reaction is catalysed by tertiary amines (e.g., DABCO) or organotin compounds (e.g., dibutyltin dilaurate).

$$\text{R–N=C=O} + \text{H–O–R'} \longrightarrow \text{R–NH–CO–O–R'}$$

The mechanism proceeds via:
1. **Nucleophilic attack:** The lone pair on the polyol oxygen attacks the electrophilic carbon of the –N=C=O group.
2. **Proton transfer:** A proton migrates from oxygen to nitrogen, forming the stable carbamate (urethane) linkage.

### 2.1.3 Structure–Property Relationships
- **Soft segments** (long polyol chains) → flexibility, low-Tg elastomeric behaviour.
- **Hard segments** (diisocyanate + chain extender) → rigidity, hydrogen bonding, crystallinity.
- This **microphase separation** of hard and soft domains is the key to PU's tunable properties.

### 2.1.4 Applications
- Flexible PU foams (mattresses, seat cushions)
- Rigid PU foams (insulation panels, refrigerators)
- PU elastomers (spandex, shoe soles, rollers)
- PU coatings and adhesives
- Reaction Injection Moulding (RIM) parts (car bumpers)

---

## 2.2 PTFE — Theory

### 2.2.1 Monomer Preparation — Tetrafluoroethylene (TFE, CF₂=CF₂)
The industrial route proceeds in three steps:

**Step 1:** Chloroform + HF → dichlorodifluoromethane (R-12)
$$\text{CHCl}_3 + 2\ \text{HF} \xrightarrow{\text{SbF}_5} \text{CHClF}_2 + 2\ \text{HCl}$$

**Step 2:** Pyrolysis of R-12 to TFE (≈ 700–900 °C, vacuum)
$$2\ \text{CHClF}_2 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{CF}_2{=}\text{CF}_2 + 2\ \text{HCl}$$

**Step 3:** TFE is purified by distillation (bp = –76 °C). TFE is **highly explosive** and must be handled with extreme care — this is a frequently tested KTU fact.

### 2.2.2 Polymerisation
- **Free-radical polymerisation** in aqueous medium (emulsion or suspension) under high pressure (10–100 atm) at 40–80 °C.
- Initiator: persulphates, peroxides, or redox systems.
- The polymerisation is **highly exothermic** (ΔH ≈ –172 kJ/mol).

### 2.2.3 Structure–Property Relationships
- The C–F bond is one of the strongest in organic chemistry (**≈ 485 kJ/mol**).
- The fluorine atoms form a continuous helical sheath around the carbon backbone, providing a **steric and electronic shield**.
- PTFE is essentially **semicrystalline** (≈ 50–70 % crystallinity) with a very high melting point.
- The helical conformation prevents close packing only below 19 °C (phase II → phase IV transition).

### 2.2.4 Applications
- Non-stick cookware coatings
- Chemical-resistant gaskets, seals, valve seats
- High-frequency electrical insulation (radomes, cable insulation)
- Bearings and bushings (self-lubricating)
- Medical implants (expanded PTFE — Gore-Tex)

---

## 2.3 PVF (Polyvinyl Fluoride) — Theory

### 2.3.1 Monomer Preparation — Vinyl Fluoride (VF, CH₂=CHF)
Two main routes:

**Route A (Acetylene route — predominant industrially):**
$$\text{CH}{\equiv}\text{CH} + \text{HF} \xrightarrow{\text{HgCl}_2/\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3, 40\text{–}100\ ^\circ\text{C}} \text{CH}_2{=}\text{CHF}$$
$$\text{CH}_2{=}\text{CHF} + \text{HF} \xrightarrow{} \text{CH}_3\text{–CHF}_2 \text{ (by-product, recycled)}$$

**Route B (Ethylene route):**
$$\text{CH}_2{=}\text{CH}_2 + \text{HF} \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{–CHF} \xrightarrow{-\text{HCl}} \text{CH}_2{=}\text{CHF}$$

### 2.3.2 Polymerisation
- **Free-radical polymerisation** at 50–150 °C, 10–300 atm.
- Initiator: peroxides, azo compounds.
- Suspension or emulsion polymerisation is preferred.

### 2.3.3 Properties
- Excellent **UV and weather resistance** (used as Tedlar film — DuPont).
- Tough and abrasion-resistant; transparent films.
- Melting point ≈ 190–200 °C.
- **Cannot be melt-processed** (degrades before melting) — must be processed from solution or as a film.

### 2.3.4 Applications
- Protective films for solar panels, aircraft interiors, building facades
- Lamination on metal and plastic sheets
- Release films in composite manufacture

---

## 2.4 PVDF (Polyvinylidene Fluoride) — Theory

### 2.4.1 Monomer Preparation — Vinylidene Fluoride (VDF, CH₂=CF₂)
The main industrial route is **dehydrohalogenation of HCFC-142b**:
$$\text{CH}_3\text{–CClF}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{600–900 °C, vacuum}} \text{CH}_2{=}\text{CF}_2 + \text{HCl}$$
HCFC-142b itself is obtained by:
$$\text{CH}_3\text{–CCl}_3 + 2\ \text{HF} \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{–CClF}_2 + 2\ \text{HCl}$$

### 2.4.2 Polymerisation
- **Free-radical polymerisation** in suspension, emulsion, or bulk.
- Operating conditions: 10–130 atm, 30–150 °C.

### 2.4.3 Properties
- Highly crystalline (up to 70 %).
- **Multiple crystal forms (phases):** α, β, γ, δ — the β-phase is responsible for piezoelectric behaviour.
- Melting point ≈ 171–180 °C.
- Excellent chemical resistance, mechanical strength, and flame retardancy.
- **Piezoelectric coefficient (d₃₃):** ≈ –33 pC/N in β-phase.

### 2.4.4 Applications
- Chemical process piping and tanks
- Wire and cable insulation (high-purity)
- **Piezoelectric sensors, actuators, transducers**
- Lithium-ion battery binders and separators
- Architectural coatings (Kynar 500)

---

## 2.5 HIPS (High Impact Polystyrene) — Theory

### 2.5.1 Composition
- **Polystyrene (PS) matrix:** 85–95 %
- **Polybutadiene (PBD) rubber:** 5–15 % (in the form of dispersed particles of 1–10 µm)
- **Graft copolymer (PS-g-PBD):** formed in situ at the interface

### 2.5.2 Polymerisation — Two-Stage Process
1. **Pre-polymerisation (bulk or solution):** Dissolve PBD rubber in styrene monomer; initiate free-radical polymerisation at ≈ 80–120 °C. As PS forms, it phase-separates. The growing PS radicals graft onto PBD backbones, producing a **graft copolymer (PS-g-PBD)** that acts as a **surfactant** at the PS/PBD interface.
2. **Suspension or bulk finish:** Convert remaining monomer to PS while maintaining rubber particle morphology.

### 2.5.3 Toughening Mechanism
- Rubber particles act as **stress concentrators**.
- Under impact, **crazing** is initiated at rubber–matrix interfaces.
- Crazes absorb impact energy → greatly increased **notch impact strength** (10–20× higher than plain PS).
- Rubber particles also help **arrest crack propagation**.

### 2.5.4 Applications
- Refrigerator linings and door liners
- TV and computer housings
- Disposable cups and food packaging (yoghurt cups)
- Toys (LEGO bricks were originally ABS, now HIPS for some parts)
- Automotive interior trims

---

## 2.6 Polyacetal (POM) — Theory

### 2.6.1 Monomer Preparation

**Route A — Formaldehyde route:**
Methanol is oxidised to formaldehyde:
$$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \tfrac{1}{2}\ \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Ag or Fe–Mo catalyst}} \text{HCHO} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
Formaldehyde is then polymerised.

**Route B — Trioxane route (more common industrially):**
Formaldehyde is trimerised to **1,3,5-trioxane** in the presence of acid:
$$3\ \text{HCHO} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} (\text{CH}_2\text{O})_3$$
Trioxane is then polymerised with a small amount of **1,3-dioxolane** (comonomer) for stability.

### 2.6.2 Polymerisation
- **Anionic polymerisation** of pure formaldehyde (gives **homo-polymer**, e.g., Delrin by DuPont).
- **Cationic ring-opening polymerisation** of trioxane (gives **co-polymer**, e.g., Hostaform, Celcon).
- The co-monomer (1,3-dioxolane) introduces –(CH₂–CH₂–O)– units that interrupt long –CH₂–O– sequences and **prevent "unzipping"** of the chain by thermal depolymerisation back to formaldehyde.

### 2.6.3 Structure–Property Relationships
- Highly crystalline (60–80 %).
- Strong, stiff, hard material.
- Excellent fatigue resistance and creep resistance.
- Low water absorption → high dimensional stability.
- Continuous use temperature ≈ 100 °C.

### 2.6.4 Applications
- Precision gears, bearings, conveyor belts
- Fasteners, zippers, snap-fit assemblies
- Automotive fuel system components, window lift mechanisms
- Plumbing fittings (push-fit)
- Medical devices (insulin pens, inhaler housings)

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet & Key Parameter Table

| Property / Parameter | PU | PTFE | PVF | PVDF | HIPS | POM |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Repeat Unit | –NH–CO–O– | –CF₂–CF₂– | –CH₂–CHF– | –CH₂–CF₂– | PS + PBD | –CH₂–O– |
| Monomer(s) | Diisocyanate + Polyol | TFE (CF₂=CF₂) | VF (CH₂=CHF) | VDF (CH₂=CF₂) | Styrene + PBD | HCHO or trioxane |
| Polymerisation Type | Step-growth (polyaddition) | Free-radical (high pressure) | Free-radical | Free-radical / suspension | Free-radical (with rubber) | Anionic / cationic |
| Density (g/cm³) | 1.10–1.25 | 2.13–2.20 | 1.37–1.39 | 1.74–1.78 | 1.04–1.06 | 1.41–1.42 |
| Melting Point (°C) | Amorphous (Tg ≈ –50 to 130) | 327 | 190–200 | 171–180 | Amorphous (Tg ≈ 100) | 165–181 |
| Tensile Strength (MPa) | 10–80 | 10–40 | 50–110 | 35–55 | 15–40 | 60–75 |
| Special Feature | Microphase separation | Lowest µ, chemical inert | UV resistant | Piezoelectric | High impact | Low friction, fatigue |
| Typical Use | Foams, elastomers | Non-stick coatings | Films | Sensors | Packaging | Gears, bearings |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Trick:** When asked for the **monomer of PTFE**, students often write "tetrafluoroethane." The correct answer is **tetrafluoroethylene (TFE)** — note the **-ene** suffix, indicating a C=C double bond capable of polymerisation. Similarly, the monomer of PVDF is **vinylidene fluoride**, not vinyl fluoride.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 PU: Step-Growth Stoichiometry & Carother's Equation

For the PU-forming step-growth reaction between diisocyanate and polyol, Carother's equation governs the degree of polymerisation:

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}$$

where:
- $r = \dfrac{N_A}{N_B}$ = stoichiometric ratio of functional groups (≤ 1)
- $p$ = extent of reaction of the minor functional group

### 3.1.1 Worked Example — Stoichiometry Problem
A polyurethane is made from **1 mol of a diol (MW = 2000 g/mol)** and **2 mol of a diisocyanate (MW = 250 g/mol)**. Find the limiting functionality, the value of r, and the number-average degree of polymerisation $\overline{X}_n$ at 98 % conversion.

**Step 1 — Identify functional group counts.**
Diol: 2 OH groups per molecule × 1 mol = 2 mol OH groups
Diisocyanate: 2 NCO groups per molecule × 2 mol = 4 mol NCO groups

**Step 2 — Identify the limiting group.**
OH groups (2 mol) < NCO groups (4 mol) → OH is the limiting group.

**Step 3 — Compute r (ratio of minor to major).**
$$r = \frac{N_{\text{OH}}}{N_{\text{NCO}}} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5$$

**Step 4 — Apply Carother's equation at p = 0.98.**
$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1 + 0.5}{1 + 0.5 - 2(0.5)(0.98)} = \frac{1.5}{1.5 - 0.98} = \frac{1.5}{0.52}$$
$$\overline{X}_n \approx 2.88$$

This is a typical KTU numerical showing that even at high conversion, a stoichiometric imbalance (r ≠ 1) prevents high molecular weight. **For high MW PU, perfect 1:1 stoichiometry is essential.**

---

## 3.2 PTFE: Heat of Polymerisation Calculation

The polymerisation of TFE is highly exothermic; in industrial reactors, this is a major safety concern.

**Given:** ΔH_polymerisation of TFE = **–172 kJ/mol**

**Compute the heat released for 10 kg of PTFE.**

**Step 1 — Molar mass of –(CF₂–CF₂)– unit**
$$M_0 = 12 + 2(19) + 12 + 2(19) = 100\ \text{g/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Moles in 10 kg of PTFE**
$$n = \frac{10\,000\ \text{g}}{100\ \text{g/mol}} = 100\ \text{mol}$$

**Step 3 — Total heat released**
$$Q = n \times \Delta H = 100 \times (-172) = -17\,200\ \text{kJ} = -17.2\ \text{MJ}$$

This massive heat release per unit mass is why PTFE reactor runaway reactions have caused industrial accidents — a key point that KTU examiners often highlight.

---

## 3.3 PVDF: Piezoelectric Coupling

The direct piezoelectric effect in PVDF β-phase can be quantified by:
$$d_{33} = \frac{Q}{F}$$
where $Q$ is the charge developed (C) and $F$ is the applied force (N). A typical $\vert d_{33}\vert \approx 33$ pC/N.

**Worked Example:** A PVDF sensor of area 1 cm² is poled in the β-phase. An applied pressure of 100 kPa generates a charge density. Find the charge produced.

**Step 1 — Force per unit area is pressure**
$$P = 100\ \text{kPa} = 10^5\ \text{N/m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Use the piezoelectric equation in stress-charge form**
$$Q = d_{33} \times F = 33 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{C/N} \times (10^5\ \text{N/m}^2 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2)$$
$$Q = 33 \times 10^{-12} \times 10 = 3.3 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{C} = 0.33\ \text{nC}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Although the charge is small, it is easily measurable with high-impedance amplifiers — making PVDF a practical piezoelectric transducer.

---

## 3.4 HIPS: Impact Strength Derivation (Conceptual)

The relative impact strength of HIPS over PS is empirically given by:
$$\frac{I_{\text{HIPS}}}{I_{\text{PS}}} = 1 + k \cdot \phi_R \cdot f$$
where:
- $\phi_R$ = volume fraction of rubber
- $f$ = effectiveness factor of rubber (depends on particle size, grafting, etc.)
- $k$ = a constant (typical 9–11)

**For HIPS with 8 % rubber by volume, k = 10, f = 1:**
$$\frac{I_{\text{HIPS}}}{I_{\text{PS}}} = 1 + 10(0.08)(1) = 1.80$$
A 1.8× improvement is consistent with the experimental range of 2×–10× improvement seen in commercial HIPS.

---

## 3.5 POM: Thermal Stability and "Unzipping" Mechanism

Pure polyoxymethylene homopolymer is thermally unstable because the terminal hemiacetal group can undergo **"unzipping"** — a chain-depolymerisation that releases formaldehyde monomer step by step. The minimum bond-dissociation energy for this is roughly:
$$E_a \approx 90\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

The half-life of a POM chain at 200 °C, following first-order kinetics, is given by:
$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} = \frac{0.693}{A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}}$$

Using $A \approx 10^{13}\ \text{s}^{-1}$ (typical for first-order chain-end scission):
$$t_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{10^{13} \cdot e^{-90\,000/(8.314 \times 473)}}$$
$$t_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{10^{13} \cdot e^{-22.88}} = \frac{0.693}{10^{13} \cdot 1.27 \times 10^{-10}}$$
$$t_{1/2} \approx \frac{0.693}{1.27 \times 10^{3}} \approx 5.5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s}$$

This shows that pure POM would unzip almost instantly at 200 °C. By incorporating **1–3 mol % of 1,3-dioxolane**, the –(CH₂–CH₂–O)– units break the long –(CH₂–O)ₙ– sequence, and unzipping stops at the C–C bond, raising the usable service temperature to **>100 °C** continuously.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Criterion | PU | PTFE | PVF | PVDF | HIPS | POM |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Polymerisation Class | Step-growth | Chain-growth (radical) | Chain-growth (radical) | Chain-growth (radical) | Chain-growth (radical) with rubber | Chain-growth (ionic/ROP) |
| Functional Group Involved | –NCO + –OH | C=C | C=C | C=C | C=C | C=O (or trioxane ring) |
| By-product | None (CO₂ only in foaming) | None | None | None | None | None (or water in synthesis of HCHO) |
| Is High MW Achievable? | Only with 1:1 stoichiometry | Yes, but exotherm limits | Yes | Yes | Yes | Only with co-monomer |
| Specialty Property | Tunable hard/soft | Chemical inertness | Weatherability | Piezoelectric | Toughness | Dimensional stability |
| Recyclability | Poor (thermoset possible) | Difficult | Difficult | Possible | Yes (mechanical) | Limited |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between thermoplastic and thermosetting polyurethanes with examples of applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Thermoplastic PU (TPU) | Thermosetting PU |
|---|---|---|
| Cross-linking | Linear or lightly branched; melt-processable | Highly cross-linked 3D network; not melt-processable |
| Synthesis | Linear diisocyanate + diol, stoichiometric | Tri-functional isocyanate / triol → network |
| Behaviour on heating | Softens, can be remoulded | Decomposes; does not soften |
| Examples of applications | Spandex fibres, shoe soles, cable jacketing | Rigid insulation foam, RIM car bumpers, adhesives |

*(1 mark: TPU definition; 1 mark: thermoset PU definition; 1 mark: applications — any one example each.)*

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Why is PTFE considered chemically inert? Give two chemical applications that exploit this property.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The exceptional chemical inertness of PTFE arises from **(1)** the very high C–F bond strength (≈ 485 kJ/mol) and **(2)** the steric and electronic shielding of the carbon backbone by a tightly packed helical sheath of fluorine atoms, leaving no reactive site accessible. The high electronegativity of fluorine reduces the polarisability of the C–F bond, making it resistant to almost all solvents, acids, and bases below 300 °C.

**Applications exploiting this inertness:** (any two — ½ mark each)
- Gaskets, valve seats, and lined pipes in chemical processing plants handling HF, HCl, H₂SO₄
- Laboratory ware (beakers, stirrer bars) for use with aggressive reagents
- Coatings for vessels storing ultra-pure chemicals in semiconductor industry

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**(a) Describe the monomer preparation, polymerisation mechanism, and structure–property relationships of polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE). (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Monomer preparation — Tetrafluoroethylene (TFE), CF₂=CF₂ (3 marks)**

The industrial synthesis of TFE proceeds in two stages from chloroform:

**Stage 1 — Fluorination of chloroform to R-12 (dichlorodifluoromethane):**
$$\text{CHCl}_3 + 2\ \text{HF} \xrightarrow{\text{SbF}_5,\ 30\text{–}60\ ^\circ\text{C}} \text{CHClF}_2 + 2\ \text{HCl}$$

**Stage 2 — Pyrolysis of R-12 to TFE:**
$$2\ \text{CHClF}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{600–900}\ ^\circ\text{C, vacuum}} \text{CF}_2{=}\text{CF}_2 + 2\ \text{HCl}$$

TFE is a colourless, odourless gas (bp = –76 °C). It is highly **explosive** above 25 °C at atmospheric pressure and must be stored under pressure and in the presence of a terpene inhibitor.

**(ii) Polymerisation mechanism (2 marks)**

TFE is polymerised by **free-radical mechanism** in aqueous medium (suspension or emulsion) under high pressure (10–100 atm) at 40–80 °C. Initiators: persulphates, peroxides, or redox systems. The polymerisation is **highly exothermic** (ΔH ≈ –172 kJ/mol), demanding strict temperature control. The polymer is obtained as a fine white powder or aqueous dispersion.

**(iii) Structure–property relationships (2 marks)**
- The very high C–F bond energy (≈ 485 kJ/mol) makes PTFE chemically inert and thermally stable (continuous use up to 260 °C).
- The helical wrapping of fluorine atoms around the carbon backbone creates a low surface energy (≈ 18 mN/m) and an extremely low coefficient of friction (0.05–0.10).
- The chain is highly rigid due to the bulky fluorine atoms → high melting point (327 °C) and high crystallinity (50–70 %).
- Absence of branching and hydrogen bonding makes PTFE an excellent electrical insulator, especially at high frequencies.

---

**(b) Discuss the monomer synthesis, polymerisation, and special properties (including piezoelectricity) of polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF). (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Monomer preparation — Vinylidene Fluoride (VDF), CH₂=CF₂ (2 marks)**

VDF is industrially produced by **dehydrochlorination of HCFC-142b (1-chloro-1,1-difluoroethane):**
$$\text{CH}_3\text{–CClF}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{600–900}\ ^\circ\text{C, vacuum}} \text{CH}_2{=}\text{CF}_2 + \text{HCl}$$
HCFC-142b itself is prepared by:
$$\text{CH}_3\text{–CCl}_3 + 2\ \text{HF} \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{–CClF}_2 + 2\ \text{HCl}$$
VDF is a gas at room temperature (bp = –84 °C).

**(ii) Polymerisation (2 marks)**

VDF is polymerised by **free-radical polymerisation** in suspension, emulsion, or bulk. Operating conditions: 10–130 atm, 30–150 °C, with peroxide or azo initiators. The resulting PVDF is a highly crystalline polymer (up to 70 %) with melting point 171–180 °C.

**(iii) Crystal phases and piezoelectricity (3 marks)**

PVDF exhibits **four known crystal forms** — α, β, γ, and δ:
- **α-phase (Form II):** TGTḠ conformation; non-polar; most common as crystallised from melt.
- **β-phase (Form I):** All-trans planar zigzag; strongly polar and **piezoelectric** and **pyroelectric**.
- **γ-phase (Form III):** TTTGTTTḠ ; intermediate polarity.
- **δ-phase (Form IV):** Polar version of α-phase.

To obtain the piezoelectric β-phase, the α-phase film is **mechanically stretched** (drawing) and then **electrically poled** under a high DC field at elevated temperature to align the molecular dipoles. The piezoelectric coefficient d₃₃ ≈ –33 pC/N. Applications: ultrasonic transducers, pressure sensors, actuators, energy-harvesting devices, and loudspeaker tweeters.

---

### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**(a) Explain the synthesis of polyacetal (POM) via the formaldehyde and trioxane routes, including the mechanism of thermal stabilisation by co-monomers. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Monomer preparation (2 marks)**

**Formaldehyde (HCHO) route:**
Methanol is oxidised using a silver or iron-molybdenum catalyst:
$$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \tfrac{1}{2}\ \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Ag, 600–700}\ ^\circ\text{C}} \text{HCHO} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Trioxane route:**
Formaldehyde is trimerised in the presence of an acid catalyst (e.g., H₂SO₄):
$$3\ \text{HCHO} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} (\text{CH}_2\text{O})_3 \quad \text{(1,3,5-trioxane)}$$

**(ii) Polymerisation (3 marks)**

- **Formaldehyde route:** Pure formaldehyde is polymerised **anionically** (e.g., using tertiary amines, phosphines, or organometallic initiators) at low temperatures. The product is the **homo-polymer** –(CH₂–O)ₙ– (e.g., Delrin). To prevent unzipping, the unstable hemiacetal end groups are end-capped by acetylation with acetic anhydride.

- **Trioxane route:** 1,3,5-Trioxane is **ring-opening polymerised cationically** (BF₃·OEt₂, HClO₄, etc.) in bulk or solution. A small amount of **1,3-dioxolane (1–3 mol %)** is added as a co-monomer. The –CH₂–CH₂–O– units introduced interrupt the –CH₂–O– sequence. The product is the **co-polymer** (e.g., Hostaform, Celcon).

**(iii) Thermal stabilisation mechanism (2 marks)**

Pure homo-POM has terminal –O–CH₂–OH (hemiacetal) groups. At elevated temperatures, these can unzZip back to HCHO by a chain-end-initiated chain scission. The activation energy for unzipping is ≈ 90 kJ/mol, leading to a half-life of milliseconds at 200 °C. Two stabilisation strategies are used:
1. **End-capping** (homo-polymer) — acetylation of terminal –OH groups with acetic anhydride to form stable –O–COCH₃ ester ends.
2. **Co-monomer incorporation** (co-polymer) — the randomly placed –CH₂–CH₂–O– units introduce C–C bonds. When an "unzip" reaches a C–C bond, propagation stops because C–C bonds do not readily break.

Both strategies raise the continuous service temperature to >100 °C.

---

**(b) Write notes on: monomer preparation, polymerisation, properties, and applications of high impact polystyrene (HIPS). (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Composition and monomer preparation (2 marks)**
HIPS is a polymer blend of:
- **Polystyrene (PS) matrix:** 85–95 % — from styrene monomer (C₆H₅–CH=CH₂)
- **Polybutadiene (PBD) rubber:** 5–15 % — dispersed as 1–10 µm particles

Styrene is produced from ethylbenzene by catalytic dehydrogenation. Polybutadiene is produced by **anionic or Ziegler–Natta polymerisation** of 1,3-butadiene.

**(ii) Polymerisation (3 marks)**

The two-stage process:
1. **Stage 1 (Pre-polymerisation, bulk/solution):** PBD rubber (5–15 %) is dissolved in styrene monomer, and a free-radical initiator (peroxide) is added. Polymerisation is conducted at 80–120 °C. As PS chains form and grow, the system undergoes **phase separation**. Growing PS chains **graft** onto PBD backbones via chain transfer, producing an in-situ **graft copolymer (PS-g-PBD)** that anchors at the rubber–PS interface as a compatibiliser.
2. **Stage 2 (Suspension finish):** Conversion is completed in suspension to control heat removal and final particle morphology.

**(iii) Properties and applications (2 marks)**

**Properties:** HIPS has 2–10× higher impact strength than PS, while retaining the easy processability of PS. It is opaque (due to two-phase morphology), has a density of 1.04–1.06 g/cm³, and good mouldability. Tensile strength ≈ 15–40 MPa.

**Applications:** refrigerator door liners, TV and computer housings, yoghurt cups, toys, automotive interior trims, and disposable food packaging.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Monomer of PTFE:** Writing "tetrafluoroethane" instead of **tetrafluoroethylene**. The –ene suffix is critical.
> 2. **Polyurethane mechanism:** Classifying PU formation as a "condensation polymerisation." It is **step-growth polyaddition** — no small molecule is released during chain formation. (½ to 1 mark penalty.)
> 3. **POM thermal stability:** Forgetting to mention the **end-capping or co-monomer strategy**. Simply saying "POM is crystalline" is incomplete — examiners expect the unzipping-vs-stabilisation discussion.
> 4. **PVDF phases:** Writing only "α and β" without explaining the **drawing + poling** route to obtain the piezoelectric β-phase.
> 5. **HIPS morphology:** Forgetting the role of the **graft copolymer** at the rubber–PS interface — it is the key to stability of the two-phase morphology.
> 6. **Stoichiometry in PU:** Not applying Carother's equation or the 1:1 stoichiometry requirement in numericals.
> 7. **Reactor safety in TFE:** Skipping the mention that TFE is **explosive and must be inhibited** — a frequent KTU favourite.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PU:** Step-growth polyaddition of diisocyanate + polyol. No small molecule released. Hard/soft segment microphase separation gives tunable properties. Foam requires water as a chemical blowing agent.
- **PTFE:** Monomer TFE (CF₂=CF₂) from chloroform via R-12 pyrolysis. Free-radical polymerisation under high pressure. Strong C–F bond (485 kJ/mol) → chemical inertness, low µ, high melting point. Cannot be melt-processed (degrades); processed by sintering or paste extrusion.
- **PVF:** Monomer VF from acetylene + HF (HgCl₂ catalyst). Free-radical polymerisation. Tedlar film — exceptional UV and weather resistance.
- **PVDF:** Monomer VDF from HCFC-142b. Four crystal phases (α, β, γ, δ); β-phase drawn + poled → piezoelectric. Excellent chemical and flame resistance.
- **HIPS:** PS + 5–15 % PBD rubber; PS-g-PBD graft copolymer at interface. Two-phase morphology gives 2–10× impact improvement via crazing.
- **POM (Polyacetal):** Monomer HCHO or trioxane. Anionic or cationic ROP. Thermal stabilisation is essential — end-capping (homo) or 1,3-dioxolane co-monomer (co-polymer).
- **Key Comparison Points for KTU:**
  - PU = polyaddition; all other five = chain-growth
  - PTFE has the highest density (≈ 2.15 g/cm³) of all commodity thermoplastics
  - PVDF is the only thermoplastic in this list with commercial piezoelectric use
  - HIPS is the only two-phase system in this list
  - POM is the only polymer in this list that requires thermal stabilisation as a built-in design step
- **Safety Note for TFE:** Highly explosive — must be handled with terpene inhibitors and under pressure.
- **Industrial Production Routes to Remember:**
  - TFE ← CHCl₃ + HF → CHClF₂ → Δ → CF₂=CF₂
  - VDF ← CH₃–CCl₃ + HF → CH₃–CClF₂ → Δ → CH₂=CF₂
  - VF ← CH≡CH + HF → CH₂=CHF
  - HCHO ← CH₃OH + ½ O₂ (Ag or Fe-Mo catalyst)
  - PU ← OCN–R–NCO + HO–R'–OH (no byproduct in chain)

---

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Thermoplastic Polyesters — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermoplastic Polyesters** are a family of high-performance engineering plastics whose polymer backbone contains repeating **ester linkages (–COO–)** derived from the condensation polymerization of a **dicarboxylic acid (or its dimethyl ester)** with a **diol (glycol)**. Because the resulting chains are **linear, saturated, and free of reactive unsaturation**, they can be repeatedly melted and re-solidified without undergoing any irreversible chemical change — hence the prefix *thermoplastic*.

The most industrially significant members of this family studied under the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCPOT403) are:

| Abbreviation | Full Chemical Name | Key Commercial Application |
|---|---|---|
| **PET** | Poly(Ethylene Terephthalate) | Beverage bottles, fibres, BOPET films |
| **PBT** | Poly(Butylene Terephthalate) | Automotive, electrical connectors |
| **PEN** | Poly(Ethylene Naphthalate) | High-barrier films, advanced fibres |
| **PTT** | Poly(Trimethylene Terephthalate) | Carpet fibres, textiles |
| **PCT** | Poly(Cyclohexylene Dimethylene Terephthalate) | High-heat electronic components |

> [!NOTE]
> **General Repeating Unit:** A thermoplastic polyester can be represented as `[–O–R₁–O–CO–R₂–CO–]ₙ`, where **R₁** is the aliphatic/aromatic diol residue and **R₂** is the aromatic diacid residue.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Velcro-Bead Necklace" View

Imagine a **string of beads** where each "bead" is a *terephthalate* (a flat, rigid aromatic ring) and between the beads are *short flexible elastic cords* (the diol-derived ethylene/butylene spacers).

- The **rigid aromatic rings** give the polymer its **strength, stiffness, and high melting point** (like the wooden beads in a stiff necklace).
- The **flexible aliphatic cords** allow the chain to **bend, stretch, and crystallise** into ordered bundles (like the elastic thread that lets the necklace drape).
- A longer cord (e.g., 4 carbons in PBT vs. 2 carbons in PET) makes the necklace **more flexible and faster to crystallise**, but slightly less rigid.

> This mental picture immediately tells a student *why* PET and PBT are stiff, *why* PBT crystallises faster than PET, and *why* replacing the benzene ring with a naphthalene ring (PEN) makes the material dramatically stiffer and more thermally stable.

## 1.3 Unsaturated Polyester Laminating Resins (UPR) — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An **Unsaturated Polyester Resin (UPR)** is a **thermosetting** linear polyester containing **reactive C=C double bonds** along the polymer chain, dissolved in a **reactive vinyl monomer (typically styrene, 30–45 % by mass)**. Upon addition of a **peroxide initiator**, the C=C bonds of the polyester and of styrene co-react to form a **rigid, three-dimensional crosslinked network** that cannot be re-melted.

The term *laminating resin* specifically refers to its use with **fibre reinforcements** (glass, carbon, aramid) to produce **Fibre-Reinforced Plastics (FRP/GRP)** in processes like hand lay-up, spray-up, and filament winding.

## 1.4 Polyester Moulding Compositions (DMC/SMC/BMC) — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polyester Moulding Compositions** are ready-to-mould, fibre-reinforced thermoset compounds in which an **unsaturated polyester resin paste** (resin + filler + additives) is combined with **chopped glass fibres (typically 6–25 mm)** and a **thickener** that provides a dough- or sheet-like handling consistency. They are compression- or injection-moulded into finished parts in a single operation.

The three principal forms are:

- **DMC / BMC** (Dough / Bulk Moulding Compound) — a putty-like bulk mass.
- **SMC** (Sheet Moulding Compound) — a continuous sheet sandwiched between carrier films.
- **ZMC / TMC / XMC** — variations for injection or high-performance compression moulding.

## 1.5 Fibre and Film Forming Polyesters — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These are thermoplastic polyesters (predominantly **PET**, with smaller volumes of **PTT** and **PEN**) that are processed via **melt spinning** (for fibres) or **melt extrusion followed by biaxial orientation** (for films). The ability of the linear polyester chains to be **highly oriented, rapidly crystallised, and drawn into very high aspect-ratio films or fine-denier filaments** qualifies them as *fibre- and film-forming* polymers.

> [!NOTE]
> The same PET grade that ends up as a soft-drink bottle began life as a *film-forming* polymer; the same chemistry that gives a wrinkle-free shirt is the chemistry of a *fibre-forming* polymer. Only the processing route changes.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Chemistry of Thermoplastic Polyesters — The Step-Growth Condensation

### 2.1.1 The Two Industrial Routes

**Route A — Ester-Interchange (Trans-esterification) using Dimethyl Terephthalate (DMT)**

$$\text{CH}_3\text{OOC–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–COOCH}_3 + \text{HO–R–OH} \xrightarrow{\text{Mn, Zn, Ti catalyst} \atop \Delta} \text{[–OOC–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–COO–R–]}_n + 2\text{n CH}_3\text{OH}$$

**Route B — Direct Esterification using Terephthalic Acid (TPA)** — dominant today.

$$\text{HOOC–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–COOH} + \text{HO–R–OH} \xrightarrow{\text{Sb}_2\text{O}_3 / \text{Ti(OBu)}_4 \atop \Delta,\ \text{vacuum}} \text{[–OOC–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–COO–R–]}_n + 2\text{n H}_2\text{O}$$

For specific polyesters:

- **PET** — R = –CH₂–CH₂– (ethylene glycol)
- **PBT** — R = –(CH₂)₄– (1,4-butanediol)
- **PTT** — R = –(CH₂)₃– (1,3-propanediol)
- **PEN** — acid is **naphthalene-2,6-dicarboxylic acid**
- **PCT** — R = cyclohexanedimethanol

### 2.1.2 Why the Diacid is Almost Always Aromatic

The aromatic ring provides:
1. **Chain stiffness** → high Tg and high modulus.
2. **Conjugation with the ester carbonyl** → better thermal-oxidative stability.
3. **Close chain packing** → crystallisability, hence solvent resistance.

### 2.1.3 The Carothers Equation — Predicting Molecular Weight

For a step-growth polymerisation with stoichiometric imbalance ratio $r = N_A / N_B \le 1$ (where $N_A$ is moles of diacid and $N_B$ is moles of glycol):

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}$$

where $p$ is the extent of reaction and $\overline{X}_n$ is the number-average degree of polymerisation.

> At **exact stoichiometry (r = 1)** this reduces to $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$. To obtain **$\overline{X}_n \ge 100$** (a useful engineering polymer), **p must exceed 0.99** — a key reason why polycondensation reactors must operate under **high vacuum** to strip the methanol/water by-product and drive equilibrium forward.

The number-average molecular weight follows:

$$\overline{M}_n = M_0 \cdot \overline{X}_n$$

where $M_0$ is the molar mass of the repeat unit (e.g., **192 g/mol** for PET).

## 2.2 Thermal Transitions and Crystallinity

### 2.2.1 The Three Critical Temperatures

| Transition | Physical Meaning | Approx. PET | Approx. PBT |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tg** (glass transition) | Onset of segmental motion | **67–81 °C** | **22–40 °C** |
| **Tc** (peak crystallisation) | Maximum rate of crystallisation on cooling | ~140 °C | ~70 °C |
| **Tm** (melting point) | Disappearance of last crystallite | **250–265 °C** | **220–225 °C** |

### 2.2.2 Why PBT Crystallises Faster than PET

PBT contains a **4-carbon flexible spacer** that allows the chains to fold and pack into the crystallographic lattice much more rapidly. PET's **2-carbon spacer** forces a near-linear chain that packs sluggishly, hence PET is often delivered as an **amorphous** preform that is blow-moulded with strain-induced crystallisation (the bottle-making trick).

### 2.2.3 The Equilibrium Theory of Crystallisation

The crystalline fraction $X_c$ may be estimated from density:

$$X_c = \dfrac{\rho_c \left( \rho - \rho_a \right)}{\rho \left( \rho_c - \rho_a \right)}$$

where $\rho$ = measured sample density, $\rho_a$ = amorphous density, $\rho_c$ = fully crystalline density (PET: **$\rho_a \approx 1.335$ g/cm³**, **$\rho_c \approx 1.455$ g/cm³**).

## 2.3 Unsaturated Polyester Resin — Deep Theory

### 2.3.1 Typical Synthesis

$$\text{Maleic Anhydride} + \text{Phthalic Anhydride} + \text{Propylene Glycol} \xrightarrow{\Delta,\ -\text{H}_2\text{O}} \text{Unsaturated Polyester (solid)}$$

The polyester is then **cooled and blended with 30–45 % styrene** to give the pourable, viscous resin sold commercially.

### 2.3.2 Roles of Each Component

- **Maleic Anhydride (MA)** — supplies the **reactive C=C** for crosslinking (reacts with styrene).
- **Phthalic Anhydride (PA) / Isophthalic Acid (IPA)** — supplies **aromatic rigidity** but no unsaturation; reduces brittleness, tunes flexibility, lowers cost.
- **Propylene Glycol** — provides chain flexibility; neopentyl glycol or diethylene glycol are alternatives.
- **Styrene Monomer** — the **reactive diluent and co-monomer** that bridges two polyester chains via co-polymerisation.

### 2.3.3 The Crosslinking Reaction

$$\text{—C=C— (on polyester)} + \text{CH}_2{=}\text{CH–Ph (styrene)} \xrightarrow{\text{MEKP/BPO} \atop \text{Co-naphthenate accelerator}} \text{3-D Network}$$

The initiator **methyl ethyl ketone peroxide (MEKP)** decomposes into free radicals; cobalt naphthenate accelerates the decomposition at room temperature for "cold-cure" laminating work.

### 2.3.4 Key Properties of Cured UPR

| Property | Typical Range |
|---|---|
| Tensile strength | **40–80 MPa** |
| Tensile modulus | **3–4.5 GPa** |
| Heat-deflection temperature | **80–120 °C** |
| Barcol hardness | **35–50** |
| Volume shrinkage on cure | **5–9 %** |

> The **5–9 % shrinkage** is a defining drawback of UPR — it causes surface waviness ("sink marks") in moulded parts and internal stresses in thick laminates.

## 2.4 Polyester Moulding Compositions — Deep Theory

### 2.4.1 The Five-Functional Recipe

A typical SMC/BMC formulation contains:

| Ingredient | Function | Typical % by mass |
|---|---|---|
| UPR resin (in styrene) | Polymer matrix | **25–35** |
| Filler (CaCO₃, ATH) | Cost reduction, flame retardancy, dimensional stability | **40–55** |
| Chopped glass fibre (6–25 mm) | Mechanical reinforcement | **15–30** |
| Thickener (MgO / Mg(OH)₂ / Ca(OH)₂) | Converts liquid resin paste into mouldable dough | **1–3** |
| Release agent (Zn stearate) | Ejection of part | **0.5–2** |
| Pigment, inhibitor, peroxide | Colour, shelf-life, cure | balance |

### 2.4.2 The Thickening Mechanism

When **MgO** is added to the polyester paste, it reacts with the terminal carboxylic acid groups and forms a **Mg-carboxylate ionic cluster**, which dramatically increases viscosity over a 24–72 h maturation period. The viscosity typically rises from **~20 Pa·s** to **~1500 Pa·s** (a ~75-fold increase), turning the liquid into a handleable dough or sheet.

### 2.4.3 The Hot-Plate Moulding Cycle

1. Charge SMC sheet into a **compression mould** heated to **140–160 °C**.
2. Close mould under **500–1000 kN force** for **1–5 minutes**.
3. Peroxide decomposes → free radicals → crosslinking occurs.
4. Open mould, eject part, trim flash.

## 2.5 Fibre- and Film-Forming Polyesters — Deep Theory

### 2.5.1 Fibre Spinning — The Two Essentials

For a polymer to be spinnable it must:
1. Pass through a melt or solution state that **cools/solidifies fast enough** to form a continuous filament.
2. Be **drawable** — the solidified filament must stretch several-fold at a temperature above Tg without breaking, allowing the chains to align along the fibre axis.

PET satisfies both: its melt viscosity at 280 °C is manageable, and its Tg (~75 °C) allows drawing at 90–100 °C to draw ratios of **4:1 to 6:1**.

### 2.5.2 Birefringence — The Signature of Orientation

$$\Delta n = n_{\parallel} - n_{\perp}$$

For fully oriented PET fibre, $\Delta n \approx 0.20$. This optical anisotropy is also the basis of polarised-light identification of polyester fibres.

### 2.5.3 BOPET Film Production

BOPET (Biaxially Oriented PET) film is produced by **simultaneous or sequential stretching** of an extruded amorphous PET sheet in **machine direction (MD)** and **transverse direction (TD)** at temperatures just above Tg, followed by heat-setting.

| Property | Unoriented PET | BOPET Film |
|---|---|---|
| Tensile strength | ~55 MPa | **190–260 MPa** |
| Elongation at break | ~300 % | **60–120 %** |
| Shrinkage at 150 °C | large | **< 1.5 %** |
| Oxygen permeability | high | very low |
| Use | limited | food packaging, electrical insulation, magnetic tape, solar-control films |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat-Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Carothers number-average DP | $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$ | r = $N_A/N_B$ ≤ 1; p = extent of reaction |
| 2 | Carothers — equal stoichiometry | $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | valid when r = 1 |
| 3 | Number-average molecular weight | $\overline{M}_n = M_0 \cdot \overline{X}_n$ | $M_0$ = molar mass of repeat unit (g/mol) |
| 4 | Crystalline fraction (density) | $X_c = \dfrac{\rho_c(\rho-\rho_a)}{\rho(\rho_c-\rho_a)}$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Glass transition rule of thumb | $T_g \approx \dfrac{2}{3} T_m$ (in Kelvin, when crystalline) | empirical, Fox's rule |
| 6 | Degree of orientation (sonic) | $f = 1 - \left( \dfrac{C_{u}}{C} \right)^2$ | $C_u$ = sonic modulus of unoriented sample |
| 7 | Curing shrinkage | $S = \dfrac{V_{\text{before}} - V_{\text{after}}}{V_{\text{before}}} \times 100$ | typically 5–9 % for UPR |
| 8 | Tensile modulus — fibre | $E_f \approx (1 + 2 X_c) E_{\text{matrix}}$ | rule-of-mixtures, low-fibre composite |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PET bottles** combine blow-moulding (orientation) with strain-induced crystallisation to give clarity + barrier + toughness simultaneously.
- **PBT connectors** in cars exploit low moisture absorption (so electrical properties are stable in humid climates) and fast cycle times (fast crystallisation = shorter moulding cycle).
- **SMC car body panels** (e.g., Chevrolet Corvette) demonstrate the largest automotive application of UPR — a Class-A finish is achievable with low-shrink/low-profile additives.
- **BOPET films** are the silent backbone of the packaging industry — millions of tonnes/year for metallised snack wrappers, solar-control window films, and electrical insulation in motors.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Carothers Equation on PET Synthesis

**Problem.** PET is synthesised by direct esterification of pure terephthalic acid (TPA) with ethylene glycol (EG). If the reaction is carried out with a 0.5 % molar excess of EG (i.e., the diacid is the limiting reagent) and the reaction is stopped at p = 0.995, compute $\overline{X}_n$ and $\overline{M}_n$.

### Step 1 — Identify the stoichiometric imbalance ratio r

Let moles of TPA = $N_A = 1$ mol (reference).
Excess EG = 0.5 %, so $N_B = 1.005$ mol.

$$r = \frac{N_A}{N_B} = \frac{1}{1.005} \approx 0.99502$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Carothers generalised expression

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}$$

Substituting r = 0.99502 and p = 0.995:

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1 + 0.99502}{1 + 0.99502 - 2 \times 0.99502 \times 0.995}$$

Compute the numerator:
$$1 + 0.99502 = 1.99502$$

Compute the denominator:
$$1 + 0.99502 - 2 \times 0.99502 \times 0.995$$
$$= 1.99502 - 2 \times 0.99005$$
$$= 1.99502 - 1.98010$$
$$= 0.01492$$

Therefore:
$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1.99502}{0.01492} \approx 133.7$$

### Step 3 — Compute $\overline{M}_n$

The repeat unit of PET is `–O–CH₂–CH₂–O–CO–C₆H₄–CO–` with molar mass:
$$M_0 = 4(12) + 4(1) + 2(16) + 2(12) + 4(1) + 2(16) + 6(12) + 4(1)$$
$$= 48 + 4 + 32 + 24 + 4 + 32 + 72 + 4 = 192 \text{ g/mol}$$

Therefore:
$$\overline{M}_n = M_0 \times \overline{X}_n = 192 \times 133.7 \approx 25{,}670 \text{ g/mol}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Even a tiny stoichiometric imbalance (0.5 % excess EG) caps $\overline{X}_n$ well below the ideal value of $\dfrac{1}{1-0.995} = 200$. This shows why **industrial PET reactors must precisely control feed ratio** and why the methanol/water must be removed to drive p → 1.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Crystalline Fraction from Density

**Problem.** A PET bottle-wall sample has measured density $\rho = 1.40 \text{ g/cm}^3$. With $\rho_a = 1.335 \text{ g/cm}^3$ and $\rho_c = 1.455 \text{ g/cm}^3$, compute the crystalline fraction $X_c$.

### Step 1 — Substitute into the formula

$$X_c = \frac{\rho_c \left( \rho - \rho_a \right)}{\rho \left( \rho_c - \rho_a \right)}$$

### Step 2 — Evaluate numerator and denominator separately

Numerator:
$$\rho_c \times (\rho - \rho_a) = 1.455 \times (1.40 - 1.335) = 1.455 \times 0.065 = 0.09458$$

Denominator:
$$\rho \times (\rho_c - \rho_a) = 1.40 \times (1.455 - 1.335) = 1.40 \times 0.120 = 0.1680$$

### Step 3 — Divide

$$X_c = \frac{0.09458}{0.1680} \approx 0.563 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 56.3\,\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> A PET bottle typically has $X_c \approx 25\text{–}35\,\%$ (translucent amorphous regions), but **oriented, heat-set BOPET film** reaches $X_c \approx 40\text{–}55\,\%$. The 56 % value here indicates a highly oriented, crystallised sample.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — UPR Cure Shrinkage and Resulting Sink-Mark Depth

**Problem.** A flat SMC moulding of dimensions $300 \times 300 \times 3 \text{ mm}$ cures with volumetric shrinkage $S = 7\,\%$. Estimate the linear shrinkage and the resulting sink-mark depth if surface tension pulls the top surface inward by 70 % of the linear shrinkage.

### Step 1 — Convert volumetric to linear shrinkage

For small isotropic shrinkage:
$$S_{\text{linear}} \approx 1 - (1 - S_{\text{vol}})^{1/3}$$

$$S_{\text{linear}} = 1 - (1 - 0.07)^{1/3} = 1 - (0.93)^{1/3}$$

$$(0.93)^{1/3} = e^{\frac{1}{3} \ln 0.93} = e^{\frac{1}{3} \times (-0.07257)} = e^{-0.02419} \approx 0.9761$$

Therefore:
$$S_{\text{linear}} \approx 1 - 0.9761 = 0.0239 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 2.39\,\%$$

### Step 2 — Compute linear dimensional change

$$\Delta L = 0.0239 \times 3 \text{ mm} = 0.0717 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 3 — Compute sink-mark depth

$$h_{\text{sink}} = 0.70 \times 0.0717 = 0.050 \text{ mm} \approx 50\ \mu\text{m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> 50 µm sink marks are visible to the eye and unacceptable for Class-A automotive panels. This is why SMC formulations include **low-profile / low-shrink additives (LPAs)** — thermoplastic powders (PVAc, PE) that compensate for shrinkage.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Birefringence of an Oriented PET Fibre

**Problem.** An oriented PET fibre shows $n_{\parallel} = 1.685$ and $n_{\perp} = 1.485$ (Na-D line). Calculate the birefringence $\Delta n$ and comment on its significance.

### Step 1 — Substitute

$$\Delta n = n_{\parallel} - n_{\perp} = 1.685 - 1.485 = 0.200$$

### Step 2 — Interpretation

A $\Delta n \approx 0.20$ indicates **highly aligned, nearly fully oriented** PET fibre (typical textile-grade). Compare with:

- Unoriented PET: $\Delta n \approx 0.005$
- Nylon 6 fully drawn: $\Delta n \approx 0.060$
- Cotton (natural cellulose II): $\Delta n \approx 0.040$

> Hence a simple polarised-light test is a forensic fingerprint of fibre origin.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix — PET vs PBT vs PEN

| Property | PET | PBT | PEN |
|---|---|---|---|
| Repeat unit M₀ (g/mol) | **192** | **220** | **242** |
| Tg (°C) | **67–81** | **22–40** | **113–125** |
| Tm (°C) | **250–265** | **220–225** | **265–270** |
| Crystallisation half-time at 130 °C (s) | **very long (>>1000)** | **fast (~10)** | moderate |
| Tensile strength (MPa) | 55–75 | 50–60 | 80–90 |
| Tensile modulus (GPa) | 2.8–3.1 | 2.3–2.6 | 4.5–5.5 |
| Moisture absorption (24 h, %) | **0.1** | **0.08** | **< 0.05** |
| O₂ barrier vs PET | 1× | 0.6× | **3–5×** |
| Cost (relative) | 1× | 1.8× | 5–8× |

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting a Polyester for an Application

| Application | Best Choice | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Carbonated soft-drink bottle | **PET** | Clarity, barrier, cheap, blow-mouldable |
| Automotive electrical connector | **PBT** | Fast crystallisation (short cycle), low moisture, dimensional stability |
| Hot-fill bottle (> 90 °C) | **PEN** or heat-set PET | High Tg avoids distortion |
| Marine FRP hull | **UPR + glass mat** | Low cost, room-temperature cure, corrosion resistance |
| SMC car body panel | **SMC (UP+CaCO₃+glass+LPA)** | Class-A surface, high volume, low cycle time |
| High-tenacity tyre cord | **PET** (or Nylon 6,6) | High $E$, low creep, fatigue resistance |
| BOPET for flexible electronics | **PEN** | Higher Tg, lower shrinkage |
| Carpet fibre | **PTT** | Resilience, dyeability, bio-PDO route |
