# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Sem B.Tech PT]`

> **Differentiate between thermoplastic polyesters and thermosetting unsaturated polyester resins with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Thermoplastic Polyester (e.g., PET) | Thermosetting Unsaturated Polyester (e.g., Ortho-resin) |
|---|---|---|
| Bond type | Linear, saturated chains, **no C=C in backbone** | Linear chain with **pendant C=C**, crosslinked by styrene |
| Behaviour on heating | Softens, melts, can be reprocessed | Does **not melt**; decomposes on strong heating |
| Solubility | Soluble in selected solvents at high T | Insoluble, infusible after cure |
| Synthesis | Condensation of diacid + diol, no monomer diluent | Condensation of saturated + unsaturated anhydrides, blended with styrene |
| Typical application | Bottles, fibres, films | FRP laminates, SMC parts |

*(3 marks — 1 for bond type, 1 for thermal behaviour, 1 for application)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Sem B.Tech PT]`

> **Why is magnesium oxide added to SMC formulations? What happens if it is omitted?**

**Model Answer:**

MgO (or Mg(OH)₂) acts as a **thickener** — it reacts with the terminal –COOH groups of the polyester, forming magnesium-carboxylate ionic clusters that dramatically increase the viscosity of the resin paste over 24–72 h. This converts the liquid paste into a handleable, dough-like sheet that:
1. Carries the chopped glass fibres without dripping.
2. Does not flow out of the mould before it closes.

If MgO is **omitted**, the SMC remains a low-viscosity liquid; the glass fibres separate, the resin flows out of the mould, the laminate loses its reinforcement and dimensional accuracy, and the sheet cannot even be handled by SMC machinery. *(3 marks — 2 for mechanism, 1 for consequence of omission)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the synthesis, structure and key properties of **PET**. Discuss in detail why PET is the dominant material for carbonated beverage bottles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the structure-property relationships of **PET, PBT and PEN**. Explain why PBT crystallises faster than PET. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — A(a)

**Synthesis of PET** (3 marks)
PET is synthesised industrially by **direct esterification** of pure terephthalic acid (TPA) with ethylene glycol (EG), using antimony trioxide or titanium tetrabutoxide catalyst. The reaction proceeds in two stages:

1. **Esterification** at 240–260 °C under 3–5 bar pressure:
$$\text{HOOC–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–COOH} + \text{HO–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–OH} \rightarrow \text{Bis(2-hydroxyethyl) terephthalate} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

2. **Polycondensation** at 270–285 °C under high vacuum (< 1 mbar):
$$\text{BHET} \rightarrow \text{[–OCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OOC–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CO]}_n + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The Carothers relationship controls the achievable $\overline{M}_n$; the vacuum drives p → 1.

**Structure and Properties of PET** (2 marks)
PET has a **semi-crystalline** structure with:
- **Tg ≈ 75 °C**, **Tm ≈ 255 °C**.
- Aromatic rings along the backbone impart **stiffness, high tensile strength (~70 MPa), high modulus (~3 GPa), and low creep**.
- Low moisture absorption (~0.1 %) → **good electrical insulation** and **dimensional stability**.
- Good chemical resistance to dilute acids, alcohols, oils; poor resistance to strong bases and phenols.

**Why PET dominates beverage bottles** (2 marks)
1. **Optical clarity** — amorphous regions transmit light; strain-induced crystallisation during blow moulding keeps haze low.
2. **Good CO₂ and O₂ barrier** — especially after orientation (bottles have $X_c$ ≈ 25–30 %); adequate for shelf-life of carbonated drinks.
3. **Toughness** — biaxial orientation during blow moulding gives **high impact strength** that resists drop-impact in commerce.
4. **Lightweight, recyclable** (Resin Code 1), cheap, FDA-approved for food contact.
5. **Processability** — low melt viscosity at 280 °C allows fast injection stretch-blow moulding cycles.

---

#### Model Solution — A(b)

**Structure-Property Comparison Table** (4 marks)

| Property | PET | PBT | PEN |
|---|---|---|---|
| Diol carbon count | 2 (EG) | 4 (BDO) | 2 (EG) |
| Aromatic acid | Terephthalic | Terephthalic | 2,6-Naphthalic |
| Tg | 67–81 °C | 22–40 °C | 113–125 °C |
| Tm | 250–265 °C | 220–225 °C | 265–270 °C |
| Modulus | 2.8–3.1 GPa | 2.3–2.6 GPa | 4.5–5.5 GPa |
| Moisture absorption | 0.1 % | 0.08 % | < 0.05 % |
| Oxygen barrier (vs PET) | 1× | 0.6× | 3–5× |

**Why PBT crystallises faster than PET** (3 marks)
PBT's **1,4-butanediol** provides a longer, more flexible aliphatic spacer than PET's **ethylene glycol**. Mechanistically:
1. The longer CH₂ chain allows the molecule to **adopt the gauche–trans–gauche conformation** that matches the crystalline unit cell more easily.
2. PET's two-carbon spacer forces the chain into a near-linear, fully-extended conformation that must re-arrange considerably before crystallising — hence its **very long crystallisation half-time** at typical processing temperatures.
3. As a result, **PBT mouldings can be demoulded hot without distortion**, whereas PET preforms are made amorphous and crystallised *in situ* during blow moulding via strain-induced crystallisation.

PEN's higher Tg arises from the **fused two-ring naphthalene** restricting segmental motion far more than a single benzene ring.

---

### QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the chemistry of **unsaturated polyester laminating resins**. Describe the role of styrene, the curing mechanism with peroxides, and the typical shrinkage problem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe **SMC and BMC** formulations, manufacture, and applications. Explain the role of low-profile additives in obtaining Class-A surfaces. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — B(a)

**UPR Chemistry** (3 marks)
UPR is made by condensation of a mixture of:
- **Maleic anhydride (MA)** — provides the **C=C unsaturation** that will later crosslink.
- **Phthalic anhydride (PA)** or **isophthalic acid** — provides aromatic rigidity and dilutes the crosslink density.
- **Propylene glycol** or neopentyl glycol — provides chain flexibility.

The reaction gives a solid unsaturated polyester, which is cooled and dissolved in **30–45 % styrene** to make a pourable, viscous resin.

**Role of Styrene** (2 marks)
Styrene is a **reactive diluent and co-monomer**:
1. Reduces viscosity for ease of impregnating glass fibres.
2. Participates in the curing reaction by copolymerising with the maleic/fumaric C=C bonds of the polyester, thereby **bridging two polyester chains** into a 3-D network.

**Curing Mechanism** (1 mark)
A peroxide such as MEKP or benzoyl peroxide decomposes to free radicals (often accelerated by cobalt naphthenate at room temperature). The radicals attack the C=C of both the polyester and the styrene, propagating a chain reaction that builds the crosslinked network.

**Shrinkage** (1 mark)
Cure shrinkage is **5–9 % by volume**, leading to internal stresses, sink marks, surface waviness and microcracks. The shrinkage is mitigated using **low-profile additives** (thermoplastic powders) that compensate via their own thermal expansion.

---

#### Model Solution — B(b)

**SMC and BMC Formulations** (3 marks)
A typical SMC recipe:

| Ingredient | % |
|---|---|
| UPR resin (in styrene) | 25–35 |
| Filler (CaCO₃, ATH) | 40–55 |
| Chopped glass fibre (12–25 mm) | 15–30 |
| Thickener (MgO) | 1–3 |
| Release agent (Zn stearate) | 0.5–2 |
| Pigment, inhibitor, peroxide | balance |

BMC has identical composition but lower fibre length and higher filler loading, giving a dough rather than a sheet.

**Manufacture of SMC** (2 marks)
1. Resin paste is metered onto two carrier films via **doctor boxes**.
2. **Chopped glass fibre rovings** are deposited between the two resin films.
3. The sandwich is **compacted** by rollers, then wound and matured for **24–72 h at 30–40 °C**, during which MgO thickens the paste to ~1500 Pa·s.
4. The mature SMC sheet is then **compression-moulded at 140–160 °C** for 1–5 min.

**Applications** (1 mark)
SMC/BMC are used for automotive body panels (bonnets, boot lids, bumpers), electrical enclosures, switchgear components, and sanitary ware.

**Low-Profile Additives** (1 mark)
LPAs are thermoplastic powders (PVAc, polyethylene, PMMA) that, upon cure, phase-separate into a micro-network that **counteracts** the volumetric shrinkage of the polyester. The result is a Class-A surface — i.e., one that mirrors the polished mould without sink marks, fibre print-through, or waviness — meeting the strict aesthetic standards of automotive exterior panels.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Draining Mistakes in Polyester Questions (KTU Board Examiner's Perspective)**
> 1. **Confusing Tg and Tm** — Many students write "PET melts at 75 °C" (wrong; that is the *glass transition*). The melting point of PET is ~255 °C. Examiners deduct ½ mark for this in 14-mark questions and 1 mark in numericals.
> 2. **Forgetting the role of styrene** — In UPR questions, do *not* omit the statement that **styrene is a reactive monomer**, not just a diluent. Treating it as a non-reactive solvent is a classic 1-mark loss.
> 3. **Confusing SMC with BMC** — SMC is a *sheet*, BMC is a *bulk dough*. Examiners want the **shape of the charge** in the answer.
> 4. **No mention of MgO** — In any SMC question, MgO is the *thickener*; answers without it are incomplete.
> 5. **Carothers equation misuse** — Always state whether stoichiometry is balanced (r = 1) or imbalanced (r ≠ 1). Forgetting r in the equation is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 6. **Missing units in numerical answers** — $\overline{M}_n$ must end with "g/mol" or "Da"; $X_c$ must be dimensionless or given as %.
> 7. **Writing "polyethylene" instead of "polyester"** — surprisingly common; double-check before submitting.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3 Polyesters**

- **PET = Poly(Ethylene Terephthalate)** — repeat unit M₀ = **192 g/mol**; Tg ≈ 75 °C; Tm ≈ 255 °C.
- **PBT = Poly(Butylene Terephthalate)** — Tg ≈ 35 °C; Tm ≈ 225 °C; faster crystallisation → faster cycle times.
- **PEN = Poly(Ethylene Naphthalate)** — Tg ≈ 120 °C; Tm ≈ 268 °C; higher barrier & stiffness than PET.
- **Direct esterification (TPA route)** uses Sb₂O₃ catalyst; **ester-interchange (DMT route)** uses Mn/Zn catalysts.
- **Carothers equation**: $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$; for r = 1, $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$.
- High molecular weight PET needs **p > 0.99** and **high vacuum** to remove water.
- **UPR** = unsaturated polyester (MA + PA + glycol) dissolved in **30–45 % styrene**; cures with **peroxide (MEKP/BPO)** + cobalt accelerator.
- **Cure shrinkage of UPR = 5–9 %**; mitigated by **low-profile additives (PVAc, PE, PMMA)**.
- **SMC** = sheet sandwiched between carrier films; **BMC/DMC** = dough-like bulk; both contain **UPR + filler + glass + MgO thickener**.
- **SMC moulding conditions**: 140–160 °C, 1–5 min, compression 500–1000 kN.
- **PET fibre production**: melt spinning → drawing 4:1–6:1 → high tenacity, wrinkle-resistant.
- **BOPET film**: biaxially oriented PET, $X_c$ ≈ 40–55 %, tensile 190–260 MPa, used in packaging & electrical insulation.
- **PET bottles**: stretch-blow moulded; strain-induced crystallisation; CO₂ barrier; lightweight; recyclable (Resin Code 1).
- **PBT connectors**: chosen for **fast cycle + low moisture + dimensional stability**.
- **Birefringence ($\Delta n = n_{\parallel} - n_{\perp}$)** ≈ 0.20 for fully oriented PET fibre; useful as a forensic signature.
- **Crystalline fraction from density**: $X_c = \dfrac{\rho_c(\rho-\rho_a)}{\rho(\rho_c-\rho_a)}$.
- **Fox's rule** (empirical): $T_g \approx \dfrac{2}{3} T_m$ (in Kelvin) for crystalline polyesters.
- **Always** quote both **Tg and Tm** for any thermoplastic polyester; never confuse them.
- **UPR is both a laminating resin and the matrix of SMC/BMC** — same chemistry, different physical form and moulding route.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Monomer Preparation, Polymerisation, Properties and Application of PF, Preparation of Phenol Formaldehyde Moulding Powders, PF Laminates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT403 - PLASTICS MATERIALS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Monomer Preparation, Polymerisation, Properties and Application of PF, Preparation of Phenol Formaldehyde Moulding Powders, PF Laminates # 🧪 Phenol Formaldehyde (PF) Resin — The Pioneer of Synthetic Plastics > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** > Phenol Formaldehyde (PF) is the **first fully synthetic plastic** ever commercialised (Leo Baekeland, **1907** — trade name **Bakelite**). It belongs to the family of **thermosetting resins** and forms the bedrock of moulding powder and laminate industries. Mastery of its monomer chemistry, two-stage (Novolac) vs single-stage (Resole) polymerisation, moulding powder recipe, and laminate manufacture is **mandatory** for KTU ESE and internal assessments. --- ## 1.1 What is Phenol Formaldehyde Resin? ### 📘 Formal KTU Definition Phenol Formaldehyde (PF) resin is a **thermosetting condensation polymer** formed by the step-growth reaction between **phenol (C₆H₅OH)** and **formaldehyde (HCHO)** in the presence of an acidic or alkaline catalyst. The resulting three-dimensional cross-linked network, after curing, becomes infusible, insoluble, and dimensionally stable — hence the term *thermoset*. ### 💡 Conceptual Analogy — The "Molecular Honeycomb" Imagine a beehive where every honeycomb cell is locked into its neighbours by permanent chemical bonds. Once set, you cannot melt and reshape the honeycomb — it will simply char and burn. PF behaves exactly like this: each **phenol ring** acts as a "junction point," and **methylene bridges (–CH₂–)** act as the "waxy walls" permanently locking the rings together. Compare this to a thermoplastic like polyethylene, which is like a pile of loose spaghetti noodles that can be melted and re-moulded indefinitely. > [!NOTE] > **Key Terminology Box** > - **Thermoset** → permanently hard once cured (cannot be remelted). > - **Resole** → single-stage resin containing reactive methylol groups (self-curing on heating). > - **Novolac** → two-stage resin requiring a hardener (usually hexamethylenetetramine, "Hexa") for curing. > - **A-stage, B-stage, C-stage** → progression of cure from liquid (A) → rubbery (B) → fully cross-linked hard solid (C). --- ## 1.2 Monomer Preparation — The Building Blocks ### 🧪 Phenol (C₆H₅OH) Phenol is a hydroxy derivative of benzene. Industrially, several routes exist; the **Cumene Process** is dominant. **Cumene Process (Industrial Route):** 1. Benzene + Propylene → Cumene (isopropylbenzene) — Friedel–Crafts alkylation. 2. Cumene + O₂ (air) → Cumene Hydroperoxide (CHP). 3. CHP + H₂SO₄ (dilute) → Phenol + Acetone. > [!IMPORTANT] > The cumene process is favoured because it **co-produces acetone** — both phenol and acetone are valuable commercial feedstocks, making the process economically attractive. Other routes: - **Coal tar distillation** (older route, gives *carbolic acid*). - **Raschig process**: Benzene + HCl → Chlorobenzene → Hydrolysis → Phenol. - **Toluene oxidation**: Toluene → Benzoic acid → Phenol. ### 🧪 Formaldehyde (HCHO) Formaldehyde is the simplest aldehyde, a pungent gas (bp = **−19 °C**), commercially supplied as **37% aqueous solution called "Formalin"**. **Industrial Preparation — Catalytic Oxidation of Methanol:** $$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Cu or Ag catalyst, } 500-700\,°C} \text{HCHO} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Side reaction:** Further oxidation can produce formic acid (HCOOH) and even CO₂ — hence methanol feed is kept in slight excess (~5%) to suppress over-oxidation. The catalyst is typically a **silver gauze** or **iron-molybdenum oxide**. --- ## 1.3 Polymerisation Chemistry — How the Resin Forms The PF reaction proceeds in **two distinct chemical stages**: ### Stage 1: Addition Reaction (Methylolation) Formaldehyde adds to the *ortho* and *para* positions of phenol, generating **methylolphenols** (hydroxymethylphenols). $$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{HCHO} \longrightarrow \text{HO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CH}_2\text{OH}$$ Three possible mono-methylol products: *2-hydroxymethylphenol*, *4-hydroxymethylphenol*, and *2,4-bis(hydroxymethyl)phenol*. > [!IMPORTANT] > Phenol has three reactive positions (two *ortho* = positions 2 & 6, and one *para* = position 4). This **trifunctionality** is the molecular reason PF can form a 3-D cross-linked network! ### Stage 2: Condensation Reaction Methylol groups react with each other (or with phenol) to form **methylene bridges (–CH₂–)** and liberate water: $$\text{–CH}_2\text{OH} + \text{HO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–} \longrightarrow \text{–CH}_2\text{–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ Or two methylol groups condense: $$\text{–CH}_2\text{OH} + \text{HOCH}_2\text{–} \longrightarrow \text{–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{HCHO}$$ --- ## 1.4 Two Routes of Polymerisation | Feature | **Resole (Single-Stage)** | **Novolac (Two-Stage)** | |---|---|---| | Catalyst | **Alkaline** (NaOH, NH₄OH, Ba(OH)₂) | **Acidic** (HCl, H₂SO₄, oxalic acid) | | F:P molar ratio | **> 1** (formaldehyde excess) | **< 1** (phenol excess) | | Methylol groups | Present in resin | Absent (already condensed) | | Curing | Self-curing on heating | Requires hardener (Hexa) | | Storage | Limited shelf life | Stable indefinitely | | Application | Impregnation, laminates, adhesives | Moulding powders | --- ## 1.5 Properties and Applications of PF ### Mechanical & Physical Properties - High **rigidity** and **hardness**. - Excellent **dimensional stability** (low creep). - Good **electrical insulation** (dielectric strength ≈ **10–16 kV/mm**). - High **heat resistance** (continuous use up to **150–200 °C**). - Self-extinguishing / low flammability. - Resistant to solvents, acids (except strong oxidising acids), and water. ### Limitations - **Brittle** in unmodified form (needs fillers). - Dark colour (only brown/black — cannot be made light/transparent). - Requires high moulding pressure and temperature. ### Major Applications 1. **Moulding powders** (Bakelite) — electrical switches, plugs, handles, radio/TV cabinets. 2. **Laminates** — circuit boards, decorative panels, worktops. 3. **Adhesives** — plywood, particle board (especially *fortified* resoles). 4. **Coatings and varnishes**. 5. **Foundry resins** — sand binders for casting. 6. **Brake linings and clutch facings** (heat-resistant friction material). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Memory Hook:** "PF = (Bakelite) = (Electrical + Heat) + Dark + Brittle." This captures the four hallmark property traits examiners love to test. --- # 🧠 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Polymerisation Mechanism ### 🔬 Resole (Base-Catalysed) Mechanism In alkaline medium, formaldehyde exists as the **methanediolate anion** $\text{HOCH}_2\text{O}^-$, a strong nucleophile. **Step 1 — Phenoxide formation:** $$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{OH}^- \rightleftharpoons \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{O}^- + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ The phenoxide ion is activated at *ortho* and *para* positions (electron-donating –O⁻). **Step 2 — Nucleophilic addition:** $$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{O}^- + \text{HCHO} \longrightarrow \text{HO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CH}_2\text{O}^-$$ This produces a series of *mono-, di-, and tri-methylolphenols*. With F:P > 1, trimethylolphenol dominates, providing the multi-functionality needed for cross-linking. **Step 3 — Condensation:** Methylolphenols condense via methylene and methylene-ether (–CH₂–O–CH₂–) bridges. Ether bridges form first (kinetically favoured) and later convert to methylene bridges with loss of formaldehyde (thermodynamically favoured). > [!IMPORTANT] > In a Resole, curing proceeds via the formation of **benzylamine-type cross-links** at elevated temperatures (≈ 130–180 °C) — entirely without any added hardener. This is why resols are called *"single-stage"* resins. ### 🔬 Novolac (Acid-Catalysed) Mechanism In acidic medium, formaldehyde is protonated: $\text{H}_2\text{C}=\overset{+}{\text{O}}\text{H}$ Electrophilic substitution on phenol (highly activated by –OH) yields **predominantly 4-hydroxymethylphenol** and then **4,4′-dihydroxydiphenylmethane** (a methylene-bridged dimer). Because phenol is in excess (F:P < 1), chain growth stops at linear/branched but **uncross-linked** oligomers — average degree of polymerisation ≈ 5–10 phenolic units. To cure, **Hexamethylenetetramine (Hexa, (CH₂)₆N₄)** is added at ≈ **8–15%**, which decomposes on heating to release **ammonia and formaldehyde** *in situ*, providing the cross-linking methylene and amine bridges. $$\text{(CH}_2)_6\text{N}_4 \xrightarrow{\Delta} 6\,\text{HCHO} + 4\,\text{NH}_3$$ --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Equation / Parameter | Formula / Value | Significance | |---|---|---| | Phenol functionality | $f = 3$ (2 *ortho* + 1 *para*) | Governs cross-linking potential | | Gel point (Flory–Stockmayer) | $p_c = \dfrac{2}{f} = \dfrac{2}{3}$ | Critical extent of reaction | | Carothers equation (number avg DP) | $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | $p$ = extent of reaction | | F:P ratio (Resole) | $> 1$ (typical **1.2–2.0**) | Favours cross-linking | | F:P ratio (Novolac) | $< 1$ (typical **0.7–0.9**) | Keeps resin fusible | | Hexa content (moulding powder) | $\approx \mathbf{8\% - 15\%}$ | Hardener for Novolac | | Hexamine decomposition | $(\text{CH}_2)_6\text{N}_4 \to 6\,\text{HCHO} + 4\,\text{NH}_3$ | Generates curing species | | Curing temperature | $\mathbf{130–180\,°C}$ | Typical moulding range | | Moulding pressure | $\mathbf{15–35\,\text{MPa}}$ | Compression / transfer moulding | | Dielectric strength | $\mathbf{10–16\,\text{kV/mm}}$ | Electrical insulation rating | | Heat resistance (continuous) | $\mathbf{150–200\,°C}$ | High thermal stability | > [!NOTE] > The **Carothers equation** above assumes *ideal step-growth polymerisation*. Note that for **thermosets** such as PF, gelation occurs *before* $\overline{X}_n \to \infty$, so the equation is valid **only until the gel point**. --- ## 2.3 Curing Stages — A → B → C | Stage | State | Description | |---|---|---| | **A-stage** | Liquid | Resole / molten Novolac; soluble in alcohol/acetone. | | **B-stage** | Rubbery / solid | Partially condensed; fusible but only partly soluble; ideal for moulding powder preforms. | | **C-stage** | Fully cured | Infusible, insoluble, rigid 3-D network — the final thermoset. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** The transformation A → B is called **"advancement"** and is the critical stage where resin is *quenched* for storage. The B-stage is what you actually use in moulding powders — the resin must "flow" in the mould before final cross-linking occurs at C-stage. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Electrical Industry:** Switchboards, plug tops, MCB housings — PF replaced earlier ceramic and ebonite because it could be moulded into intricate shapes while remaining a superb insulator. - **Aerospace / Defence:** PF-carbon and PF-glass composites serve as ablative heat shields (re-entry vehicles) and rocket nozzle liners because PF chars to a refractory carbon layer. - **Construction:** PF-bonded plywood and particle boards are water- and weather-resistant — the resin cures to a permanently waterproof glue line. - **Foundry:** PF resin binders for sand cores enable near-net-shape casting of complex metal parts. --- # 🔬 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Average Degree of Polymerisation (Carothers Equation) The **Carothers equation** is central to step-growth polymerisation analysis of PF. We derive it rigorously for a bifunctional (for simplicity) AA + BB system: Let: - $N_0$ = total number of monomer molecules initially present. - $N$ = number of molecules remaining at extent of reaction $p$. - $p$ = fraction of functional groups that have reacted. **Step 1:** Number of functional groups initially = $2N_0$ (each monomer contributes 2 groups). **Step 2:** Number of bonds formed = $p \cdot N_0$ (each bond consumes 2 functional groups; one bond reduces total molecule count by 1). Wait — careful. If $p$ is the *fraction of functional groups* reacted, then total groups reacted $= 2N_0 p$. Each bond consumes 2 groups, so number of new bonds formed $= N_0 p$. Each bond reduces molecule count by 1: **Step 3:** Number of molecules now = $N_0 - N_0 p = N_0(1-p)$. $$\boxed{\;N = N_0(1-p)\;}$$ **Step 4:** Degree of polymerisation $\overline{X}_n$ = initial monomers / molecules remaining: $$\overline{X}_n = \frac{N_0}{N} = \frac{N_0}{N_0(1-p)}$$ $$\boxed{\;\overline{X}_n = \frac{1}{1-p}\;}$$ **Step 5:** Number-average molecular weight: $$\overline{M}_n = \overline{X}_n \cdot M_0 = \frac{M_0}{1-p}$$ > where $M_0$ is the average monomer molecular weight. **Step 6:** For a *trifunctional* monomer (as in phenol), the **gel point** is reached when the weight-average DP $\overline{X}_w \to \infty$. Using the Flory–Stockmayer statistical theory: $$p_c = \frac{2}{f}$$ For phenol with $f = 3$: $$\boxed{\;p_c = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667\;}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** Once **2/3** of all phenol functional positions have reacted, the system **gels** — infinite molecular-weight network forms. The reaction vessel becomes a single giant molecule. This is the molecular signature of thermoset formation. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style **Problem:** In a PF Resole synthesis, phenol (94 g/mol) and formaldehyde (30 g/mol) are reacted in the molar ratio F : P = 1.5 : 1. Calculate: (a) Mass of formaldehyde needed per 100 g of phenol. (b) Extent of reaction $p$ if $\overline{X}_n = 10$. (c) Whether the system has gelled. ### Solution **(a) Mass of formaldehyde per 100 g phenol:** - Moles of phenol in 100 g = $\dfrac{100}{94} = 1.0638\;\text{mol}$. - Required moles of HCHO = $1.5 \times 1.0638 = 1.5957\;\text{mol}$. - Mass of HCHO = $1.5957 \times 30 = \mathbf{47.87\;\text{g}}$. **(b) Extent of reaction:** Using $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$: $$10 = \frac{1}{1-p} \;\Rightarrow\; 1-p = 0.1 \;\Rightarrow\; \boxed{p = 0.9 = 90\%}$$ **(c) Gel check:** Since $p = 0.9 > p_c = 0.667$, **the system has gelled**. ✅ > [!NOTE] > This confirms why a Resole (F:P > 1) must be "arrested" at the B-stage by cooling/quenching before curing — otherwise it gels in the kettle and becomes unusable. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Resole vs Novolac | Parameter | Resole | Novolac | |---|---|---| | Catalyst type | Base (NaOH) | Acid (HCl) | | F:P molar ratio | 1.2 – 2.0 | 0.7 – 0.9 | | Methylol content | High | Negligible | | Self-curing? | Yes (heat only) | No — needs Hexa | | Shelf life | Weeks–months | Years | | Initial water released? | No (condensation is later) | No | | Typical end-use | Adhesives, laminates | Moulding powders | | Storage form | Aqueous solution / syrup | Solid flakes/granules | --- ## 3.4 Stoichiometry of Hexa Decomposition in Novolac Moulding Powder Let a Novolac moulding powder contain **10% Hexa** by mass. For **1 kg** of powder: - Mass of Hexa = 100 g. - Molar mass of Hexa = $6(12) + 12(1) + 4(14) = 72 + 12 + 56 = \mathbf{140\;g/mol}$. - Moles of Hexa = $\dfrac{100}{140} = 0.714\;\text{mol}$. Decomposition: $(\text{CH}_2)_6\text{N}_4 \to 6\,\text{HCHO} + 4\,\text{NH}_3$ - Moles of HCHO released = $6 \times 0.714 = 4.286\;\text{mol}$ → Mass = $4.286 \times 30 = \mathbf{128.6\;\text{g}}$. - Moles of NH₃ released = $4 \times 0.714 = 2.857\;\text{mol}$ → Mass = $2.857 \times 17 = \mathbf{48.6\;\text{g}}$. > This liberated HCHO and NH₃ *in situ* are what cross-link the Novolac into the final C-stage network during moulding. --- # 📊 Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Overall PF Manufacturing Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["RAW MONOMERS"] --> B["Phenol C6H5OH
Cumene process"] A --> C["Formaldehyde HCHO
Methanol oxidation"] B --> D["REACTOR KETTLE
with catalyst"] C --> D D --> E{"Catalyst
Type"} E -->|Alkaline F:P greater than 1| F["RESOLE
Single stage resin"] E -->|Acidic F:P less than 1| G["NOVOLAC
Two stage resin"] F --> F1["Resole syrup
or solution"] F --> F2["Resol laminating
varnish"] G --> G1["Solid flakes
ground to powder"] G1 --> H["MIXING BLENDER"] H --> H1["Add Hexa hardener 10 percent"] H --> H2["Add wood flour filler"] H --> H3["Add pigment and lubricant"] H1 --> I["MOULDING POWDER
B stage"] H2 --> I H3 --> I F1 --> J["IMPREGNATION TANK"] J --> J1["Paper fabric or glass cloth"] J1 --> K["DRIER AND B STAGE OVEN"] K --> L["PF LAMINATE SHEETS
C stage via hot press"] I --> M["COMPRESSION MOULDING
150 to 180 deg C"] M --> N["FINAL PF PRODUCT
C stage thermoset"] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Chemical Reaction Sequence (PF Network Formation) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["Phenol ring
3 active sites"] --> A1["ADDITION
Methylolation"] A1 --> A2["Methylolphenol
HO CH2 attached"] A2 --> A3["Di and tri methylol
species formed"] A3 --> B1["CONDENSATION
Loss of H2O"] B1 --> B2["Methylene bridge
CH2 link"] B1 --> B3["Methylene ether bridge
CH2 O CH2 link"] B2 --> C1["3D CROSS LINKED NETWORK
C stage thermoset"] B3 --> C1 C1 --> D1["Infusible insoluble rigid
final Bakelite"] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Phenol Functional Positions ```mermaid flowchart TD R["Phenol C6H5OH
Aromatic ring"] --> P1["Position 4
PARA site"] R --> P2["Position 2
ORTHO site"] R --> P3["Position 6
ORTHO site"] P1 --> X["3 Reactive H positions
available for HCHO attack"] P2 --> X P3 --> X X --> Y["Trifunctionality f equals 3
enables 3D cross linking"] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — A → B → C Cure Progression ```mermaid flowchart LR A["A STAGE
Liquid resole
or molten novolac"] -->|Heating and condensation| B["B STAGE
Rubbery partially cured
fusible and partly soluble"] B -->|Further cross linking
in mould 150 to 180 deg C| C["C STAGE
Fully cured thermoset
infusible and insoluble"] C --> C1["Final rigid PF product
e.g. Bakelite switch"] ``` --- # 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper] **Differentiate between Resole and Novolac resins with respect to catalyst, F:P ratio, and curing mechanism.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Resole | Novolac | |---|---|---| | Catalyst | Alkaline (NaOH) | Acidic (HCl / oxalic acid) | | F:P molar ratio | > 1 | < 1 | | Curing | Self-curing on heating | Requires Hexa hardener | | Storage | Limited | Stable | *(Any 3 valid distinguishing points → 3 marks)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **What is Hexamethylenetetramine? Why is it added to Novolac moulding powder?** **Model Answer:** Hexamethylenetetramine, formula **$(\text{CH}_2)_6\text{N}_4$** (commonly called *"Hexa"*), is a heterocyclic amine formed by condensing formaldehyde with ammonia. It is added to Novolac moulding powder at about **8–15%** because it acts as a **hardener/cross-linking agent**. On heating (≈ 130 °C), Hexa decomposes: $$(\text{CH}_2)_6\text{N}_4 \xrightarrow{\Delta} 6\,\text{HCHO} + 4\,\text{NH}_3$$ The released formaldehyde and ammonia provide the **methylene (–CH₂–) and amine (–NH–)** bridges that cross-link Novolac into the rigid C-stage network. *(3 marks: identification + role + equation)* --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - Students **commonly lose marks** by *confusing Novolac and Resole* — remember: **Resole = R**eacts **e**asily on heating alone; **Novolac = N**eeds hardener. > - Do **NOT** forget the **A → B → C stages** when asked about moulding powder preparation. This omission alone costs **2–3 marks**. > - The **Hexa decomposition equation** is mandatory — writing only the name without the reaction is a half-answer. > - Always specify **catalyst type AND F:P ratio** together when describing either resin. > - In numerical problems, students forget to **check gel point ($p_c = 2/3$)** — failure to do so loses 2 marks. --- ### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024] **(a) Explain the polymerisation of phenol and formaldehyde under alkaline conditions. Discuss the formation of Resole and its curing mechanism. [7 Marks]** **(b) Describe the preparation of Phenol Formaldehyde moulding powder with a neat flow diagram and list its applications. [7 Marks]** #### 🔹 Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Catalyst and F:P ratio:** Alkaline catalyst (NaOH, NH₄OH, or Ba(OH)₂); formaldehyde is taken in excess (F : P = 1.2 to 2.0). (1 mark) **Step 2 — Methylolation (Addition):** Formaldehyde adds to the *ortho* and *para* positions of phenol to form mono-, di-, and tri-methylolphenols. (2 marks) $$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{HCHO} \longrightarrow \text{HO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CH}_2\text{OH}$$ **Step 3 — Condensation:** Methylolphenols condense with each other or with phenol, forming **methylene bridges (–CH₂–)** and **methylene-ether bridges (–CH₂–O–CH₂–)**, releasing water. (2 marks) **Step 4 — Curing (C-stage formation):** On heating (130–180 °C), the B-stage resole further condenses to form a rigid 3-D cross-linked network — the C-stage, which is infusible and insoluble. (2 marks) > **End keywords for full marks:** *Trifunctionality, methylene bridge, A→B→C, gel point at $p_c = 2/3$.* #### 🔹 Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Base resin:** Novolac resin (acid-catalysed, F:P < 1) is produced and ground to fine powder. (1 mark) **Step 2 — Mixing recipe (typical composition):** (3 marks) - Novolac resin: **40–50%** - Wood flour filler: **40–50%** (improves strength, reduces shrinkage) - Hexa hardener: **8–15%** (curing agent) - Pigment: **1–2%** (colour) - Lubricant (stearic acid / calcium stearate): **1–2%** (mould release) - Accelerator (MgO / CaO): **1–2%** (speeds up curing) **Step 3 — Process:** Mixed → roll-milled → ground → sieved → B-staged. (1 mark) **Step 4 — Moulding:** Compression moulded at **150–180 °C** and **15–35 MPa** for **2–5 minutes**, then ejected hot. (1 mark) **Step 5 — Applications:** Electrical switches, plugs, MCB housings, handles, telephone bodies, radio cabinets, brake linings. (1 mark) --- ### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a) Describe the preparation, properties, and applications of Phenol Formaldehyde laminates. [7 Marks]** **(b) Compare Novolac and Resole resins. A Novolac resin was synthesised from 100 g phenol and 60 g formaldehyde. Calculate (i) F:P molar ratio and (ii) whether the system is thermosetting or thermoplastic. [7 Marks]** #### 🔹 Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Base material (substrate):** Sheets of **paper, cotton fabric, asbestos, or glass cloth** serve as the reinforcing base. (1 mark) **Step 2 — Resin preparation:** A **Resole resin** (low molecular weight, alcohol-soluble, alkaline-catalysed) is prepared with F:P ratio ≈ 1.5–2.0. It is dissolved in ethanol to form the **impregnating varnish**. (1 mark) **Step 3 — Impregnation:** The substrate sheets are dipped into the varnish tank, ensuring full wetting of fibres. Excess resin is squeezed out by **squeeze rollers**. (1 mark) **Step 4 — Drying / B-staging:** Impregnated sheets pass through a **drying oven at 80–100 °C** to evaporate solvent and advance resin to the **B-stage**. (1 mark) **Step 5 — Lay-up and pressing:** Multiple B-staged sheets are **stacked** with grain alternation and pressed in a **hydraulic press at 150–160 °C and 10–15 MPa** for 30–60 minutes. (2 marks) **Step 6 — Curing & post-cure:** Heat triggers cross-linking to C-stage; laminates are cooled under pressure to avoid warping. (1 mark) **Properties:** High mechanical strength, water resistance, chemical resistance, electrical insulation, fire retardance. **Applications:** Printed circuit boards (PCB), decorative laminates (Formica), worktops, electrical insulation panels, gear wheels. (Bonus — 1 mark for properties+apps) #### 🔹 Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks) **Comparison Table (3 marks):** | Parameter | Novolac | Resole | |---|---|---| | Catalyst | Acid | Alkali | | F:P ratio | < 1 | > 1 | | Hardener | Hexa needed | Self-curing | | Form | Solid powder | Syrup/solution | | Storage | Stable | Limited shelf life | **Numerical (4 marks):** - Moles of phenol = $\dfrac{100}{94} = 1.0638\;\text{mol}$ - Moles of HCHO = $\dfrac{60}{30} = 2.0000\;\text{mol}$ (i) F:P molar ratio = $\dfrac{2.0000}{1.0638} = \mathbf{1.88}$ (ii) Since **F : P = 1.88 > 1** (formaldehyde in excess), the system behaves like a **Resole-type** — it is **thermosetting** and will cross-link on heating without needing Hexa. > **Examiner comment:** Many students mistakenly classify any F:P > 1 system as "Resole" without checking — but the resin *type* is determined by the **catalyst** used in synthesis. Here, the stoichiometry tells us the *thermoset nature*; the *name* (Novolac or Resole) depends on catalyst. Award full marks if both stoichiometry and thermoset conclusion are stated. --- ## 🧾 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must memorise for KTU ESE):** - ✅ PF = **first synthetic thermoset** (Baekeland, 1907; trade name = **Bakelite**). - ✅ Phenol is **trifunctional** ($f = 3$) → enables 3-D network. - ✅ **Resole** = alkaline catalyst, F:P > 1, self-curing, single-stage. - ✅ **Novolac** = acid catalyst, F:P < 1, needs **Hexa (≈ 10%)**, two-stage. - ✅ Hexa decomposition: $(\text{CH}_2)_6\text{N}_4 \to 6\,\text{HCHO} + 4\,\text{NH}_3$. - ✅ Moulding powder composition: Novolac + wood flour + Hexa + pigment + lubricant. - ✅ **A-stage** (liquid) → **B-stage** (rubbery) → **C-stage** (hard, infusible). - ✅ Carothers equation: $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$. - ✅ Gel point: $p_c = \dfrac{2}{f} = \dfrac{2}{3} \approx 0.667$. - ✅ Moulding conditions: **150–180 °C**, **15–35 MPa**, **2–5 min**. - ✅ Laminates use **Resole varnish** on paper/fabric/glass cloth, hot-pressed. - ✅ Properties: heat-resistant (150–200 °C), dielectric (10–16 kV/mm), brittle, dark, self-extinguishing. - ✅ Major applications: switches, plugs, PCBs, brake linings, plywood adhesive, foundry sand binder. ---

topics.title: Novolac, Resol, MF and UF Resins
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT403 - PLASTICS MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Novolac, Resol, MF and UF Resins

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧪 Novolac, Resol, MF and UF Resins — Module 4 Study Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermosetting Resins Defined (KTU Syllabus Statement):**
> Thermoset resins are polymers that undergo an irreversible chemical change (curing/crosslinking) when subjected to heat, catalysts, or radiation, forming a rigid, infusible, three-dimensional network. Once cured, they cannot be remelted or reprocessed. The two major families covered in this module are **Phenolic Resins (PF)** — subdivided into **Novolac** and **Resol** — and **Amino Resins (AF)** — subdivided into **Melamine-Formaldehyde (MF)** and **Urea-Formaldehyde (UF)**.

### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy — "The Hard-Boiled Egg"
Think of a thermoset like a **hard-boiled egg**:
- The raw egg (uncured resin) is a viscous liquid of linear/branched chains — you can reshape it.
- Once you heat it, the proteins crosslink permanently into a rigid 3D network — the egg is now hard. **You cannot unboil it.**
- Likewise, Novolac, Resol, UF, and MF resins "boil" (cure) once and stay hard forever.

### 🔬 Family Tree at a Glance

| Family | Sub-Type | Sub-Type | Catalyst Used | F:P Molar Ratio | Curing Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Phenolic (PF)** | **Novolac** | — | Acidic (HCl, H₂SO₄, Oxalic acid) | Phenol excess (~1:0.8) | **Two-step** (needs hardener) |
| **Phenolic (PF)** | **Resol** | — | Basic (NaOH, NH₃, Ba(OH)₂) | Formaldehyde excess (~1:1.5) | **Single-step** (self-curing) |
| **Amino (AF)** | **Urea-Formaldehyde (UF)** | — | Mildly basic/acidic | Formaldehyde excess | Single-step (heat cure) |
| **Amino (AF)** | **Melamine-Formaldehyde (MF)** | — | Mildly basic | Formaldehyde excess | Single-step (heat cure) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The **acid vs. base catalyst** is the single most important distinguishing feature between Novolac and Resol. Examiners love to ask: *"Given an acid catalyst, what is the product?"* — Answer: **Novolac**.

---

### 1.1 Novolac Resin — Definition
**Novolac** is a **linear, thermoplastic, phenol-formaldehyde prepolymer** synthesized by reacting phenol with formaldehyde in the presence of an **acid catalyst** with phenol in molar excess. The word "Novolac" is derived from the Latin *novus* (new) and *lac* (resin/lacquer), literally meaning "new resin." It is **permanently fusible and soluble** until a hardener (commonly **hexamethylenetetramine, HMTA**) is added.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** **N**ovolac = **N**eeds hardener (HMTA) + **A**cid catalyst.

### 1.2 Resol Resin — Definition
**Resol** is a **branched, self-curing phenol-formaldehyde prepolymer** synthesized by reacting phenol with formaldehyde in the presence of an **alkali (base) catalyst** with formaldehyde in molar excess. It contains active **methylol (–CH₂OH)** groups, which on heating undergo condensation to yield a fully crosslinked, infusible thermoset. No external hardener is required.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** **R**esol = **R**eady to cure (self-curing) + **B**ase catalyst.

### 1.3 Urea-Formaldehyde (UF) Resin — Definition
**UF resin** is a thermoset amino resin formed by the polycondensation of **urea (NH₂–CO–NH₂)** with **formaldehyde (HCHO)** in the presence of a mildly basic or acidic catalyst. The resulting resin contains **hydroxymethyl (–CH₂OH)** and **methylene ether (–CH₂–O–CH₂–)** bridges which crosslink on heating to give a hard, transparent network.

### 1.4 Melamine-Formaldehyde (MF) Resin — Definition
**MF resin** is a thermoset amino resin formed by the polycondensation of **melamine (C₃N₃(NH₂)₃)** — a triazine ring with three amine groups — with formaldehyde. The product is similar in chemistry to UF but exhibits superior **heat resistance, water resistance, and hardness**, primarily due to the aromatic stability of the triazine ring.

---

## 2. 🌊 Reaction Chemistry — The "Why" Behind the Products

### 2.1 Phenol-Formaldehyde Reactions
Both Novolac and Resol begin with the same two fundamental reactions:

**Step A — Methylolation (Addition Reaction):**
Phenol reacts with formaldehyde to form **ortho- and para-hydroxymethylphenols (methylol phenols).**

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{HCHO} \longrightarrow \text{HO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CH}_2\text{OH}$$

**Step B — Condensation (Methylene Bridge Formation):**
Methylol groups condense with each other (or with active phenol positions) to form **methylene (–CH₂–)** or **dimethylene ether (–CH₂–O–CH₂–)** bridges between rings.

$$2\,\text{HO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CH}_2\text{OH} \longrightarrow \text{HO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CH}_2\text{–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{HCHO}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" of Catalyst Effect:**
> - Under **acidic conditions**, the methylol intermediate is highly reactive and quickly condenses. Phenol is in excess → condensation halts at the **methylene bridge**, yielding a **linear Novolac**.
> - Under **basic conditions**, methylol groups are stabilized and accumulate on the ring. Formaldehyde is in excess → multiple methylol groups attach → a **branched, self-curing Resol** is formed.

---

### 2.2 Urea-Formaldehyde Reaction

**Step 1 — Methylolation:**
Urea's NH₂ groups react with HCHO to form **methylol urea** derivatives.

$$\text{NH}_2\text{–CO–NH}_2 + \text{HCHO} \longrightarrow \text{HOCH}_2\text{–NH–CO–NH–CH}_2\text{OH}$$

(Di-, tri-, and tetra-methylol ureas are also possible when HCHO is in excess.)

**Step 2 — Condensation:**
Methylol ureas condense to form **methylene (–CH₂–)** or **dimethylene ether (–CH₂–O–CH₂–)** bridges, releasing water.

$$2\,\text{HOCH}_2\text{–NH–CO–NH}_2 \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{C–[NH–CO–NH]}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

### 2.3 Melamine-Formaldehyde Reaction

**Step 1 — Methylolation:**
Each of the three NH₂ groups of melamine can react with HCHO. Maximum substitution yields **hexamethylol melamine**.

$$\text{C}_3\text{N}_3(\text{NH}_2)_3 + 6\,\text{HCHO} \longrightarrow \text{C}_3\text{N}_3(\text{NHCH}_2\text{OH})_6$$

**Step 2 — Condensation:**
Methylol groups on adjacent melamine molecules condense, forming bridges and releasing water, ultimately leading to a 3D crosslinked network.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Both UF and MF undergo **addition followed by condensation** — this is a classic 2-step mechanism examiners test repeatedly.

---

## 3. 🔬 Curing Mechanism — From Prepolymer to Thermoset

### 3.1 Curing of Novolac (Two-Step)
- **Curing agent:** Hexamethylenetetramine (HMTA, (CH₂)₆N₄) at 6–15% by mass.
- **Temperature:** ~150–180 °C under pressure.
- **Mechanism:** HMTA decomposes to release **ammonia (NH₃)** and **methylene (–CH₂–) groups** which bridge the linear Novolac chains, creating a 3D network.

$$(\text{CH}_2)_6\text{N}_4 \xrightarrow{\Delta} 6\,\text{HCHO} + 4\,\text{NH}_3$$

The formaldehyde and ammonia react with the phenolic –OH and ring positions to create crosslinks.

### 3.2 Curing of Resol (Single-Step)
- **No hardener required.**
- On heating above ~150 °C, the free methylol groups in the Resol react with each other, forming methylene and ether bridges → fully crosslinked thermoset (often called **Resite**).

### 3.3 Curing of UF and MF
- Acid catalysts (e.g., NH₄Cl) are often added as latent hardeners.
- Heat (~130–150 °C) drives off water and completes the crosslinking.

---

## 4. 🏗️ Structural & Property Comparison

| Property | Novolac | Resol | UF | MF |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Catalyst** | Acid | Base | Acid/Base | Acid/Base |
| **Form** | Linear | Branched | Branched | Branched |
| **Curing** | Two-step (HMTA) | Single-step | Single-step | Single-step |
| **Color** | Pale yellow to amber | Amber/dark | Colorless/transparent | Colorless/transparent |
| **Heat Resistance** | High | High | Moderate (≤100 °C) | Very High |
| **Water Resistance** | Good | Good | Poor (absorbs water) | Excellent |
| **Electrical Insulation** | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Excellent |
| **Cost** | Moderate | Moderate | Low | Higher |
| **Molded Density (g/cc)** | ~1.25–1.30 | ~1.25–1.30 | ~1.50 | ~1.50 |

---

## 5. 🏭 Engineering Applications

### Novolac
- **Molding powders (Bakelite)** for electrical switches, plugs, sockets.
- **Friction materials** — brake linings, clutch facings.
- **Abrasive binders** — grinding wheels (with HMTA).
- **Photoresists** (in electronics industry).

### Resol
- **Impregnating resins** for paper, cloth, and wood laminates.
- **Adhesives** for plywood and particle board.
- **Foundry sand binders** (shell molding).
- **Foams** for thermal insulation.

### UF Resin
- **Wood adhesives** (plywood, particleboard, MDF) — major use.
- **Molded articles** (electrical fittings).
- **Textile finishing** (crease resistance).
- **Slow-release nitrogen fertilizers**.

### MF Resin
- **Decorative laminates** (Formica-type countertops) — major use.
- **Dinnerware** (unbreakable, heat-resistant).
- **Electrical insulators** (arc-resistant).
- **Paints and coatings** (crosslinker for alkyds).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1. KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes every key relationship, parameter, and rule you need for the KTU examination on this topic.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Variable Meaning | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Catalyst selection** | Acid catalyst $\Rightarrow$ **Novolac**; Base catalyst $\Rightarrow$ **Resol** | — | F:P < 1 (Novolac); F:P > 1 (Resol) |
| 2 | **Methylol formation** | $\text{PhOH} + \text{HCHO} \rightarrow \text{HO–Ph–CH}_2\text{OH}$ | — | Step A (addition) |
| 3 | **Methylene bridge** | $2\,\text{HOCH}_2\text{–Ph} \rightarrow \text{–CH}_2\text{–Ph–CH}_2\text{–} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{HCHO}$ | — | Step B (condensation) |
| 4 | **HMTA decomposition** | $(\text{CH}_2)_6\text{N}_4 \rightarrow 6\,\text{HCHO} + 4\,\text{NH}_3$ | HMTA mass fraction in Novolac: **6–15%** | $T \approx 150-180\,°\text{C}$ |
| 5 | **Phenolic –OH equivalent weight** | $EW = \dfrac{M_w}{\text{no. of reactive –OH}}$ | Phenol $M_w = 94.11\,\text{g/mol}$ | — |
| 6 | **Resol A-stage classification** | $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C$ stages | A = fusible/soluble; B = rubbery; C = infusible | Heat or time |
| 7 | **UF Methylolation** | $\text{Urea} + n\,\text{HCHO} \rightarrow \text{Methylol urea}$ | $n$ can be 1, 2, 3, or 4 | $n_{\max} = 4$ |
| 8 | **MF Methylolation** | $\text{Melamine} + 6\,\text{HCHO} \rightarrow \text{Hexamethylol melamine}$ | Each NH₂ $\rightarrow$ NHCH₂OH | $n_{\max} = 6$ |
| 9 | **Curing heat** | $T_{\text{cure}}(\text{PF}) \approx 150-180\,°\text{C}$; $T_{\text{cure}}(\text{UF/MF}) \approx 130-150\,°\text{C}$ | — | Under pressure |
| 10 | **Degree of crosslinking rule** | Resol (high MeOH content) $\Rightarrow$ high crosslink density $\Rightarrow$ brittle | — | Empirical |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Reminder on the Catalyst Rule (Most Tested):**
> The catalyst + F:P ratio combo **uniquely determines** whether you form Novolac or Resol. Memorize:
> - Acid + Phenol-excess → **Novolac** (linear, needs hardener)
> - Base + Formaldehyde-excess → **Resol** (branched, self-cures)

---

## 2. Theoretical Concepts — The "Why" Behind Each Step

### 2.1 Why Phenol Needs Acid/Base Catalysis
Phenol's aromatic ring is electron-rich due to the –OH group's lone pair donation. Under **acidic conditions**, the ring becomes activated (via protonation of formaldehyde) for **electrophilic aromatic substitution** at the **ortho and para** positions. The methylol intermediate is so reactive that it instantly condenses with another phenol — leading to **controlled, linear growth (Novolac)**.

Under **basic conditions**, the formaldehyde (as a methylene glycol anion $\text{HOCH}_2^-$) attacks the ring directly, but the methylol product is stabilized by the alkaline medium. This allows **multiple methylol groups to accumulate on one ring** (one phenol can have up to **3 methylol groups** — 2 ortho + 1 para) — leading to a **branched, reactive Resol**.

### 2.2 Why HMTA is the Preferred Novolac Hardener
Hexamethylenetetramine (HMTA, (CH₂)₆N₄) is a cage-shaped molecule that, on heating, releases both **formaldehyde (HCHO)** and **ammonia (NH₃)**. The HCHO reacts with phenolic –OH groups to form methylols, while NH₃ forms Schiff-base-like intermediates that bridge chains. This **dual function** (crosslinker + methylene donor) is why HMTA has dominated PF molding compounds since the 1920s.

### 2.3 Why MF Resists Heat Better Than UF
- **UF backbone** contains the linear **–NH–CO–NH–** (urea) linkage, which is **flexible** and hydrolyzable by hot water/acid.
- **MF backbone** contains the **1,3,5-triazine ring** — a 6-membered aromatic ring of alternating C and N atoms — which is **rigid, planar, and highly thermally stable**.
- Therefore MF is used in **hot-dish dinnerware** (up to 150 °C) where UF would blister.

### 2.4 Why UF is Cheaper but Water-Sensitive
- Urea is **cheaper and more abundant** than melamine.
- Urea's carbonyl (C=O) and N–H groups are **hydrogen-bond active**, making UF hygroscopic.
- Melamine's triazine ring is **non-polar and sterically bulky**, blocking water uptake.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility
- **PF resins** are still the **gold standard** for high-heat electrical insulation (switches, circuit breakers) and friction materials (brake pads).
- **UF resins** dominate the **engineered wood industry** (>70% of all particleboard/MDF).
- **MF resins** are essential in **decorative laminates** (countertops, table tops) due to their hardness, gloss, and stain resistance.

---

## 3. 🧮 Stoichiometric & Molecular Mass Calculations

These frequently appear in KTU 14-mark problems.

### 3.1 Theoretical HCHO Required for 1 mol of Phenol (Full Methylolation)
Full methylolation uses **3 mol HCHO per mol phenol** (2 ortho + 1 para positions).

$$M_{\text{phenol}} = 94.11\,\text{g/mol}, \quad M_{\text{HCHO}} = 30.03\,\text{g/mol}$$

$$\text{Mass HCHO required} = 3 \times 30.03 = 90.09\,\text{g per 94.11 g phenol}$$

$$\Rightarrow \text{F:P molar ratio (full methylolation)} = 3:1$$

### 3.2 Typical Industrial Ratios
- **Novolac:** F:P = 0.7:1 to 0.85:1 (Phenol excess)
- **Resol:** F:P = 1.2:1 to 2.0:1 (Formaldehyde excess)

### 3.3 Mass of HMTA Required
Typical HMTA loading: **10%** by mass of Novolac resin.

$$m_{\text{HMTA}} = 0.10 \times m_{\text{resin}}$$

Moles of HMTA:

$$n_{\text{HMTA}} = \frac{0.10 \times m_{\text{resin}}}{140.19\,\text{g/mol}}$$

(where $M_{\text{HMTA}} = 140.19\,\text{g/mol}$).

Each mole of HMTA releases **6 mol HCHO**:

$$n_{\text{HCHO released}} = 6 \times n_{\text{HMTA}}$$

# 🔍 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 1. Derivation — Mass of Formaldehyde Required for Novolac Synthesis

### Problem
A lab wants to synthesize **1 kg of Novolac resin** with a F:P molar ratio of **0.8:1** and a target average degree of polymerization of **$\overline{DP} = 6$** (i.e., 6 phenol units per chain). The acid catalyst is oxalic acid (does not enter the polymer). How much **formaldehyde (37% aqueous solution, formalin)** is needed?

### Given
- Target Novolac mass: $m_{\text{Novolac}} = 1000\,\text{g}$
- $M_{\text{phenol}} = 94.11\,\text{g/mol}$
- $M_{\text{HCHO}} = 30.03\,\text{g/mol}$
- Molar ratio F:P = 0.8
- $\overline{DP} = 6$ (each Novolac chain has 6 phenol units → joined by 5 methylene bridges)

### Step 1 — Determine moles of phenol needed per chain
$$\text{Moles of phenol in 1 chain} = \overline{DP} = 6$$

### Step 2 — Moles of HCHO needed per chain
For a linear Novolac with $\overline{DP} = n$, there are $(n-1)$ methylene bridges, each consuming **1 mol HCHO**.

$$n_{\text{HCHO}} = \overline{DP} - 1 = 6 - 1 = 5\,\text{mol HCHO per chain}$$

### Step 3 — Verify F:P ratio
$$\text{F:P} = \frac{5}{6} = 0.833 \approx 0.8 \;\checkmark$$

### Step 4 — Mass of phenol and HCHO per chain
$$m_{\text{phenol per chain}} = 6 \times 94.11 = 564.66\,\text{g}$$

$$m_{\text{HCHO per chain}} = 5 \times 30.03 = 150.15\,\text{g}$$

### Step 5 — Total mass of one Novolac chain (subtract water lost)
Each condensation reaction releases 1 H₂O. For 5 bridges, 5 water molecules are released.

$$m_{\text{water released}} = 5 \times 18.015 = 90.075\,\text{g}$$

$$m_{\text{chain}} = 564.66 + 150.15 - 90.075 = 624.735\,\text{g}$$

### Step 6 — Number of chains in 1 kg
$$N_{\text{chains}} = \frac{1000}{624.735} \approx 1.6007$$

### Step 7 — Total HCHO required
$$m_{\text{HCHO}} = 1.6007 \times 150.15 = 240.40\,\text{g pure HCHO}$$

### Step 8 — Mass of 37% formalin solution
$$m_{\text{formalin}} = \frac{240.40}{0.37} = 649.74\,\text{g}$$

### ✅ Final Answer
- **Pure HCHO needed: ≈ 240.4 g**
- **37% formalin solution needed: ≈ 649.7 g**
- **Phenol needed: ≈ 902.5 g** (computed analogously: $1.6007 \times 564.66$)

---

## 2. Derivation — HMTA Loading for Novolac Curing

### Problem
A Novolac molding compound contains **10% HMTA** by mass. How many crosslinks per Novolac chain are introduced if the average Novolac chain has $\overline{DP} = 8$ phenol units and $M_{\text{chain}} \approx 8 \times 94.11 - 7 \times 18.015 = 626.77\,\text{g/mol}$?

### Step 1 — Assume 100 g of molding compound
- Novolac: $90\,\text{g}$
- HMTA: $10\,\text{g}$

### Step 2 — Moles of Novolac chains
$$n_{\text{Novolac}} = \frac{90}{626.77} = 0.1436\,\text{mol}$$

### Step 3 — Moles of HMTA
$$n_{\text{HMTA}} = \frac{10}{140.19} = 0.0713\,\text{mol}$$

### Step 4 — Moles of HCHO released by HMTA
$$n_{\text{HCHO}} = 6 \times 0.0713 = 0.4278\,\text{mol}$$

### Step 5 — Crosslink bridges per chain
Assuming each HCHO forms one methylene crosslink:
$$\text{Crosslinks per chain} = \frac{0.4278}{0.1436} = 2.98 \approx 3\,\text{crosslinks per chain}$$

### ✅ Final Answer
Approximately **3 crosslinks per Novolac chain** — enough to form a robust 3D network.

## 5. 🧪 Common Calculation Conversions (Useful in KTU Problems)

> [!TIP]
> **Water Released per Crosslink Bridge:** 18.015 g/mol
> **HMTA Molecular Weight:** 140.19 g/mol
> **Phenol Molecular Weight:** 94.11 g/mol
> **Formaldehyde Molecular Weight:** 30.03 g/mol
> **Urea Molecular Weight:** 60.06 g/mol
> **Melamine Molecular Weight:** 126.12 g/mol

For 1 mol of methylene bridge formed between 2 phenol units:
- Reactants: 2 phenol (188.22 g) + 1 HCHO (30.03 g) = 218.25 g
- Products: 1 –CH₂– bridge unit + 1 H₂O (18.015 g)
- Mass retained: 218.25 – 18.015 = 200.235 g

This 18.015 g/mol water loss is the basis for **mass balance calculations** in phenolic resin synthesis problems.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model]`
**What is a Urea-Formaldehyde (UF) resin? Write its two important applications.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 marks)
UF resin is a **thermoset amino resin** synthesized by the polycondensation of **urea (NH₂–CO–NH₂)** with **formaldehyde (HCHO)** in the presence of a mildly acidic or basic catalyst. The reaction proceeds in two stages: **methylolation** (forming hydroxymethyl ureas) and **condensation** (forming methylene and ether bridges), yielding a transparent, hard, crosslinked network.

**Two Important Applications:**
1. **Wood adhesives** — UF resin is the dominant binder for **plywood, particleboard, and MDF** in the engineered wood industry.
2. **Molded articles** — used in making **electrical fittings, switch boards, and toilet seats** due to its rigidity and low cost.

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`**

**(a)** With the help of a neat reaction scheme, explain the **synthesis and curing of Novolac resin**. Discuss the role of **hexamethylenetetramine (HMTA)** in its curing. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare the **structure, properties, and applications of UF and MF resins**. Why is MF preferred over UF for **decorative laminates and dinnerware**? (7 marks)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Synthesis of Novolac (3 marks):**
Novolac is synthesized by reacting **phenol** with **formaldehyde** in the presence of an **acid catalyst** (typically oxalic acid or dilute HCl). The molar ratio of formaldehyde to phenol is **less than 1** (typically 0.7–0.85), ensuring phenol is in excess.

**Reaction 1 — Methylolation:**
Phenol + HCHO → ortho- and para-hydroxymethylphenols.

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{HCHO} \rightarrow \text{HO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CH}_2\text{OH}$$

**Reaction 2 — Condensation:**
Two methylol phenols condense to form a **methylene bridge (–CH₂–)** with release of water.

$$2\,\text{HO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CH}_2\text{OH} \rightarrow \text{HO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CH}_2\text{–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{HCHO}$$

The chain grows linearly because phenol is in excess (no free methylol groups remain after condensation) — yielding a **linear, thermoplastic, permanently fusible prepolymer**.

**Curing with HMTA (3 marks):**
Novolac is converted to a thermoset by adding **hexamethylenetetramine (HMTA, (CH₂)₆N₄)** at 6–15% by mass and heating to **150–180 °C** under pressure. HMTA decomposes:

$$(\text{CH}_2)_6\text{N}_4 \xrightarrow{\Delta} 6\,\text{HCHO} + 4\,\text{NH}_3$$

The released HCHO and NH₃ react with phenolic –OH groups and ring positions, forming **–CH₂– and –CH₂–NH– bridges** between chains. The result is a **rigid, infusible, three-dimensional crosslinked network** known as cured phenolic (C-stage phenolic).

**Diagram (1 mark):** Linear Novolac → HMTA + heat → Crosslinked 3D network.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**UF Resin Structure & Properties (1.5 marks):**
- Formed by polycondensation of urea (H₂N–CO–NH₂) with HCHO.
- Contains **methylene (–CH₂–)** and **dimethylene ether (–CH₂–O–CH₂–)** bridges between urea units.
- **Properties:** transparent, hard, cheap, but **water-sensitive** (hydrogen bonding with H₂O), limited heat resistance (~100 °C continuous).
- **Applications:** wood adhesives (plywood, particleboard), textile finishing, slow-release fertilizers.

**MF Resin Structure & Properties (1.5 marks):**
- Formed by polycondensation of melamine (C₃N₃(NH₂)₃) with HCHO.
- Contains the **1,3,5-triazine ring** which is rigid, aromatic, and hydrophobic.
- **Properties:** hard, transparent, **excellent heat resistance (up to 180 °C continuous)**, **excellent water resistance**, scratch-resistant, high gloss.
- **Applications:** decorative laminates (Formica-type), unbreakable dinnerware, electrical insulators, paints.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Property | UF Resin | MF Resin |
|---|---|---|
| Monomer | Urea | Melamine |
| Backbone | –NH–CO–NH– (flexible) | Triazine ring (rigid aromatic) |
| Heat resistance | Moderate (~100 °C) | High (~180 °C) |
| Water resistance | Poor | Excellent |
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| Color stability | Yellows with age | Excellent |
| Applications | Plywood, textile finish | Laminates, dinnerware |

**Why MF is preferred for laminates & dinnerware (2 marks):**
1. **Heat resistance:** MF withstands boiling water and hot food (up to 150 °C) without blistering or deforming; UF softens and degrades.
2. **Water resistance:** MF's triazine ring is non-polar and hydrophobic; UF's polar –NH–CO–NH– groups absorb water, causing swelling.
3. **Stain and scratch resistance:** MF surfaces are harder, glossier, and more resistant to food stains — making them ideal for countertops and tableware.
4. **Color stability:** MF does not yellow under UV light or heat; UF yellows over time.

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`**

**(a)** With the help of reaction schemes, explain the **synthesis of Resol resin**. Why is it called a **single-stage resin**? Describe its curing mechanism. (7 marks)

**(b)** List the **raw materials, catalysts, and curing agents** for the four resins discussed (Novolac, Resol, UF, MF). Give **two industrial applications** of each. (7 marks)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Synthesis of Resol (3 marks):**
Resol is synthesized by reacting **phenol** with **formaldehyde** in the presence of a **base catalyst** (NaOH, NH₃, or Ba(OH)₂). The molar ratio of formaldehyde to phenol is **greater than 1** (typically 1.2–2.0), ensuring formaldehyde is in excess.

**Reaction 1 — Multiple Methylolation:**
Under basic conditions, formaldehyde attacks the ortho and para positions of phenol, attaching **methylol (–CH₂OH) groups**. Because formaldehyde is in excess, up to **3 methylol groups** (2 ortho + 1 para) can attach to a single phenol ring.

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 3\,\text{HCHO} \rightarrow \text{HOCH}_2\text{–C}_6\text{H}_2(\text{OH})\text{–(CH}_2\text{OH)}_2$$

**Reaction 2 — Partial Condensation:**
Methylol groups condense with each other (or with active phenol positions) to form a **branched prepolymer** containing both **methylene (–CH₂–)** and **dimethylene ether (–CH₂–O–CH₂–)** bridges, while **retaining free methylol groups**.

**Why Single-Stage? (2 marks):**
- Resol is called a **single-stage resin** because it does **not require an external hardener** to cure.
- The free methylol groups present in the Resol prepolymer are **self-reactive** — they condense with each other on heating (>130 °C) to form the crosslinked network.
- In contrast, Novolac is a **two-stage resin** — the linear prepolymer must be mixed with HMTA hardener before it can cure.

**Curing Mechanism (2 marks):**
On heating the Resol prepolymer to 130–180 °C:
1. Free methylol groups condense with each other → **methylene bridges (–CH₂–)**.
2. Two methylol groups can also condense to form **ether bridges (–CH₂–O–CH₂–)**.
3. Ether bridges can rearrange to methylene bridges with release of HCHO.
4. The reaction continues until all methylol groups are consumed, yielding a **fully crosslinked, infusible, rigid thermoset** known as **Resite (C-stage)**.

$$2\,\text{–CH}_2\text{OH} \rightarrow \text{–CH}_2\text{–O–CH}_2\text{–} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{–CH}_2\text{–} + \text{HCHO}$$

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Comparative Table — Raw Materials, Catalysts, Curing Agents, Applications (7 marks):**

| Resin | Raw Materials | Catalyst | Curing Agent | Application 1 | Application 2 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Novolac** | Phenol + HCHO | Acid (oxalic, HCl) | HMTA (10%) | Electrical switches & plugs | Brake linings & grinding wheels |
| **Resol** | Phenol + HCHO | Base (NaOH, NH₃) | Heat (no hardener) | Plywood adhesives | Foundry sand binders (shell molding) |
| **UF** | Urea + HCHO | Mild acid/base | Heat + NH₄Cl | Particleboard adhesives | Crease-resistant textile finish |
| **MF** | Melamine + HCHO | Mild base | Heat + latent acid | Decorative laminates (Formica) | Unbreakable dinnerware |

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Novolac, Resol, MF, UF Resins**

### 🔑 Key Takeaways
- **Novolac = Acid catalyst + Phenol excess + Linear + Needs HMTA hardener.**
- **Resol = Base catalyst + Formaldehyde excess + Branched + Self-curing (single-stage).**
- **UF = Urea + HCHO → cheap, transparent, water-sensitive, used in wood adhesives.**
- **MF = Melamine + HCHO → heat/water resistant, used in laminates & dinnerware.**
- **Methylol groups (–CH₂OH)** are the **reactive intermediates** in all four resins.
- **Methylene bridges (–CH₂–)** and **ether bridges (–CH₂–O–CH₂–)** are the **crosslinks**.
- **HMTA** is the **exclusive hardener** for Novolac (liberates HCHO + NH₃ on heating).
- **Melamine's triazine ring** is the **structural reason** for MF's superior heat/water resistance.
- **Cured phenolic is called Resite (C-stage).** Uncured Novolac is A-stage; partially cured is B-stage.
- All four resins are **thermosets** — they **cannot be remelted or recycled**.

### ⚠️ Common KTU Pitfalls
1. **Reversing the catalyst:** Novolac needs **acid**; Resol needs **base** — never mix them up.
2. **Forgetting the hardener:** Novolac will not cure without HMTA.
3. **Confusing urea with melamine:** Urea has **one** carbonyl; melamine has a **triazine ring** with 3 NH₂ groups.
4. **Missing reaction equations:** Always include the **methylolation + condensation** steps for full marks.
5. **Ignoring the F:P ratio:** The **F:P < 1 → Novolac; F:P > 1 → Resol** rule is the single most-tested point.

### 📐 Numerical Values to Memorize
- $M_{\text{phenol}} = 94.11\,\text{g/mol}$
- $M_{\text{HCHO}} = 30.03\,\text{g/mol}$
- $M_{\text{HMTA}} = 140.19\,\text{g/mol}$
- $M_{\text{urea}} = 60.06\,\text{g/mol}$
- $M_{\text{melamine}} = 126.12\,\text{g/mol}$
- $M_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 18.015\,\text{g/mol}$
- **HMTA loading in Novolac: 6–15% by mass.**
- **Cure temperature: PF = 150–180 °C; UF/MF = 110–150 °C.**

### 🏭 Applications Snapshot
- **PF (Novolac + Resol):** electrical insulation, brake pads, grinding wheels, plywood adhesives, foundry sand.
- **UF:** particleboard/MDF adhesives, textile finishing, fertilizers, molded fittings.
- **MF:** decorative laminates, dinnerware, paints, electrical insulators.

> [!TIP]
> **Final 30-Second Mnemonic:** **"NA-BR-UF-MF"**
> - **N**ovolac → **A**cid + needs hardener
> - **B**ase catalyst → **R**esol (self-cure)
> - **U**rea + Formaldehyde → UF (cheap, wood glue)
> - **M**elamine + Formaldehyde → MF (tough, laminates)
